|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Tricyclic**<br>**antidepressants,**<br>**e.g. amitriptyline,**<br>**imipramine**<br>Toxic dose:<br>despiramine,<br>trimipramine, and<br>nortriptyline >2.5<br>mg/kg<br>Protriptyline >1<br>mg/kg<br>All others >5<br>mg/kg|**Cardiovascular:**<br>hypotension,<br>dysrrythmias, cardiac<br>arrest<br>**Central nervous**<br>**system:**excitation,<br>restlessness,<br>myoclonus,<br>hyperref exia,<br>disorientation,<br>confusion,<br>hallucination, coma,<br>seizures<br>**Anticholinergic:**<br>hyperthermia, urinary<br>retention, paralytic<br>ileus, mydriasis,<br>dry mouth, f ushing<br>of skin|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 39 and Section 3.1).<br>• Give activated charcoal if patient presents within 2 hours after<br>ingestion, provided airway is protected.<br>• Monitor blood gases, correct hypoxia.<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG, measure the<br>QRS width.<br>• If shock persists, give vasopressors (see Section 3.1.4) –<br>_norepinephrine is preferred_or give epinephrine.<br>• Correct acidosis if can measure bicarbonate.<br>• Sodium bicarbonate (50 ml of 8.4% or 1–2 mmol/kg) should be<br>given to all patients with QRS prolongation (>120 millisecond)<br>or arrhythmias._Give repeated boluses of sodium bicarbonate_<br>_to keep QRS at <120 millisecond and arterial pH between_<br>_7.45–7.55._<br>• Seizures should be treated with diazepam (see Quick Check<br>page 19 and Section 3.5). Avoid the use of phenytoin.<br>• Following seizures, a dose of bicarbonate is suggested to<br>correct acidosis and reduce risk of further toxicity.|
|**Chloroquine**<br>Toxic dose: >20<br>mg/kg is toxic|Nausea, vomiting,<br>diarrhoea, and<br>abdominal<br>pain, dizziness,<br>convulsions, coma,<br>hypotension,<br>arrhythmias, sudden<br>cardiac arrest|• Manage airway and assist ventilation, as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 39 and Section 3.1).<br>• If shock persists, give vasopressors (see Section 3.1.4) –<br>epinephrine is preferred.<br>• Give activated charcoal if airway is protected and within 1<br>hour of ingestion.<br>• Observe for a minimum of 12 hours, monitor vital signs.<br>• Monitor blood glucose, urea, electrolytes, blood gases.<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG, and measure the<br>QRS width. If >120 millisecond, give sodium bicarbonate (50<br>ml of 8.4% or 1–2 mmol/kg). Give repeated boluses of sodium<br>bicarbonate to keep QRS at <120 millisecond and arterial pH<br>between 7.45–7.55.<br>• Correct hypokalaemia if <3 to no more than 3.5 (beware of<br>rebound increase in potassium).<br>• Seizures should be treated with diazepam (see Quick Check<br>page 41 and Section 3.5). Avoid barbiturates as these may<br>precipitate cardiac arrest. Avoid phenytoin.|



Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

Poisoning 

**173** 

**==> picture [324 x 18] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Quinine**<br>Toxic dose: >15<br>mg/kg could be<br>toxic|Tinnitus, deafness,<br>abdominal pain,<br>visual changes,<br>blindness, ataxia,<br>coma, convulsions,<br>arrhythmia,<br>torsade de pointes,<br>hypoglycaemia|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 39 and Section 3.1).<br>• Give activated charcoal if airway is protected.<br>• In severe cases, provided airway is protected, give repeat<br>doses of activated charcoal.<br>• Monitor urea, electrolytes, blood glucose, blood gases.<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG and measure<br>the QRS width – if >120 milliseconds there is a risk of cardiac<br>arrhythmias.<br>• If shock persists, give vasopressors to treat hypotension (see<br>Section 3.1.4).<br>• Treat cardiotoxicity (hypotension, wide QRS complexes, and<br>QTc prolongation) with sodium bicarbonate (50 ml of 8.4% or<br>1–2 mmol/kg). Give repeated boluses of sodium bicarbonate<br>to keep QRS at <120 millisecond and arterial pH between<br>7.45–7.55.<br>• Treat torsade de pointes with magnesium sulfate 1–2 grams IV.<br>• Seizures should be treated with diazepam (see Quick Check<br>page 41 and Section 3.5). Avoid barbiturates and phenytoin.|
|**Digoxin, oleander**<br>**(Thevetia**<br>**peruviana,**<br>**Nerium oleander,**<br>**Digitalis spp)**<br>Toxic dose<br>digoxin:≥3 mg<br>(produces toxic<br>level in adults).<br>Note:≥10 mg is<br>often lethal.<br>Patients on<br>digoxin therapy<br>are more<br>susceptible in<br>overdose.|Nausea, vomiting,<br>abdominal pain,<br>visual changes,<br>headache, fatigue,<br>coma,<br>Heart block and<br>tachy – or brady–<br>arrhythmias|• Give a dose of activated charcoal if presenting within 1 hour.<br>• Multiple doses of activated charcoal (every 4 hours for 24<br>hours) may be considered in the absence of digoxin antibodies.<br>• Monitor ECG.<br>• Monitor electrolytes at least every 6 hours and correct if<br>necessary (particularly potassium).<br>•_Monitor blood gases and pH and correct metabolic acidosis_<br>_with sodium bicarbonate._<br>•_Digoxin antibodies_should be given, if available, for the<br>following indications:<br> °<br>serum potassium >6 mmol/l<br> °<br>bradycardia or heart block with hypotension<br> °<br>tachyarrhythmia with hypotension.<br>• Treat hyperkalaemia: if K >5.5 mmol/l give sodium bicarbonate<br>(1mmol/kg), glucose (0.5 g/kg IV), PLUS insulin (0.1 U/kg IV)<br>(see Section 5.2.2). Note: Do not use calcium, furosemide, or<br>salbutamol as these may worsen toxicity.<br>• Give atropine for bradycardia or heart block associated with<br>hypotension.<br>• If readily available, consider referral for insertion of a<br>temporary pacing wire if there is evidence of signif cant<br>bradycardia or AV block with haemodynamic compromise.<br>• Ventricular tachyarrhythmia – give magnesium sulfate 2 g IV<br>over 20 minutes in an adult initially. If no response consider<br>lidocaine.|



Poisoning 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**174** 

**==> picture [324 x 18] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Antidiabetic**<br>**agents:**<br>**hypoglycaemic**<br>**agents**<br>(if metformin see<br>separate entry)<br>Toxic dose: for<br>suphonylurea<br>and insulin, more<br>than the usual<br>recommended<br>dose|Sweating, agitation,<br>giddiness, confusion,<br>coma. Delayed onset<br>of hypoglycaemia<br>possible, also<br>recurrent<br>hypoglycaemia|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If unconscious, give 25–50 ml D50 (see Quick Check page 41). A<br>continuous infusion of 10% dextrose (1 litre over 8 hours) may<br>be required if blood sugar falls to <3 mmol/l (see Section 3.4.2).<br>• When the patient recovers consciousness, give sugary drinks<br>and food, followed by a long-acting carbohydrate (e.g. bread,<br>rice, maize) to prevent recurrent symptoms.<br>• Give activated charcoal if airway is protected and it is within 1<br>hour of ingestion of an oral hypoglycaemic.<br>• Check blood sugar and monitor every 1 to 2 hours.<br>• Continue monitoring for at least 24 hours.<br>• Monitor level of consciousness using AVPU.<br>• Correct asymptomatic hypoglycaemia with sweet drinks (not<br>diabetic or sugar-free), e.g. cola, juice, sweet water, oral<br>glucose powder or tablets (see Section 3.4.2).<br>• Do not give prophylactic dextrose without symptoms or a low<br>blood glucose.<br>•_Octreotide_, if available, could be given to patients whose blood<br>sugar does not normalise after above measures.|
|**Antidiabetic**<br>**agents: metformin**<br>Toxic dose:<br>variable response|Lactic acidosis<br>(does not cause<br>hypoglycaemia)|• Give activated charcoal if airway is protected and it is within 2<br>hours of ingestion.<br>•_Monitor blood gases and lactate._<br>•_If acidotic, ensure that patient is adequately ventilated and_<br>_perfused and give IV sodium bicarbonate._|
|**Opioids**<br>e.g. morphine,<br>diamorphine<br>(heroin), raw<br>opium, codeine,<br>methadone,<br>dextropropoxy-<br>phene,<br>oxycodone,<br>tramadol<br>Toxic dose:<br>variable|Respiratory<br>depression,<br>central nervous<br>system depression<br>(drowsiness to<br>coma), miosis,<br>hypotension,<br>hypothermia, ataxia,<br>respiratory arrest,<br>non-cardiogenic<br>pulmonary oedema<br>Tramadol: seizures,<br>serotonin syndrome<br>Dextropropoxyphene:<br>cardiac dysrhythmias|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• Give naloxone (see Quick Check page 40 and Section 3.6).<br>• Give activated charcoal if within 2 hours of ingestion and<br>airway is protected.<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG if<br>dextroproxyphene taken. If QRS >120 millisecond, give sodium<br>bicarbonate (50 ml of 8.4% or 1–2 mmol/kg).<br>• For serotonin syndrome, see SSRIs.|



Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

Poisoning 

**175** 

**==> picture [324 x 18] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Paracetamol**<br>**(acetaminophen)**<br>Note: Risk<br>of toxicity is<br>increased in<br>patients taking<br>enzyme-inducing<br>drugs, e.g.<br>carbamazepine,<br>phenobarbital,<br>phenytoin,<br>primidone,<br>rifampicin.|Vomiting, right upper<br>quadrant abdominal<br>pain, hepatic<br>encephalopathy|• Give activated charcoal if less than 2 hours after ingestion.<br>• Obtain blood level if possible; however, the sample should be<br>taken at 4 hours or more after the ingestion.<br>• Eff cacy of antidote declines from 8 hours post-ingestion, so<br>give antidote based on history only if there is a delay in getting<br>the paracetamol level or it cannot be obtained.<br>• See paracetamol nomogram below.<br>• If paracetamol level not available, base treatment on ingested<br>dose:<br> °<br>75 mg/kg if high risk (nutritionally def cient, acute starvation,<br>AIDS, alcoholic, on enzyme-inducing drugs);<br> °<br>150 mg/kg if not high risk.<br>• Give acetylcysteine IV or orally:<br> °<br>IV acetylcysteine: initially 150 mg/kg over 15 minutes, then<br>50 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours. Admi-<br>nistration: dilute requisite dose in glucose intravenous infu-<br>sion 5% as follows – initially 200 ml given over 15 minutes,<br>then 500 ml over 4 hours, then 1 litre over 16 hours.<br> °<br>Oral acetylcysteine (acetylcysteine solution intended for<br>antidotal use, not granules for mucolytic use) – administer<br>a loading dose of 140 mg/kg body weight. Four hours after<br>administration of the loading dose, initiate a maintenance<br>dose of 70 mg/kg administered at 4-hourly intervals for 17<br>doses. The acetylcysteine solution should be given until 72<br>hours post-ingestion – continue for longer if LFTs abnormal.<br>Dilute to a 5% solution in soda pop, juice, or water prior to<br>oral or nasogastric administration.<br> °<br>Check liver function tests, INR (prothrombin time), creati-<br>nine and BUN, and electrolytes.|
|**Selective**<br>**serotonin**<br>**reuptake**<br>**inhibitors (SSRI)**<br>**e.g. f uoxetine,**<br>**paroxtine,**<br>**sertraline**<br>Toxic dose:<br>variable|Nausea, vomiting, dry<br>mouth, tachycardia,<br>drowsiness, coma<br>Serotonin syndrome<br>may occur: agitation,<br>confusion, delirium,<br>drowsiness, coma,<br>tremor, teeth<br>grinding, myoclonus<br>and hyperref exia,<br>hypertension<br>or hypotension,<br>seizures,<br>hyperthermia,<br>rhabdomyolysis,<br>renal failure,<br>coagulopathies may<br>develop|• Give activated charcoal within 2 hours of ingestion.<br>• Perform 12-lead ECG.<br>• Manage serotonin syndrome:<br> °<br>Monitor urea, electrolytes, CK, and renal function.<br> °<br>_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG.<br> °<br>Give IV f uids to maintain good urine output. If in shock, give<br>rapidly (see Quick Check page 39).<br> °<br>If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br> °<br>Sedate with diazepam if agitated or if having seizures (see<br>Quick Check pages 41, 59).<br> °<br>Hyperthermia (>40.5°C) should be treated with rapid cooling<br>(see Section 10.1).<br> °<br>_Cyproheptadine can be considered if available, and no_<br>_response to above measures. Give 4 to 8 mg every 1 to_<br>_4 hours. Repeat until therapeutic response is achieved._<br>_Maximum dose of 32 mg over 24 hours._<br> °<br>In cases of severe hyperthermia (>41°C) not improving<br>despite sedation and cooling measures, consider deeper<br>sedation and paralysis, provided advanced airway manage-<br>ment is possible – either manual ventilation or transfer to a<br>hospital with a mechanical ventilator..|



Poisoning 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**176** 

**==> picture [324 x 18] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Monoamine**<br>**oxidase inhibitors**<br>**(MAOI), e.g.**<br>**phenelzine,**<br>**tranylcypromine**<br>Toxic dose: In<br>adults >5 tablets<br>of any preparation<br>can be toxic|Anxiety, vomiting,<br>restlessness,<br>confusion,<br>f ushing, sweating,<br>hypertension,<br>hyperthermia,<br>seizures<br>Note: MAOIs interact<br>with a wide range<br>of drugs and some<br>foods to cause<br>severe hypertension.<br>They have a<br>life-threatening<br>interaction with<br>pethidine.<br>Serotonin syndrome<br>may occur.|• Give activated charcoal if airway is protected and within 2<br>hours of ingestion.<br>• If symptomatic, monitor pulse, blood pressure, temperature,<br>respiratory rate, and AVPU every 30 minutes.<br>• Check urea and electrolytes and full blood count.<br>• Check creatine kinase activity in all symptomatic patients.<br>• Hypertension: give IV diazepam (0.1–0.2 mg/kg). If ineffective,<br>then treat with IV nitrates, e.g. sodium nitroprusside. Beta<br>blockers are contraindicated.<br>• Give diazepam for agitation or seizures (see Quick Check<br>pages 41, 59).<br>• Hyperthermia (>40.5°C) should be treated with rapid cooling<br>(see Section 10.1).<br>• In cases of severe hyperthermia (>41°C) not improving despite<br>sedation and cooling measures, then consider deeper sedation<br>and paralysis, provided advanced airway management is<br>possible, either manual ventilation or_transfer to a hospital with_<br>_mechanical ventilator._<br>• If convulsions unresponsive to f rst- and second-line<br>antiepileptics (see Quick Check page 41 and Section 3.5),<br>and advanced airway management is feasible, consider<br>anaesthetic (e.g. thiopental or propofol).<br>• See also management of serotonin syndrome under SSRIs.|
|**Iron (ferrous**<br>**salts)**<br>Toxic dose:<br>>40 mg/kg<br>elemental<br>iron, or if there<br>is persistent<br>vomiting or<br>diarrhoea<br>Approximate<br>elemental iron<br>content of ferrous<br>salts is:<br>• ferrous<br>fumarate 210<br>mg (68 mg<br>iron)<br>• ferrous<br>gluconate 300<br>mg (35 mg<br>iron)<br>• ferrous<br>succinate 100<br>mg (35 mg<br>iron)<br>• ferrous sulfate<br>300 mg (60 mg<br>iron)<br>• dried ferrous<br>sulfate 200 mg<br>(65 mg iron)<br>Note: Check label<br>to make sure.|Vomiting and<br>diarrhoea – often<br>bloody; drowsiness,<br>lethargy, coma,<br>shock, convulsions,<br>liver failure Delayed<br>pyloric stenosis.<br>Note: Initial<br>symptoms may be<br>followed by apparent<br>recovery, then a<br>relapse. Therefore all<br>symptomatic patients<br>should be observed<br>for minimum of 12<br>hours.|• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 39 and Section 3.1).<br>• If more than 40 mg/kg body weight of elemental iron ingested<br>then:<br> °<br>do abdominal X-ray (if possible) to check if tablets are<br>visible in gut (Note: A negative X-ray does not necessarily<br>exclude iron ingestion.)<br>• If within 4 hours of ingestion, initiate whole bowel irrigation<br>with osmotically balanced polyethylene glycol-electrolyte<br>solution (2 litres per hour for adults and 0.5 litres/hour in<br>children – see above).<br>• If WBI is not available, give gastric lavage (with a wide-bore<br>tube) within 1 hour of ingestion or if radiography reveals<br>tablets in the stomach.<br>• Monitor urea and electrolytes, WBC, blood glucose, LFTs,<br>whole blood clotting time, renal function, and blood gases.<br>•_If possible, check iron level 4 hours post-ingestion and give_<br>_deferoxamine if the serum iron level is over 90 µmol/l._<br>• If iron levels are not available, give deferoxamine if patient has:<br> °<br>taken 60 mg/kg elemental iron (see table of elemental iron<br>content or check label), or<br> °<br>any of the following: metabolic acidosis, hypotension,<br>shock, coma, convulsions.<br>• Give deferoxamine by slow IV infusion: initially 15 mg/kg/hour,<br>reduced after 4–6 hours so that total dose does not exceed 80<br>mg/kg in 24 hours.|



Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

Poisoning 

**177** 

**==> picture [324 x 18] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Lithium**<br>Toxic dose:<br>Acute overdose is<br>>2 g in adults<br>Note: Acute<br>overdose is<br>usually well-<br>tolerated.<br>Acute-on-chronic:<br>any amount more<br>than the usual<br>daily dose could<br>be toxic|Mild toxicity: nausea,<br>vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>f ne tremor<br>Moderate toxicity:<br>confusion,<br>fasciculation, and<br>hyperref exia<br>Severe toxicity:<br>coma, convulsions<br>and cardiac<br>arrhythmias|• Acute overdose with normal renal function – no gut<br>decontamination is needed.<br>• Overdose in patient on lithium therapy (taking sustained-<br>release) or with impaired renal function – consider the use of<br>whole bowel irrigation.<br>All:<br>• If hypotension or shock, give rapid IV f uids (see Quick Check<br>page 39 and Section 3.1) – NS preferred. Titrate to ensure good<br>urine output.<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG.<br>• Monitor renal function.<br>• Monitor and correct electrolyte imbalance.<br>• Seizures should be treated with diazepam (see Quick Check<br>page 41 and Section 3.5).<br>•_Haemodialysis for patients with coma, convulsions, respiratory_<br>_failure, or acute renal failure._|
|**Phenobarbital**<br>Toxic dose:<br>variable response|Drowsiness,<br>lethargy, slurred<br>speech, nystagmus,<br>coma, respiratory<br>depression,<br>hypotension,<br>tachycardia,<br>hypothermia|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 39 and Section 3.1).<br>• If symptomatic, give repeat doses of activated charcoal<br>provided bowel sounds are present and airway is protected.<br>• Manage hypothermia.<br>• Give supportive care.<br>• Monitor pulse, respiratory rate, BP, temperature, AVPU.<br>•_Haemodialysis if ileus, failure to respond to supportive care._|
|**Theophylline**<br>Toxic dose >20<br>mg/kg|Vomiting (may<br>be protracted),<br>haematemesis,<br>agitation,<br>tachycardia,<br>hypertension,<br>hypotension,<br>hyperventilation,<br>cardiac<br>dysrhythmias,<br>seizures, acid-base<br>disturbance,<br>hypokalaemia,<br>rhabdomyolysis,<br>respiratory arrest|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 39 and Section 3.1).<br>• Give multiple dose activated charcoal.<br>• Give antiemetic such as metoclopramide (may need large<br>dose) or ondansetron.<br>• Monitor electrolytes and cautiously correct hypokalaemia if <3<br>to no more than 3.5 (beware of rebound increase in potassium).<br>• Monitor vital signs.<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG.<br>• Treat SVT if it is causing haemodynamic compromise. Give<br>a beta-blocker (preferably beta-1 selective blockers, such<br>as_esmolol, metoprolol_, but beware of bronchospasm in<br>asthmatics and those with COPD – in these cases consider<br>_verapamil or adenosine_).<br>• For ventricular arrhythmias causing haemodynamic<br>compromise, use magnesium or lidocaine. If severe, treat with<br>DC cardioversion.<br>• Diazepam for seizures (see Quick Check pages 41, 59). If<br>unresponsive, follow with phenobarbital. If convulsions are<br>unresponsive to f rst-line antiepileptics (see Section 3.5),<br>and advanced airway management is feasible, consider<br>anaesthetic (e.g. thiopental). Do not use phenytoin.<br>•_Consider haemodialysis, if available, for life-threatening_<br>_toxicity._|



Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**178** 

**==> picture [324 x 18] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Warfarin**|See anticoagulant<br>pesticides further<br>down.||
|**Pesticides**|||
|**Aluminium or zinc**<br>**phosphide**|Retrosternal burning,<br>persistent vomiting,<br>hypotension,<br>shock, bradycardia<br>or tachycardia,<br>myocardial<br>depression,<br>refractory<br>hypotension,<br>headache, dizziness,<br>restlessness,<br>hypoglycaemia,<br>metabolic acidosis,<br>non-cardiogenic<br>pulmonary oedema,<br>acute respiratory<br>distress syndrome,<br>acute renal failure,<br>hepatic damage|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 40 and Section 3.1).<br>• If shock persists after f uid resuscitation, start vasopressors<br>(see Section 3.1).<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG.<br>• Monitor for and correct hypoglycaemia.<br>• Monitor for and correct electrolyte imbalance.<br>•_Give sodium bicarbonate (1–2 mmol/kg) for metabolic acidosis._<br>• Magnesium sulfate may improve cardiac output – give 1 g 6<br>hourly.<br>• Other supportive care as required.<br>• Monitor renal and hepatic function.|
|**Anticoagulant**<br>**rodenticides**<br>**(raticides, rat and**<br>**mouse killers)**<br>**or anticoagulant**<br>**therapy (warfarin**|Bleeding:<br>spontaneous<br>bruising,<br>haematomas,<br>haematuria, rectal<br>bleeding and<br>haemorrhage into<br>any internal organ<br>Delayed onset and<br>may be prolonged|•_Monitor INR at 24 and 48 hours._<br>•_If poisoning and INR mild to moderately elevated without major_<br>_bleeding, give oral vitamin K 10–20 mg._<br>•_If patient is on anticoagulant therapy and there is no active_<br>_bleeding but the INR is prolonged (INR 5.0–9.0), omit 2 doses_<br>_of warfarin, then repeat the INR. Further doses may be missed_<br>_as needed, titrated to INR. Restart at lower maintenance dose_<br>_once the INR is in the therapeutic range._<br>•_If patient is on anticoagulant therapy and there is no active_<br>_bleeding but the INR is dangerously prolonged (INR_≥_9.0),_<br>_warfarin should be stopped and give vitamin K 2.5 to 5 mg_<br>_orally. Further doses may be given as necessary, titrated to_<br>_the INR._<br>• If serious or life-threatening bleeding, stop warfarin and give<br>vitamin K 10 mg IV by slow infusion (over 20 to 60 minutes),<br>supplemented by transfusions of fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 2-3<br>units initially, or prothrombin complex concentrate.<br>• In case of long-acting anticoagulant rodenticides, vitamin K<br>therapy may be needed for several weeks. The dose should be<br>titrated to response.|



Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**179** 

**==> picture [324 x 18] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Chlorphenoxy**<br>**herbicides**<br>e.g. MCPA, 2,<br>4-D|Burning pain in<br>the mouth and<br>epigastrium. Muscle<br>pain and rigidity,<br>muscle twitching,<br>agitation, seizures,<br>hyperpyrexia,<br>rhabdomyolysis<br>leading to renal<br>failure. Metabolic<br>acidosis,<br>hyperventilation,<br>tachycardia,<br>hypotension, ECG<br>abnormalities,<br>prolonged coma|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 40 and Section 3.1). Titrate to maintain adequate urine<br>output.<br>• Monitor_blood gases_, renal and liver function, creatine kinase.<br>• Look for dark-coloured urine (_check for myoglobin_).<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG.<br>• In symptomatic cases, alkalinise the urine to pH>7.5 with IV<br>sodium bicarbonate. Suggested regimen: sodium bicarbonate<br>225 mmol (225 ml of an 8.4% solution) intravenously over 1 hour.<br>Give additional boluses of intravenous sodium bicarbonate to<br>maintain urine pH in the range 7.5–8.5. Urinary alkalinisation<br>should only be done if there are facilities to monitor plasma<br>bicarbonate and urine pH.<br>• Treat rhabdomyolysis with f uid replacement to maintain good<br>renal output together with urinary alkalinisation.<br>•_In severe poisoning use haemodialysis, if available._|
|**Organophos-**<br>**phates and**<br>**carbamates**|Muscarinic effects:<br>DUMBBELS<br>(defecation,<br>urination, miosis,<br>bronchospasm,<br>bronchorrhea,<br>emesis, lacrimation,<br>salivation)<br>Nicotinic effects:<br>weakness,<br>fasciculation,<br>paralysis, mydriasis<br>Other: agitation,<br>confusion, lethargy,<br>convulsions, coma|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 40 and Section 3.1). Titrate to maintain adequate urine<br>output.<br>• Give atropine 1–3 mg intravenously as a bolus.<br>• Listen to lungs, take pulse, and measure blood pressure.<br>• Aim for clear lungs, stable blood pressure<br>• (>90 mmHg systolic), dry mucous membranes, and oxygen<br>saturation of >95%.<br>• Recheck at f ve minutes. If no improvement, give double the<br>initial dose of atropine. Dilated pupils and tachycardia alone<br>should not be considered as end points.<br>• Continue to give doubling doses of atropine every<br>• 5–10 minutes until the patient is stable. If the lung crepitations<br>persist after 3 to 5 boluses of atropine (doubling doses),<br>consider that the patient may have aspirated.<br>• If blood pressure does not improve with atropine, consider<br>giving f uid boluses and exclude metabolic acidosis.<br>• Once the patient has been atropinized, initiate an infusion of<br>atropine (20% of the total dose required to atropinize) as an<br>hourly infusion.<br>• Monitor signs of atropine toxicity (agitation, confusion,<br>hyperthermia) every 4 to 6 hours. If atropine toxicity develops,<br>stop the infusion and restart at 70% of the last infusion rate<br>once the toxicity settles.<br>• Monitor respiratory rate, pulse rate, and blood pressure.<br>Prepare to intubate and if necessary ventilate.<br>• Give diazepam 5–10 mg IV for agitation, seizures, and<br>fasciculations (see Quick Check pages 41, 59 and Section 3.5).<br>Repeat dose as necessary.<br>•_For organophosphates ONLY, give pralidoxime, if available.4 _|



- 4 Pralidoxime chloride or mesylate: 30 mg/kg IV over 5–10 minutes, followed by the same dose every 4–6 hours, or by IV infusion of 8 mg/kg/hour, maximum of 12 g in 24 hours. Most useful within 24–48 hours. Pralidoxime is not on the WHO EML..

|---|---|---|
||||
|**Paraquat**|Early stages (hours to<br>a few days): burning<br>pain of the mouth,<br>lips, and tongue.<br>Gastrointestinal<br>corrosion leading to<br>painful swallowing<br>(odynophagia),<br>nausea, vomiting,<br>abdominal pain.<br>Following large<br>ingestions: coma<br>convulsions,<br>cardiovascular<br>collapse, and<br>shortness of breath.<br>Burning sensation of<br>the skin.<br>Later (few days):<br>ulceration of the<br>tongue and oral<br>cavity with contact<br>bleeding, shortness<br>of breath due to<br>acute alveolitis,<br>pulmonary oedema,<br>pneumothorax, and<br>pneumomediastinum.<br>Acute renal failure<br>and hepatitis. Acute<br>pancreatitis.<br>Later (weeks).<br>Chronic hypoxia due<br>to progressive lung<br>f brosis. Renal failure.|• If shock, give rapid IV f uids (see Quick Check page 40 and<br>Section 3.1). Titrate to maintain adequate urine output.<br>• Avoid giving supplemental oxygen if possible as this worsens<br>lung injury. Oxygen may be needed in late stage as f brosis<br>develops.<br>• Give activated charcoal or Fullers earth for patients presenting<br>within 2 hours.<br>• Insert a nasogastric tube as early as possible to facilitate<br>feeding.<br>•_Conf rm systemic absorption with urine dithionite test, if_<br>_available._<br>• Assess baseline electrolytes, creatinine, FBC, and blood gases,<br>and correct all reversible abnormalities.<br>• Screen and treat for sepsis – monitor temperature, check<br>WBC, blood cultures when indicated. Start empirical<br>antibiotics (see Section 3.1.5).<br>• Give IV f uids to maintain good renal output.<br>• Liberal pain relief and sedation with opioids and<br>benzodiazepines as needed.|

|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|

|**Propanil**|Causes<br>methaemoglobin-<br>aemia. Nausea,<br>vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>dizziness, cyanosis,<br>headache,<br>tachycardia,<br>hypotension,<br>respiratory<br>depression, lactic<br>acidosis, chest pain,<br>confusion, coma, and<br>convulsions. Dark<br>brown or reddish<br>urine.|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 40).<br>• Give activated charcoal.<br>• Monitor blood gases with_co-oximeter_(Note: A pulse<br>oximeter will give a misleading result in the presence of<br>methaemoglobin).<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_until patients maintain stable<br>cardiovascular status.<br>• Check haemoglobin level to detect anaemia due to haemolysis.<br>• Patients who present with depressed level of consciousness<br>tend to have poor prognosis. These patients should be closely<br>observed.<br>•_Check methaemoglobin concentration, if possible._<br>• A qualitative test for methaemoglobin is to place 1 to 2 drops<br>of the patient’s blood on white paper. Normal blood will be<br>dark red or violet and will brighten on exposure to oxygen.<br>Methaemoglobin will appear “chocolate” brown and will not<br>change colour.<br>• If the patient_has a methaemoglobin level of >20–30%_or is<br>symptomatic (confusion, tachycardia, hypotension, chest pain,<br>cyanosis) in the absence of methaemoglobin level, treat with<br>methylthioninium chloride (methylene blue).<br> °<br>Give a loading dose of methylthioninium chloride 2 mg/kg<br>IV of 1% solution (10 mg/ml) over 5 minutes. Assess after 15<br>minutes. If no improvement, give a further dose of 1 mg/kg<br>and transfer if possible for further treatment with methyl-<br>thioninium chloride.<br> °<br>After 6 hours_recheck methaemoglobin level_, clinical status,<br>and blood gases. Then, if necessary repeat the dose of 1<br>mg/kg. Continue to repeat 6 hourly while patient is sympto-<br>matic or_methaemoglobin level remains >30%._<br> °<br>May need methylthioninium chloride for 2–3 days.<br> °<br>If patient is deteriorating on this therapy, consider possibi-<br>lity of G6PD def ciency or haemolysis.<br> °<br>If methylthioninium chloride is not available or patient has<br>G6PDD, give IV or oral ascorbic acid 500 mg every 12 hours.<br> °<br>If patient continues to deteriorate consider exchange<br>transfusion.|

Dysphagia,<br>hypersalivation<br>(drooling), hoarse<br>voice, and stridor.<br>Gastrointestinal<br>bleeding and<br>haematemesis.<br>Perforation, shock.<br>Aspiration<br>pneumonia, airway<br>obstruction.<br>A**cids**cause<br>coagulation necrosis.<br>Strong acetic<br>acid also causes<br>haemolysis and renal<br>failure.<br>**Alkalis**cause<br>liquefaction necrosis,<br>which may result in<br>extensive penetration<br>of tissue.|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If stridor, consider advanced airway management (see Quick<br>Check pages 62–65) and surgical airway (see Quick Check<br>page 69 and Section 3.2.2).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 40 and Section 3.1).<br>• Do NOT induce vomiting or give gastric lavage or activated<br>charcoal.<br>• Do NOT attempt neutralization.<br>• Give adequate pain relief with IV opioids.<br>•_Refer all patients for assessment of gastrointestinal injury by_<br>_cautious endoscopy between 6–24 hours of ingestion._<br>• If grade III injury, put nasojejunal tube under endoscopy or<br>perform feeding jejunostomy.<br>• Monitor_pH, f uid_, and electrolyte status, haemoglobin and<br>clotting time.<br>• If possible perform abdominal and chest X-ray to assess for<br>aspiration and perforation.<br>• Patients with acid ingestion:_correct metabolic acidosis with_<br>_sodium bicarbonate._<br>• Consider surgical intervention for any signs of perforation.|

Poison or toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|
||||
|**Ethylene glycol**|Drunken-state,<br>nausea, vomiting,<br>metabolic acidosis,<br>renal failure,<br>calcium oxalate<br>crystals in urine,<br>hypocalcaemia,<br>seizures, coma,<br>tetany|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 40 and Section 3.1) and titrate to maintain good urine<br>output.<br>• Give strong alcoholic drink (e.g. brand-named vodka or whisky,<br>not informally distilled spirit) to block metabolism. Oral loading<br>dose (by PO or by NG tube) – 1.8 ml/kg of a 40–43% alcohol<br>drink over 15–30 minutes (diluted). Patient should be managed<br>at tertiary facility. Transfer at this stage if necessary.<br>• Continue oral administration of alcohol drink as follows.<br>• Maintenance dose:<br> °<br>0.2 ml/kg/hour (non-drinker)<br> °<br>0.46 ml/kg/hour (heavy alcohol user).<br>• Maintenance dose during dialysis:<br> °<br>0.5 ml/kg/hour (non-drinker)<br> °<br>0.77 ml/kg/hour (heavy alcohol user).<br>• May need to give alcohol for 2–3 days.<br>• Correct metabolic acidosis with sodium bicarbonate (may<br>need high doses) and f uid replacement. Important to monitor<br>electrolytes for hypernatraemia and hypokalaemia.<br>• To conf rm diagnosis, if possible, check osmolar gap, anion<br>gap, and serum ethanol. In the early stages a gap of >19<br>mOsm/kgH2O may be indicative of ethylene glycol poisoning<br>if serum ethanol is 0 (if not, subtract 24 mOsm/kgH2O per 100<br>mg/dl of ethanol). Later a raised anion-gap metabolic acidosis<br>develops.<br>•_Haemodialysis if there is a severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.25_<br>_or base def cit >15 mm despite buffer) or renal failure._Consider<br>peritoneal dialysis if haemodialysis is not available.<br>•_Fomepizole_is an alternative to ethanol. Give a bolus dose of<br>15 mg/kg followed by 10 mg/kg twice daily for a maximum of 4<br>doses; followed by 15 mg/kg IV every 12 hours thereafter.<br>• If hypocalcaemia – cautious correction with calcium<br>gluconate.<br>• If readily available, pyridoxine 50 mg IV or IM every 6 hours<br>for 6 doses, and thiamine 100 mg IV or IM every 8 hours for 6<br>doses. These may be benef cial if the patient is alcoholic.|

Transfer at this stage if necessary.<br>• Continue oral administration of alcohol drink as follows.<br>• Maintenance dose:<br> °<br>0.2 ml/kg/hour (non-drinker)<br> °<br>0.46 ml/kg/hour (heavy alcohol user).<br>• Maintenance dose during dialysis:<br> °<br>0.5 ml/kg/hour (non-drinker)<br> °<br>0.77 ml/kg/hour (heavy alcohol user).<br>• May need to give alcohol for 2–3 days.<br>• Correct metabolic acidosis with sodium bicarbonate and f uid<br>replacement.<br>• To conf rm diagnosis, if possible, check osmolar gap, anion<br>gap and serum ethanol). In the early stages a gap of >19<br>mOsm/kgH2O may be indicative of ethylene glycol poisoning<br>if serum ethanol is 0 (if not subtract 24 mOsm/kgH2O per 100<br>mg/dl of ethanol). Later a raised anion-gap metabolic acidosis<br>develops.<br>•_Haemodialysis if there is a severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.25_<br>_or base def cit >15 mm despite buffer) or signs of end organ_<br>_toxicity, coma and seizures, renal failure, or signs of visual_<br>_disturbances._Consider peritoneal dialysis if haemodialysis not<br>available.<br>• Folinic acid 50 mg IV every 4 hours for 6 doses.|
|**Petrol, kerosene**<br>**and other volatile**<br>**hydrocarbons –**<br>**ingestion**|Nausea, vomiting,<br>abdominal pain,<br>haematemesis,<br>coughing,<br>shortness of breath,<br>tachypnoea,<br>pulmonary oedema,<br>coma.|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see Quick<br>Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35).<br>• If hypotension or shock, give IV f uids rapidly (see Quick Check<br>page 40 and Section 3.1).<br>• Do NOT induce vomiting, attempt gastric lavage, or give<br>activated charcoal.<br>• If acute lung injury, see Section 3.2.<br>• Observe for at least 6 hours for respiratory symptoms. If<br>asymptomatic, discharge.<br>• Immediate chest X-ray if symptomatic.|

**186** 

**3.8.2  Inhaled poisons** Inhaled poisons may take the form of gases, vapours, or aerosols. These may cause systemic toxicity (e.g. carbon monoxide, mercury vapour) or respiratory irritation (e.g. chlorine). 

|**Table: Inhaled poisons or toxins, symptoms of toxicity, and brief guidance on specif c**<br>**management**|**Table: Inhaled poisons or toxins, symptoms of toxicity, and brief guidance on specif c**<br>**management**|**Table: Inhaled poisons or toxins, symptoms of toxicity, and brief guidance on specif c**<br>**management**|
|---|---|---|
|**Poison or**<br>**toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|Carbon<br>monoxide|Mild to moderate toxicity:<br>dizziness, headache, nausea,<br>vomiting, weakness, and<br>confusion.<br>Severe toxicity: syncope,<br>tachypnoea, dyspnoea,<br>respiratory failure or pulmonary<br>oedema, coma, seizures, cerebral<br>oedema, cardiac dysrhythmias,<br>myocardial ischemia, bullous<br>lesions of the skin, muscle<br>necrosis, rhabdomyolysis,<br>compartment syndrome.<br>There may be delayed<br>neuropsychiatric complications.|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see<br>Quick Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• Give high-f ow oxygen aiming at 100% for 6–24 hours (see<br>Quick Check pages 33–35 and Section 3). Give regardless<br>of oxygen saturation and do not titrate.<br>•_Cardiac monitoring_and perform 12-lead ECG.<br>• Monitor urea, electrolytes and renal function, blood gases,<br>and pH.<br>•_Measure carboxyhaemoglobin level, if possible._<br>• Treat seizures (see Section 3.5).<br>• Give supportive care.<br>• If cerebral oedema is suspected, consider advanced<br>airway management for hyperventilation (see Quick Check<br>page 62).<br>• The benef ts of hyperbaric oxygen therapy in preventing<br>neurological complications are uncertain.<br>• Check if there are other victims.|
|Chlorine|Mild to moderate poisoning:<br>cough, shortness of breath,<br>chest pain, burning sensation in<br>the throat and substernal area,<br>nausea or vomiting, ocular and<br>nasal irritation, choking, muscle<br>weakness, dizziness, abdominal<br>discomfort, headache.<br>Severe poisoning: upper airway<br>oedema, laryngospasm, severe<br>non-cardiogenic pulmonary<br>oedema, pneumonia, persistent<br>hypoxemia, respiratory failure,<br>acute lung injury.|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see<br>Quick Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• Consider early intubation if stridor is present.<br>• If severe respiratory distress or SpO2<90, give oxygen.<br>• Give salbutamol for wheezing (see Quick Check page 37).<br>• Irrigate the eyes.<br>• Check peak f ow.<br>• Do chest X-ray if symptomatic.<br>• Monitor SpO2and electrolytes.<br>• Treat non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (see Section<br>3.2.3).|

Poison or<br>toxin Symptoms Management<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Poison or**<br>**toxin**|**Symptoms**|**Management**|
|---|---|---|

|Cyanide|Headache, dyspnoea,<br>confusion, coma, convulsions,<br>cardiovascular collapse,<br>metabolic acidosis|• Manage airway and assist ventilation as needed (see<br>Quick Check pages 29–32, 62).<br>• Give high-f ow oxygen aiming at 100% (see Quick Check<br>pages 33–35 and Section 3). Give regardless of oxygen<br>saturation and do not titrate.<br>• Measure lactate.<br>•_Correct persistent metabolic acidosis with sodium_<br>_bicarbonate._<br>• Severe toxicity (comatose patients):<br> °<br>Give sodium nitrite: 300 mg (10 ml of 3% solution) by<br>slow IV injection over 5–20 minutes.<br> °<br>Then give sodium thiosulphate: 12.5 g (50 ml of 25%<br>solution) by slow IV injection over 10 minutes.<br> °<br>If no response after 30 minutes, give further dose of<br>sodium nitrite 150 mg followed by sodium thiosulphate<br>12.5 g.<br>• Alternatively, hydroxocobalamin 5 grams IV over 15<br>minutes can be given, if available.<br>• Moderate toxicity (recovered from a period of<br>unconsciousness, convulsions, cyanosis), smoke inhalation<br>victim, or a presumed cyanide poisoning:<br> °<br>give sodium thiosulphate 12.5 g (83 ml of 15% solution)<br>by slow IV injection over 10 minutes.|



## **3.8.3  Chemicals on the skin or in the eye** 

## ~~**Health worker protection**~~ 

It is very important that the person administering fi rst aid wears appropriate protective clothing, e.g. gloves and apron to avoid exposing themselves to the chemicals. 

Remember, emergencies of the airway, breathing, and circulation take precedence. 

## ~~**Manage chemicals in the eye**~~ 

- Hold the eyes open (the patient may need a local anaesthetic to prevent blepharospasm). 

- Wash any chemicals out with cool, clean water for 15–20 minutes. Take care that run-off does not enter the other eye. In the case of acids or alkalis, check the pH of the conjunctival fl uid and continue irrigation until the pH is 7.4. 

- Do not let the patient rub the eyes. 

- Treat pain. 

- If light causes pain, cover the eye with a sterile pad. 

- Examine the eye (see Section 10.12). 

## ~~**Manage chemicals on the skin**~~ 

- Remove the patient’s clothing or ask the patient to do it. Avoid pulling clothes over the head. Cut clothing off if necessary. 

- Rinse the skin for about 15 minutes with large amounts of water. 

Poisoning 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**188** 

- In the case of alkali burns, rinse with water until the pH of the skin is neutral. 

- Watch for signs of poisoning from an absorbed chemical. 

- Consult a poison reference or a poison centre for advice on specifi c chemicals. 

- Put contaminated clothes in a sealable bag to protect against secondary contamination. 

## ~~**Manage organophosphates or carbamate on skin**~~ 

- Prevent further absorption by moving the patient to fresh air, removing contaminated clothing, and washing contaminated skin with soap and water. 

## ~~**Manage exposure to tear gas (e.g. CN or CS gas)**~~ 

- Tear gas is also called a ‘lacrimator’ because it irritates the mucous membranes of the eyes, causing a stinging sensation and tears. It may also irritate the upper respiratory tract , causing coughing, choking, and general debility. 

- Contaminated clothing may continue to emit gas for some time, affecting other people nearby. Therefore, if possible, have the victim remove clothing before entering the treatment area. 

- Follow the advice above for decontaminating eyes and skin. However, wash the skin with soap and water and then rinse with tepid water for 15 minutes.[6] Soothing lotions such as calamine can be applied to irritated skin once decontamination has been done. 

- 6 _Public health response to biological and chemical weapons._ WHO Guidance, 2004. Available at http://www.who. int/csr/delibepidemics/biochemguide/en/ 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

Poisoning 

**189** 

## **3.9  Snake-bite[1,2]** 

## **In this section:** 

- 3.9.1 Snake-bite assessment 

   - Establish the circumstances of the bite 

   - Clinical features and diagnosis 

- Table: Some snakes of medical importance and major features of envenomation 

- 3.9.2 Snake-bite treatment 

   - Treatment of systemic envenomation 

   - Manage complications 

   - Manage local necrosis and compartment syndrome 

   - Snake venom ophthalmia (cobra-spit) 

   - Manage muscle weakness (neurotoxicity) 

   - Manage bleeding from clotting factor defects 

   - Important myths 

Snake venoms vary considerably in their effect, ranging from venoms that produce no effects or minimal effects to venoms that are potentially life-threatening. Usually, there is a history of snake-bite, but snake-bite should also be considered in any patient with severe pain or swelling of a limb of unknown origin and when a patient with any unexplained illness presents with bleeding or abnormal neurological signs. 

## **3.9.1 Snake-bite assessment** 

## ~~**Establish the circumstances of the bite**~~ 

In most snake-bite victims the bite marks are obvious, and the majority of patients will experience signifi cant local pain. However, bites by neurotoxic snakes may be virtually painless and, in some cases, the bite site may be diffi cult to detect. In addition, not all snake-bites lead to signifi cant envenoming: 10–50% of bites may be “dry bites”, i.e. insuffi cient venom was injected to cause clinical effects. If there is any doubt, observe the patient closely. 

If a bite occurred, consider the following: 

- Time since the bite 

- Can the snake be identifi ed? Local knowledge is important to help identify the correct species. Also, some snakes change considerably in appearance during their life cycle. If there is any doubt, treat the bite as if it is from an unknown species. 

- Are there any obvious symptoms of envenoming? In some regions particular species may be associated with characteristic clinical syndromes (see Table: Some snakes of medical importance and major features of envenomation). 

> 1 _Guidelines for the prevention and clinical management of snake-bite in Africa._ Chapters 10, 12, 15. WHO Regional Offi ce for Africa, 2010. Available at http://www.afro.who.int/en/clusters-a-programmes/hss/essentialmedicines/highlights/2731-guidelines-for-the-prevention-and-clinical-management-of-snake-bite-in-africa.html 

> 2 _Guidelines for the management of snake-bites._ WHO Regional Offi ce for South-East Asia, 2010. Available at http://www.searo.who.int/LinkFiles/BCT_snake_bite_guidelines.pdf 

Snake-bite 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**190** 

## ~~**Clinical features and diagnosis**~~ 

Clinical assessment should be directed towards determining whether envenoming has occurred. Clinical features may not be apparent until many hours after the bite. Therefore, repeat serial assessment is required. 

Serial assessment includes the following: 

- Perform the Quick Check looking at Airway, Breathing, and Circulation (see Quick Check pages 17–19). 

- Examine the site of the bite for signs such as fang marks, local necrosis, blister formation, or bleeding. 

- Regional lymph nodes may be tender or enlarged. 

- Local swelling may gradually extend up the bitten limb. This may lead to a compartment syndrome. 

- Non-specifi c symptoms of systemic envenomation include nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, dizziness, and headache. 

- Assess for bleeding 

- ° external, from gums, wounds, or ulcers, needle puncture sites; 

- ° internal, especially intracranial, haematuria, and a prolonged whole blood clotting time. The 20-minute whole blood clotting time test (see below) should be performed routinely. Also see Sections 7.2.18 and 10.19. 

- Assess for signs of neurotoxicity, including: 

- ° ophthalmoplegia (ptosis), double vision, diffi culty swallowing (bulbar palsy) and talking, muscle weakness, diffi culty breathing, and fl accid paralysis with respiratory failure. 

- Assess for signs of muscle breakdown, including muscle pains and black urine (a urine dipstick test positive for blood is indicative of muscle breakdown resulting in myoglobinuria). 

It is diffi cult to give advice that can be generalized to all regions and situations, and local knowledge and adaptation of the management plan are important.[3] 

Note: Due to the wide spectrum of toxic components in snake venoms, a combination of clinical syndromes is common in individual snake-bite victims. See the table below with some snakes of medical importance and the major features of envenomation). 

## **Twenty-minute whole blood clotting test** 

2−3 ml of whole blood should be collected into a new, clean, dry, glass tube and allowed to stand at room temperature for 20 minutes. Tilt the tube gently to see if a clot has formed. The test is positive if blood has not clotted. The vessel must be glass rather than plastic in order to activate blood coagulation. Glass vessels may not activate coagulation, however, if they have been cleaned with detergent or are wet. 

> 3 Updated snake distributions maps are available at http://apps.who.int/bloodproducts/snakeantivenoms/ database/ 

Snake-bite 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**191** 

**4 Table: Some snakes of medical importance and major features of envenomation[4]**

Snake-bite

**192**

|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

|**Europe**|||||||

Note: This table provides a general guide only since there may be interspecies differences in the spectrum and severity of clinical effects.  Key: + mild, ++ moderate, +++ severe, ± may or may not be present 

## **3.9.2  Snake-bite treatment** 

Snake-bite victims are generally extremely anxious and restless. First aid measures include reassurance of the victim, immobilization of the bitten limb, and rapid transport to a medical facility. 

Some snake-bites lead to rapid onset of respiratory failure and cardiovascular collapse. Use Quick Check pages 17–18 for regular assessment of airway, breathing, and circulation. It is important to remove rings and bangles, as the swelling of limbs may worsen. Once the patient is in a medical facility, the most important aspect of management is to determine the need for antivenom and, if indicated, to administer it as soon as possible. 

## ~~**Treatment of systemic envenomation**~~ 

Antivenom is required if there is evidence of systemic envenomation (clinical or biochemical) from a venomous snake. Such evidence may include: 

- neurotoxicity 

- clotting disorder (spontaneous bleeding or a positive 20-minute whole blood clotting time) 

- muscle breakdown – muscle pains or black urine or a 3+ result for blood on a urinary dipstick 

- hypotension, shock, arrhythmia that persists 

- local necrosis or extensive swelling (more than half the bitten limb), rapidly progressive local swelling, bites on fi ngers and toes. 

If these symptoms and signs are not present, continue to observe the patient closely. On an hourly basis check the patient for weakness (including droopy eyelids and diffi culty swallowing), muscle strength, any breathing diffi culty and for signs of bleeding. Carry out a 20-minute whole blood clotting test if there is suspected bleeding or in suspected haemostatic snake-bite. 

In general, antivenom administration should not be started until there is evidence of systemic envenomation or local necrosis. If antivenom is not available, consider transferring the patient to a facility where antivenom is available (see Quick Check page 70). In the interim fl uid replacement, _administration of fresh frozen plasma or initiation of dialysis_ (see Section 11.31) should be considered in such situations. If the patient is in severe respiratory distress, see Quick Check pages 17−18 and consider advanced airway management (see Quick Check pages 62−67). 

Snake-bite 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**193** 

## **Administration of antivenom** 

## **Clinical points** 

- The dose required depends on the quantity of venom injected; therefore, the dose is not related to whether the patient is an adult or a child. 

- Antivenom should always be given intravenously. 

- Epinephrine (adrenaline) should be available for use immediately in case of anaphylaxis. For management of anaphylaxis, see Quick Check page 17 and Section 3.1.3. 

- Antivenoms are more effective if given early (within hours of envenomation). However, improvement is possible even days after envenomation from some snakes. 

## **Expected response to an antivenom** 

- Systemic symptoms usually improve over hours. 

- Clotting usually corrects itself over a number of hours (depending on the type of snake). Repeat a 20-minute whole blood clotting test after 3−6 hours. 

- Weakness tends to stop worsening, but may not immediately get better. 

- Local necrosis will not be reversed but should not progress. 

- Muscle breakdown may stop progressing, but kidney failure may still occur. 

## **Reasons for a patient’s failure to respond to an antivenom** 

- It could be the wrong type of antivenom (particularly if monospecifi c). 

- The antivenom could be inactive or not effi cient. 

- There was an insuffi cient dose. 

- There was an excessive delay after envenomation in administration of the antivenom. 

## ~~**Manage complications**~~ 

All patients with snake-bite envenomation should be monitored for development of complications. This requires regular clinical examination (respiratory rate, breath volumes by observation or with _spirometry_ , pulse and blood pressure; signs of compartment syndrome and gangrene), review of charts (urine output and urine colour, temperature) and biochemical investigations (serum potassium, creatinine, and clotting profi le). 

Prevention of renal failure requires adequate fl uid intake. A deteriorating level of consciousness may be an indicator of intracerebral haemorrhage. 

## ~~**Manage local necrosis and compartment syndrome**~~ 

The degree of local necrosis depends on the type of venom. Early administration of antivenom is the best way to prevent muscle damage. Compartment syndrome is rare and is diffi cult to distinguish from local tissue necrosis. 

- Give analgesia for pain. 

- It is important to involve a surgeon if there is signifi cant swelling of digits or a limb. 

- Fasciotomy should be considered only if: 

- ° there is clinical evidence of compartment syndrome (disproportionately severe pain, weakness of intracompartmental muscles, pain on passive stretching of intracompartmental muscles, hypoaesthesia of areas of skin supplied by nerves running through the compartment, and obvious tenseness of the compartment on palpation); and 

- ° the intracompartmental pressure has been measured and is >40 mmHg (in adults); and 

- ° clotting disorders have been corrected with antivenom. 

- Infection is uncommon 

- ° Antibiotics should be given only if there is a necrotic wound or signs of an established infection (e.g. local area is red, hot, swollen, and fl uctuant). 

- Tetanus toxoid vaccine should be given routinely to unvaccinated patients. 

Snake-bite 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**194** 

## ~~**Snake venom ophthalmia (cobra-spit)**~~ 

Following venom contact with the eye, the cornea should be irrigated with large volumes of clean water, and a clean pad and topical antibiotic ointment (e.g. tetracycline) applied. If necessary, use a single dose of a topical local anaesthetic to help open the eyelid so as to properly cleanse the eye. Consider the use of 0.1% epinephrine eye drops to relieve the burning sensation. Diluted antivenom is not recommended. 

## ~~**Manage muscle weakness (neurotoxicity)**~~ 

The use of polyvalent antivenom usually will not prevent the progression of neurotoxic effects in the acute phase, in particular respiratory paralysis, and the patient will not survive without life support. Late administration of antivenom may reverse weakness after envenomation by some snakes. If antivenom is not available, respiratory failure should be managed with assisted ventilation until spontaneous recovery occurs. 

Monitor the patient closely for signs of progressive muscle weakness 

- Early signs of neurotoxicity include droopy eyelids, double vision, diffi culty swallowing, and drooling of saliva. These may indicate impending respiratory paralysis. 

- Late signs of neurotoxicity include generalized weakness and weakness of the respiratory muscles. As the respiratory muscles become weak, the patient will breathe at a faster rate, take small shallow, and eventually use accessory muscles to breathe. 

- Hypoxaemia is an ominous sign; usually, it is due to inadequate ventilation or oxygenation (see Section 3.2.1). When SpO2 is <90, give oxygen (see Quick Check pages 33−35). This is a temporary measure, as giving oxygen alone will NOT improve ventilation. 

If ventilation is inadequate, assist ventilation with BVM (see Quick Check page 31). For cases that are easily reversible, BVM can continue until antivenom takes effect. In neurotoxic snake-bite, anticipate a prolonged course of weakness and consider advanced airway management with tracheal intubation (see Quick Check pages 62−67) if local manual ventilation is feasible or transfer to a hospital where mechanical ventilator is available. 

Advanced airway management should be considered if there are signs of bulbar palsy (drooling, diffi culty swallowing, aspiration), as these are signs that the patient can no longer properly protect the airway. 

Patients with neurotoxic symptoms, except those thought to have been bitten by mambas, should be given an anticholinesterase test. Ideally, edrophonium is used for this because it is short-acting; however, edrophonium is rarely available, and neostigmine can be used as an alternative. Neostigmine is widely used by anaesthetists to reverse non-depolarizing (competitive) neuromuscular blockade. 

Steps in the anticholinesterase test 

1. Take baseline observations for comparison. 

2. Then give atropine sulphate (0.6 mg for adults) by slow intravenous injection to block the unpleasant and potentially serious muscarinic effects of acetylcholine (such as colic). 

Snake-bite 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**195** 

3. Then give _edrophonium chloride_ (10 mg in adults) by slow intravenous injection, or, if edrophonium is not available, use neostigmine bromide by intramuscular injection – 0.02 mg/kg for adults. 

4. A convincing response is increased muscle power or improvement in ptosis. 

If the patient has a convincing positive response, maintain on neostigmine, 0.5–2.5 mg every 1−3 hours up to 10 mg/24 hours maximum for adults by IV/IM or SC injection, together with atropine as above. 

## ~~**Manage bleeding from clotting factor defects**~~ 

(See Section 10.19 Abnormal bleeding and bruising) 

- Spontaneous systemic bleeding usually stops within 15–30 minutes, and blood coagulation is restored within about 6 hours if an adequate dose of antivenom has been given. The 20 minute whole blood clotting test should be used to monitor the dose of antivenom in patients with coagulopathy. If the blood remains uncoagulated 6 hours after the fi rst dose, the dose should be repeated every 6 hours until blood coagulation is restored. 

- If the patient starts bleeding excessively, correct with _fresh frozen plasma, platelets or cryoprecipitates_ in addition to antivenom. If these blood products are not available, use fresh whole blood (see Section 10.19). 

- Heparin should not be given. 

- Central venous lines and surgery should not be attempted unless clotting has been corrected with antivenom. 

## ~~**Manage muscle breakdown (rhabdomyolysis)**~~ 

- An early sign includes muscle pain and a positive urine dipstick test for blood (cross-reacting with myoglobin from muscle). 

- Late signs include dark urine and renal failure. 

- Give IV LR or NS fl uids (more than 3 litres per day). Keep patient very well hydrated by maintaining the JVP (visually) to be slightly higher than normal, and use furosemide when appropriate (see Section 11.31). 

- Urine output should be monitored, and the rate of fl uid administration adjusted accordingly. 

- Correct acidosis and electrolyte disturbances. 

- Haemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis may be required to treat acute renal failure and associated complications such as hyperkalaemia and acidosis (see Section 11.31). 

## ~~**Important myths**~~ 

1. “Any antivenom will do” – FALSE. 

- [Antivenoms are very specifi c to the type of snake. For example, antivenom ] made for snakes in India will not be effective for snakes in Papua New Guinea. However, many antivenoms are polyvalent. This means that the venoms of more than one snake (there may be 10 or more) are used in their preparation. 

2. “Cut the bite out” – FALSE. 

- [This may result in more extensive injuries than caused by the snake. If ] clotting problems are present, the patient may bleed to death. 

Snake-bite 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**196** 

3. “Tying a tourniquet stops the poison spreading” – FALSE. 

- [Cutting off the blood supply may not stop the venom spreading, and it may ] endanger the limb through lack of blood. 

4. “Snake-bite pills” and other herbal remedies are effective in treating snakebites – FALSE. 

- [Intravenous antivenom is the only specifi c treatment for snake-bite. No oral ] tablets, plant extracts, or treatments applied directly on the skin have been shown to reverse the effects of venom. This includes the use of special “black stones”, coals, or ash. 

Other false myths include the use of scarifi cation, injection of the wound with Condy’s crystals, the use of electric current, and sucking on the wound. 

Snake-bite 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**197** 

## **3.10  Burns[1]** 

## **In this section:** 

- 3.10.1 Initial management and stabilization of burns using Quick Check 

   - Airway and breathing 

   - Circulation 

   - Remove all burned clothing, and cool skin with water. 

   - Manage associated trauma. 

- Cover the burn to reduce pain, and provide appropriate analgesia. 

- 3.10.2 Assess and classify the burn 

   - Determine the degree of the burn 

   - Estimate the extent of the burn 

   - Types of burns 

- Classify the burn to decide how to manage it 

- 3.10.3 Burn management 

   - Manage inhalation injury 

   - Fluid resuscitation in patients with severe burns 

   - Burn skin care 

Burns are a severe form of trauma that can cause signifi cant soft tissue injury as well as metabolic changes affecting fl uid balance. Extensive burns are a lifethreatening emergency. The extent of the burn, extremes of age, co-morbidities, and the circumstances surrounding the injury all will infl uence patient outcome. 

## **3.10.1  Initial management and stabilization of burns using Quick Check** 

## ~~**Airway and breathing**~~ 

- Consider early intubation or tracheotomy for any burns of the face, anterior neck, and upper chest to protect from laryngeal swelling. 

- Administer oxygen to all patients with Quick Check emergency signs, severe burns (>15% of total body surface area (TBSA) or airway involvement), altered mental status, SpO2 <90, or suspicion of carbon monoxide poisoning (smoke inhalation, fi re in enclosed space). 

## ~~**Circulation**~~ 

- Insert IV. Calculate amount of fl uids according to the Parkland formula for patients with severe burns and Quick Check emergency signs. 

## **Parkland formula** 

calculates the amount of fl uid to be administered over the fi rst 24 hours post-burn; 4 ml x body weight in kg x percentage burns per TBSA 

> 1 _Surgical care at the district hospital._ WHO, 2003. Available at www.who.int/child_adolescent_health/ documents/9241545755/en/index.html and _Integrated management for emergency and essential surgical care._ WHO, 2003. Available at www.who.int/surgery/publications/imeesc/en/index.html 

Burns 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**198** 

## ~~**Remove all burned clothing, and cool skin with water.**~~ 

- If the burn is acute, apply cool, wet towels for 30 minutes to cool the burn. 

- Beware of hypothermia. 

## ~~**Manage associated trauma.**~~ 

## ~~**Cover the burn to reduce pain, and provide appropriate analgesia.**~~ 

## **3.10.2  Assess and classify the burn** 

## ~~**Determine the degree of the burn**~~ 

The degree of the burn indicates its depth and severity and determines if surgery will be required. 

|**1st degree**|superf cial|red or pink, painful, skin intact, no blisters|
|---|---|---|
|**2nd degree**|superf cial or deep partial thickness|red, blisters, wet, painful, blanches|
|**3rd degree**|full thickness|white or black/leathery, no sensation, dry|



Experienced burn doctors often reserve judgement on the defi nitive classifi cation of the burn until they have examined the wounds at 72 hours after the injury. 

- First-degree burns usually will heal with minimal sequelae, even without treatment. 

- Second-degree burns will heal, but often with signifi cant scarring and contractures. 

- Third-degree burns will heal (if at all) by contracture and cause severe scarring and disability. Third-degree burns also may include injury to the muscles or tendons. 

Skin grafting is indicated for deeper second-degree burns and third-degree burns to improve cosmetic and functional outcome. If the wound is not epithelialized by 21 days, it should be grafted. 

## ~~**Estimate the extent of the burn (relative to TBSA)**~~ 

- Determine the percentage of area burned using the “rule of 9s”, whereby the body is divided into 9 areas or parts. 

- If the burns do not fully cover a body part or cover more than one part, the percentage can be calculated by using the patient’s palm as approximately equal to 1% of the TBSA. 

- If a second- or third-degree burn involves the face, neck, hands, feet, or perineum or is circumferential (encircles a limb), it should be treated as a severe burn, and surgical referral is indicated, even if the TBSA is small. 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

Burns 

**199** 

## **Estimating the burned surface area in adults The rule of 9s** 

## ~~**Types of burns**~~ 

**Flame burns** are the most common. A history of a fl ame burn in an enclosed space suggests inhalation injury. Look for soot in the mouth and burned hairs in the nose. Strongly consider airway protection before laryngeal swelling makes intubation too diffi cult. Flame burns often are deep, with feathered edges of partial-thickness burn. Clean off soot and loose skin with soap and water. 

**Scald burns.** It can be very diffi cult to assess the full depth of a scald burn in the fi rst few hours. It may not be apparent until the third day. 

**Contact burns** usually are small but very deep, down to muscle, and likely to require excision and grafting. 

**Grease burns.** Cooking oil is usually very hot. These are typically deep, partialthickness or full-thickness wounds. 

**Electrical fl ash burns.** These occur when a screwdriver or other conductive tool is inserted into a live electrical box. There is an extremely hot fl ash, but electricity does not travel through the body. Such burns typically involve the face and hands. Examine the patient’s eyes with fl uorescein and blue light for corneal damage. If corneal damage is present, treat with antibiotic eye drops or ointment. Even if there is no smoke involved, electrical fl ash burns can cause laryngeal swelling, and airway protection needs to be considered. Otherwise, treat as a thermal burn. 

**Electrical conduction burns.** These result from conduction of high voltage electricity through the body. If the patient is conscious, there may be a history of the “can’t let go” phenomenon: The patient was unable to let go of the electric wire or other source. On the surface, burns are typically only small entrance and exit wounds, but suspect massive underlying tissue injury. Look for cardiac arrhythmias and fractures. Destruction of muscle leads to myoglobinuria and renal 

Burns 

Vol. 1 • 3. Approach to the severely ill patient: July 2011 

**200** 

failure (see Section 11.31). In all cases insert a urinary catheter. If the urine is dark, raise the pH of the urine by giving large volumes of 5% dextrose with 150 mEq sodium bicarbonate per litre. (Putting bicarbonate in normal saline will yield a very hypertonic solution.) Give mannitol boluses and furosemide. Assess compartment pressures in the affected limbs and perform early fasciectomy. Remember that compartment pressures will rise with fl uid resuscitation, and so re-examine the patient frequently. 

**Chemical burns.** While caused by a wide variety of chemicals, acid and alkali burns are the most common. Always protect staff fi rst! First, dust off any dry chemical, then wash the whole body for 40 minutes or more in running water to dilute the chemical. Irrigate the eyes thoroughly. 

## ~~**Classify the burn to decide how to manage it**~~
