A “pop” or sudden change in the density of the tissue being penetrated may occur, indicating that the pericardium has been accessed. Sanguineous pericardial fl uid may be distinguished from blood by dropping a small amount onto a clean, dry sponge. If it is pericardial fl uid, the resulting spot should appear much lighter than blood. 

4. In the emergency or tamponade situation, the removal of even 50 ml may at least temporarily improve haemodynamics. 

5. No more than 1 litre of fl uid at a time should be aspirated in order to avoid acute right ventricular dilatation. 

## **Investigations** 

- Gram stain, chemistry and culture 

- cytology 

- if tuberculous pericarditis is suspected, perform adenosine deaminase and send for mycobacterial culture. 

## **Complications** 

- Myocardial or coronary vessel laceration may present in a delayed fashion as hemopericardium or cardiac tamponade – (see Quick Check page 20 for immediate management and call for surgical help). 

- Acute left or right ventricular failure with pulmonary oedema (see Quick Check page 20 and Section 3.2.5). 

- Arrhythmia – obtain ECG and treat according to national guidelines. If the 

Vol. 1 • 7. Procedures: July 2011 

Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures 

**337** 

patient appears to be in shock, with fast heart rate and low blood pressure, see Quick Check page 20 for immediate management. 

- Pneumothorax (see Quick Check page 46 and Section 4.2 Trauma for immediate management). If signifi cant, the patient will require a chest tube. 

- Air embolism – if the patient becomes unstable, with fast breathing, fast heart rate, low blood pressure, or focal neurological defi cits, see Quick Check page 20 for immediate management. 

- Puncture of peritoneal cavity or abdominal organs. If the patient develops severe abdominal pain and tenderness, an abdominal organ may have been punctured. See Quick Check page 24 for immediate management and call for surgical help. 

Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures 

Vol. 1 • 7. Procedures: July 2011 

**338**

## **8.  Medicines/therapies** 

Section 8 covers only the treatment recommendations for conditions covered in this manual and does not serve as a comprehensive list of indications for each medicine. Also, it does not include routine contraception, vaccines, or immunoglobulin therapy. 

Information on medicines in this section is drawn from the WHO Model Formulary,[1] Pharmacological treatments of mental disorders in primary health care[2] , manufacturers’ product literature, UpToDate[3] , and evidence-based formularies such the British National Formulary[4] and Australian Medicines handbook.[5] Other sources include the Sanford Guide to Antimicrobial Therapy.[6] 

These summaries do not cover all adverse reactions and interactions. The information given should be interpreted in the light of professional knowledge and by reference to the approved product information for the individual drugs and should be supplemented as necessary by specialist advice. 

Where deemed important, the principles of prescribing and medicine administration are dealt with in the relevant Sections of the manual. For example, principles of prescribing in mental health disorders are in Section 10.11. 

Where possible, adverse reactions are classifi ed according to their probable incidence: common = incidence of 1% or more; infrequent or rare = incidence of less than 1%. 

Section 8 is subdivided into: 

## **8.1 Analgesics for pain relief** 

## **8.2 Information on equivalence for interchangeability- corticosteroids** 

## **8.3 Iron content of different salts** 

## **8.4 Information on individual medicines in adolescents and adults** 

Section 8 should be adapted in each country to match national guidelines and essential medicines lists. Please also refer to the updated WHO essential medicines list and the _2008 WHO Model Formulary._[1] 

The medicines list in 8.4 is not comprehensive. For a more complete list and for specifi c medicine interaction information, see the _2008 WHO Model Formulary_ .[1] 

> 1 _WHO Model Formulary._ WHO, 2008. Available at www.who.int/selection_medicines/list/WMF2008.pdf 

> 2 _Pharmacological treatment of mental disorders in primary health care._ WHO, 2009. 

> 3 _UpToDate,_ available at http://www.uptodate.com/store. 

> 4 Joint Formulary Committee (2010). _British National Formulary._ 60th ed. London, British Medical Association and Royal Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain. Available through HINARI (http://extranet.who.int/hinari/en/ journals.php). 

- 5 Rossi, S. ed, _Australian Medicines Handbook 2008._ Adelaide, Medicines Handbook Pty Ltd, 2008. 

- 6 Gilbert DN, Ellopoulos GM, Moellering RC, Saag MS, Chambers HF, eds. _The Sanford Guide to Antimicrobial Therapy._ 40th ed. Sperryville, Antimicrobial Therapy, 2010. 

Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**343** 

For renal and hepatic impairment drug dosage adjustments, please refer to appendices 4 and 5 in _2008 WHO Model Formulary_[1] and Section 11.31 in this manual. 

Some of the other common or important indications for the medicines that are not addressed in this manual are listed in the left column under “Other indications”. 

Special advice for prescribing medications for the elderly 

- Drugs that commonly cause problems in the elderly include hypnotics, diuretics, nonsteroidal anti-infl ammatory drugs, antihypertensives, psychotropics, and digoxin. 

- For some medications, start low, go slow, expect unusual side-effects and drug interactions. 

- See Section 18 Geriatric care.

## **8.4  Summary of medicines/therapies in adolescents and adults** 

## **Note: These summaries do not include dosing or administration to children less than 10 years of age.** 

References to Sections in this manual are in parentheses. 

Medicines not included in the WHO Model Formulary are in italics. 

Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**346** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not recommended during<br>pregnancy or breastfeeding.<br>**Use with caution:**Renal or hepatic impairment (give lower<br>doses)<br>Does not alter CNS effects of drinking alcohol or withdrawal<br>symptoms|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Avoid in f rst trimester of<br>pregnancy.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, acidosis,<br>severe renal impairment, severe hepatic impairment, chronic<br>angle-closure glaucoma (may mask deterioration)<br>**Use with caution:**In the elderly, diabetes mellitus, gout,<br>history of renal stones, sulfonamide allergy<br>**Counselling:**Take tablets with meals to reduce the risk of<br>stomach upset.|**Pregnancy/breast feeding:**Clinical experience indicates that<br>use of acetylcysteine for treatment of paracetamol overdose<br>is effective and benef ts outweigh risks.<br>**Use with caution:**In patients with asthma or history of<br>bronchospasm, but do not delay acetylcysteine treatment<br>**Administration:**IV: Dilute requisite dose in glucose<br>intravenous infusion 5% as follows: initially 200 ml given<br>over 15 minutes, then 500 ml over 4 hours, then 1 litre over<br>16 hours.<br>Oral: Dilute to a 5% solution in soda pop, juice, or water prior<br>to oral or nasogastric administration.<br>**Storage:**A change in the colour of the solution to light purple<br>has sometimes been noted; this colour change is not thought<br>to indicate signif cant impairment of safety or eff cacy. Store<br>below 25°C.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Pruritus, maculo-papular rash;<br>diarrhoea; changes in libido<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea, vomiting, abdominal<br>pain; bullous skin reactions, angioedema,<br>anaphylactic reactions|**Common:**Paresthesia (of hands, face, feet, or<br>mucocutaneous junctions), fatigue, drowsiness,<br>depression, decreased libido, bitter or metallic<br>taste; nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps,<br>diarrhoea, black faeces; polyuria, renal<br>stones; metabolic acidosis, hypokalaemia,<br>hyponatraemia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Transient myopia;<br>Stevens-Johnson syndrome; aplastic<br>anaemia (especially in the f rst 6 months),<br>thrombocytopenia, agranulocytosis, neutropenia|**Common:**Flushing, urticaria, itch<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Anaphylactoid or<br>hypersensitivity-like” reactions including<br>angioedema, bronchospasm/<br>respiratory distress, hypotension, and, rarely,<br>tachycardia or hypertension. Usually occur<br>15–60 minutes after start of infusion. (Manage<br>by reducing infusion rate or suspending<br>infusion until reaction has settled; stop infusion<br>if severe anaphylaxis occurs; rash – use an<br>antihistamine; acute asthma – use short-acting<br>beta2 agonist such as salbutamol.)<br>Once an anaphylactoid reaction is under<br>control, infusion can normally be restarted at<br>the lowest infusion rate (100 mg/kg in 1 litre over<br>16 hours).|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 333 mg enteric coated_**<br>>55 kg: 2 tablets 3 times daily.<br><55 kg: 2 tablets twice daily|**_Tablet: 250 mg_**<br>Oral: Initially<br>250 mg then 500-750 mg per day<br>in divided doses until patient<br>reaches  referral care.|**_Injection: 200 mg/ml (intended_**<br>**_for antidotal, not mucolytic,_**<br>**_use) Oral: 100 mg/ml_**<br>IV: Initially 150 mg/kg over<br>15 minutes; then 50 mg/kg over<br>4 hours; then 100 mg/kg over<br>16 hours<br>Oral: Loading dose of 140 mg/kg;<br>THEN<br>4 hours after loading dose,<br>initiate maintenance dose of<br>70 mg/kg administered at 4-hour<br>intervals for 17 doses.<br>Continue until 72 hours<br>post-ingestion (longer if LFTs<br>abnormal).|
|**D rug Indication**|**Acamprosate**<br>Maintenance of abstinence<br>in alcohol dependence<br>(16.5)|**Acetazolamide**<br>Pre-operative treatment<br>of acute angle-closure<br>glaucoma<br>(10.12.2)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Adjunctive treatment<br>of chronic open-angle<br>glaucoma)|**Acetylcysteine**<br>Paracetamol overdosage<br>(3.8.1)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**347** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Precautions in the f rst and<br>second  trimesters – low doses probably not harmful.<br>Consider alternative for analgesia. Use not recommended<br>in third trimester due to impaired platelet function and risk<br>of haemorrhage. Delayed onset and increased duration of<br>labour with increased blood loss; avoid analgesic doses if<br>possible in last few weeks (low doses probably not harmful);<br>with high doses, closure of fetal ductus arteriosus i_n utero_<br>and possibly persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn;<br>kernicterus in jaundiced neonates.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Short course safe in breastfeeding at<br>usual dosage (monitor infant, regular use of high doses<br>could produce hypoprothrombinaemia in infant if neonatal<br>vitamin K stores low; possible risk of Reye syndrome; avoid<br>breastfeeding 1–2 hours after dose to minimise infant<br>exposure.<br>**Contraindications:**In hypersensitivity (including asthma,<br>angioedema, urticaria or rhinitis) to acetylsalicylic acid<br>or any other NSAID, active peptic ulceration, bleeding<br>disorders, and in children and adolescents under 16 years<br>(Reye syndrome) Not for treatment of gout<br>**Use with caution:**in asthma, heart failure, uncontrolled<br>hypertension, allergic disease, previous peptic ulceration,<br>renal impairment, hepatic impairment, G6PD def ciency,<br>dehydration, and in the elderly<br>**Administration:**Give with food, large quantities (~240 ml)<br>of water (unless f uid restricted) or milk to minimize gastric<br>irritation.<br>**Counselling:**If you develop swollen ankles, diff culty in<br>breathing, black stools or vomit that looks like coffee grounds<br>stop taking the medicine.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Gastrointestinal irritation with slight<br>blood loss, tinnitus; deafness (large doses),<br>nausea, dyspepsia, vomiting, increased bleeding<br>time, headache, dizziness<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Steven Johnson syndrome,<br>iron def ciency anaemia, renal impairment,<br>oesophageal ulceration, major GI bleeding,<br>blood dyscrasias, Reyes syndrome.<br>Allergy, bronchospasm angioedema, urticaria or<br>rhinitis in hypersensitive patients (particularly in<br>people with asthma)<br>* If overdose, see Section 3.8.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 100 mg, 300 mg_**<br>Oral: 300 mg (preferably chewed<br>or dispersed in water) given<br>immediately as a single dose<br>300 mg–600 mg every 4 hours as<br>needed; higher doses (900mg<br>) may be useful for analgesia<br>in some patients; maximum<br>4 grams in 24 hours|
|**D rug Indication**|**Acetylsalicylic acid**<br>**(aspirin)**<br>Acute coronary syndrome/<br>myocardial infarction<br>(Quick Check page 20)<br>Mild to moderate pain (20.2,<br>2.4); fever (10.1.2)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>acute ischaemic<br>stroke; prophylaxis of<br>cerebrovascular disease<br>or myocardial infarction;<br>acute migraine attack;<br>inf ammatory arthritis)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**348** 

|**Special groups/comments**|P**regnancy:**Precautions in the f rst trimester<br>**Breastfeeding:**Considered safe<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, with concurrent<br>administration of other nephrotoxic drugs, use of IV form<br>in patients with underlying neurological abnormalities, in<br>elderly patients<br>**Administration:**Maintain adequate hydration with IV use<br>(balance with risk of cerebral oedema in patients with<br>encephalitis).<br>Infuse IV dose over 1 hour to prevent renal damage.<br>Ensure adequate f uid intake<br>(1.5–2 litres/day), which should also be maintained with oral<br>administration to prevent crystallization in the renal tubules.<br>Changes in renal function during treatment usually respond<br>to rehydration and/or dosage reduction.<br>**Counselling:**Drink plenty of f uids (at least<br>1.5–2 litres/daily). Tablet can be dissolved in water if desired.<br>If taking 5 times daily, take every 4 hours during waking<br>hours.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>hallucinations (with high dose), headaches,<br>lethargy, confusion, seizures<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hypersensitivity reactions;<br>agitation, vertigo, dizziness, weakness;<br>oedema, renal impairment; arthralgia;<br>sore throat; abdominal pain, constipation;<br>hepatitis, jaundice; blood disorders (anaemia,<br>thrombocytopenia, leukopenia), Stevens-<br>Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis,<br>anaphylaxis<br>Severe local inf ammation on<br>intravenous infusion|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 200 mg, 400 mg, 800 mg_**<br>**_Oral suspension: 200 mg/5 ml_**<br>**_Infusion: 250 mg vial_**<br>**_Eye ointment: 3% W/W_**<br>Oral: 400 mg 3 times daily<br>OR 200 mg 5 times daily for<br>7–10 days (longer if new lesions<br>appear or incomplete healing)<br>Oral: 200 mg 3–5 times daily OR<br>400 mg twice daily Interrupt<br>every 6–12 months for<br>reassessment.<br>IV: 10 mg/kg 3 times daily for<br>14–21 days<br>Oral: 200 mg 5 times daily OR<br>400 mg 3 times daily for 7–10 days<br>Oral: 400 mg orally 5 times daily<br>for 14–21 days<br>Oral: 400 mg twice daily AND ART<br>See dosing in 11.45.1<br>Oral: 800 mg 5 times daily for<br>7 days (begin within 72 hours<br>of appearance of rash to be<br>effective) AND 3% eye<br>ointment every 4 hours if<br>ophthalmic involvement<br>Apply 1 cm ointment 3% directly<br>to eye 5 times daily; continue<br>for at least 3 days after healing<br>is complete. OR oral aciclovir<br>400 mg 5 times daily until<br>healing is complete|
|**D rug Indication**|**Aciclovir**<br>Herpes simplex virus (HSV);<br>genital herpes, initial or<br>recurrent episode<br>(11.15)<br>Prophylaxis for recurrent<br>herpes simplex (chronic<br>suppression)(11.15)<br>HSV encephalitis or<br>hepatitis (11.15)<br>HSV oesophagitis (11.15,<br>10.7b.3):<br>• If NOT immuno-<br>compromised<br>• If immuno-compromised<br>• HSV oesophagitis<br>suppression<br>Varicella (chickenpox)<br>(11.45.1)<br>Herpes zoster (shingles)<br>(11.45.2)<br>Herpes keratitis (10.12.2)|

**349** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Recommended for use only during<br>the second and third trimesters of pregnancy and only when<br>there are no alternatives and benef t outweighs risk.<br>**Administration:**Check liver function tests and blood counts<br>before longer-term treatment and twice during each cycle<br>(every 2 weeks). Cease therapy if enzymes greater than<br>twice normal limit.<br>**Counselling:**Chew and take on an empty stomach.<br>**Contraindications:**Do not co-administer ivermectin if<br>onchocerciasis or loaisis are co-endemic with f lariasis.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not recommended in pregnancy<br>or breastfeeding; consider alternatives.<br>**Contraindications:**Hyperkalaemia, renal failure<br>**Use with caution:**In  the elderly, debilitated patients<br>with cardiopulmonary disease or uncontrolled type 1<br>diabetes; in patients with cirrhosis, may precipitate renal<br>failure, hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis, hepatic<br>encephalopathy; with medicines that can increase potassium<br>concentration (ACE inhibitors)<br>**Counselling:**Take in the morning. Dizziness with standing<br>may occur.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**During treatment of<br>neurocysticercosis, neurological symptoms<br>(fever, headache, worsening of disease,<br>probably due to CNS reaction to dying parasites)<br>**Infrequent or rare:**GI intolerance, increase<br>in liver enzymes, reversible alopecia,<br>rash, reversible leukopenia, bone marrow<br>suppression (pancytopenia, aplastic anaemia,<br>agranulocytosis), Stevens-Johnson syndrome|**Common:**Hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia,<br>hypochloraemia (especially when combined<br>with thiazide diuretics), weakness, headache,<br>nausea/vomiting, constipation, impotence,<br>dizziness, muscle cramps<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Diarrhoea, anorexia, dry<br>mouth, abdominal pain, f atulence, polyuria,<br>rash, pruritus, visual disturbances, mild<br>psychiatric disturbances, palpitations|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 400 mg (chewable)_**<br>Oral: 400 mg twice daily for 3<br>days (consider longer course in<br>immunocompro-mised patients).<br>THEN<br>maintenance at 400 mg monthly.<br>Oral: 15 mg/kg daily for 8 days<br>Oral: 400 mg as a single dose +<br>diethyl-carbamazine<br>OR<br>albendazole 400 mg/twice daily<br>for 2 weeks +<br>ivermectin<br>as a single dose<br>Oral: 400 mg as a single dose<br>Oral: 400 mg as a single dose<br>every 6 months|**Tablet: 5 mg**<br>Oral: 10 mg daily in 1–2 divided<br>doses; give in combination with<br>furosemide|
|**D rug Indication**|**Albendazole**<br>Strongyloidiasis (if<br>ivermectin not available)<br>(11.36)<br>Cysticercosis, as alternative<br>to praziquantel in<br>uncomplicated cases (11.7)<br>Filariasis<br>(11.12)<br>Ascaris (10.7), hookworm<br>(10.18)<br>• Treatment<br>• Prophylaxis|**Amiloride**<br>Oedema<br>(10.4.4)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Liver cirrhosis)|

**350** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Manufacturer advises against use unless<br>essential, particularly during f rst and third trimesters.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use with caution if indicated and if the drug<br>of choice; reversible withdrawal symptoms and adverse<br>effects possible (monitor infant for drowsiness). Preferably,<br>give as a single nightly dose after breastfeeding.<br>**Contraindications:**Patient has taken an MAO-I within<br>2 weeks, recent myocardial infarction, arrhythmias<br>(especially heart block), bipolar disorder, severe liver<br>disease. Do not use in adolescents.<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly, patients with cardiac<br>history; epilepsy, hepatic impairment, thyroid disease,<br>pheochromocytoma, history of mania, psychoses, angle-<br>closure glaucoma, history of urinary retention, concurrent<br>electroconvulsive therapy, anaesthesia. May increase the<br>risk of suicidal thinking and behaviour. Prone to multiple<br>signif cant drug interactions.<br>**Counselling:**Blurred vision and dry mouth may be<br>troublesome but may lessen or disappear after about 7 days.<br>Try to take it at night to reduce daytime drowsiness. You<br>may feel dizzy on standing when taking this medicine; get up<br>gradually from sitting or lying to minimize this effect.<br>Avoid driving and operating machinery until you know how<br>you react to this medicine.<br>Do not stop taking the medicine suddenly. If you suddenly<br>stop taking amitriptyline, you may experience withdrawal<br>symptoms such as nausea, headache, and lack of energy.<br>Your clinician will probably decrease your dose gradually.<br>May increase the effects of alcohol.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Orthostatic hypotension (fall risk),<br>dizziness, sedation, dry mouth, constipation,<br>nausea, diff culty urinating, blurred vision,<br>headache, confusion, disorientation, increased<br>liver enzymes, worsening depression, anxiety,<br>insomnia, ejaculatory problems, impotence,<br>appetite and weight changes<br>**Serious side-effects:**Cardiac arrhythmias, heart<br>attack, stroke, seizures, hyperthermia, heat<br>stroke, mania/hypomania<br>If overdose, see Section 3.8.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 25 mg, 50 mg_**<br>Oral: Start 50 mg at bedtime<br>AND<br>Increase gradually as<br>necessary by 25–50 mg every<br>1–2 weeks to<br>100–150 mg daily.<br>Maximum dose of 200 mg daily<br>for depression; up to 300 mg for<br>neuropathic pain (single dose,<br>preferably at bedtime or in<br>divided doses).<br>Note: delay in onset of effect<br>For elderly and medically ill:<br>Start at 25 mg at night, can be<br>increased gradually to maximum<br>tolerated dose of  100 mg per<br>day|
|**D rug Indication**|**Amitriptyline**<br>Depression (10.11.6);<br>neuropathic pain (10.10a);<br>herpetic neuralgia (11.45)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy**: Not known to be harmful<br>**Breastfeeding:**Considered safe<br>**Contraindications:**Known hypersensitivity/ anaphylaxis to<br>penicillins or other beta-lactams<br>**Use with caution:**If history of allergy or renal impairment.<br>Maintain adequate hydration with high doses (risk of<br>crystalluria).<br>Reduce dose if severe renal failure.<br>**Counselling:**Swallow the capsule whole with a glass of<br>water at the start of a meal or slightly before.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea;<br>hypersensitivity reaction (discontinue if severe<br>rash, urticaria, wheezing); superinfection<br>including candidiasis<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Fever, erythema, exfoliative<br>dermatitis, angioedema, C._diff cile_colitis,<br>anaphylaxis, bronchospasm, interstitial<br>nephritis, serum sickness-like syndrome,<br>haemolytic anaemia, toxic epidermal necrolysis,<br>erythematous rashes (common in glandular<br>fever, lymphocytic leukaemia, cytomegalovirus<br>infection, Epstein-Barr virus infection, possibly<br>HIV)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet/capsule: 250 mg, 500 mg_**<br>**_Powder for oral liquid:_**<br>**_125 mg/5 ml_**<br>Oral: 500–1000 mg 3 times daily<br>for 5–7 days<br>Oral: 1 gram 3 times daily for<br>7 days<br>Oral: 500 mg every 8 hours for<br>7–10 days<br>Oral: 1 g twice daily AND<br>clarithromycin AND<br>omeprazole for 1 week<br>Oral: 500 mg to 1 g 3 times daily<br>for  5–7 days<br>AND metronidazole|
|**D rug Indication**|**Amoxicillin**<br>Non-severe pneumonia<br>(10.6.3)<br>Sinusitis (11.35)<br>Non-severe cutaneous<br>anthrax  (10.2.10)<br>Refractory gastritis/<br>PUD–eradication regimen<br>for documented_H. pylori_<br>infection (10.7a.2)<br>Dental abscess (10.17.5)<br>and peritonsillar abscess<br>(10.17.9)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Bronchitis; otitis media;<br>osteomyelitis; endocarditis<br>prophylaxis; post-<br>splenectomy prophylaxis)|

**352** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not known to be harmful.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Considered safe.<br>**Contraindications**: Known severe hypersensitivity to<br>penicillins or other beta-lactams<br>**Use with caution:**In history of mild hypersensitivity to beta-<br>lactams, renal impairment, hepatic impairment<br>**Administration:**Renal dose adjustment required (do not use<br>high doses with CrCl≤30 ml/hour).<br>875 mg orally every 12 hours can be substituted for 500 mg<br>orally every 8 hours.<br>**Counselling:**To minimize stomach upset, take at the start of<br>a meal.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Use with caution in pregnancy or<br>breastfeeding, if clinically indicated. Available safety data<br>are limited.<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment use liposomal<br>amphotericin if possible (see Section 11.5).<br>**Administration:**Follow instructions in Section 11.5.<br>Give via D5W IV infusion (incompatible with NS, 1/2 NS,<br>other saline-containing solutions, or preservatives) over 2<br>to 6 hours. Infusion time may be reduced to approximately<br>1 hour in patients who tolerate treatment well. If the patient<br>experiences discomfort during infusion, the duration of<br>infusion may be increased. Existing IV line should be f ushed<br>with D5W prior to infusion (if not feasible, administer through<br>a separate line).|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Transient increase in liver enzymes<br>and bilirubin<br>See amoxicillin.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Acute generalised<br>exanthematous pustulosis; very rarely hepatic<br>events have been reported, predominantly<br>in males and elderly patients; these may be<br>associated with prolonged treatment. In some<br>cases may not become apparent until several<br>weeks after treatment has ceased. These are<br>usually reversible.|**Common:**Acute infusion reactions (fever, chills,<br>headache, hypotension) – these become less<br>frequent over time; thrombophlebitis, anaemia,<br>nephrotoxicity (major dose-limiting toxicity);<br>hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Anaphylaxis occurs rarely<br>with any IV amphotericin product. Headache,<br>anorexia, weight loss, nausea, vomiting,<br>diarrhoea; muscle and joint pain; cardiovascular<br>toxicity (arrhythmias and cardiac arrest, due<br>to rapid infusion and electrolyte disturbances);<br>neurological disorders, abnormal liver function<br>(discontinue treatment)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet/capsule: amoxicillin_**<br>**_500 mg + clavulanic acid_**<br>**_125 mg; amoxicillin 875 mg +_**<br>**_125 mg clavulanic acid_**<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily<br>for 5 days (double in severe<br>infections)<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily for<br>3–7 days<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily or 875<br>mg every 12 hours for 14 days.<br>Oral: 1 g 3 times daily or, if<br>severe, 4 times daily<br>875 mg twice daily for 7 days|**_Infusion, intravenous: 50 mg_**<br>**_vial (dissolve 50 mg in 10 ml_**<br>**_sterile water and make up to_**<br>**_500 ml with 5% glucose to give_**<br>**_100 mcg/ml)_**<br>See Section 11.5 for dosing and<br>administration.<br>IV: 0.7 mg/kg until clinical<br>improvement (usually 14 days)<br>THEN itraconazole maintenance<br>therapy<br>See Section 11.20|
|**D rug Indication**|**Amoxicillin with clavulanic**<br>**acid**<br>**(co–amoxiclav)**<br>Doses are based on<br>amoxicillin component.<br>Septic abortion (10.15.6)<br>Lower urinary tract<br>infection (11.44)<br>Dental abscess (10.17.5);<br>peritonsillar abscess<br>(10.17.9)<br>Cholecystitis (10.7a)<br>Sinusitis (11.35)<br>(**Other indications**: Otitis<br>media; cellulitis)|**Amphotericin B**<br>**(conventional)**<br>Cryptococcosis (11.5)<br>Histoplasmosis (moderate–<br>severe) (11.16); penicilliosis<br>(moderate–severe) (11.29)<br>Visceral and cutaneous<br>leishmaniasis (11.20)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>disseminated deep fungal<br>infections)|

**353** 

|**Special groups/comments**|If available, liposomal amphotericin B should be used instead<br>of conventional amphotericin B in patients with renal failure;<br>or switch from amphotericin B if renal impairment develops.<br>Available at decreased cost through WHO-Gilead partnership<br>for leishmaniasis (see http://www.gilead.com/visceral_<br>leishmaniasis).<br>**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Use with caution in pregnancy or<br>breastfeeding, if clinically indicated. Available safety data<br>are limited.<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment; continue to monitor<br>electrolytes and renal function tests despite lower incidence<br>of adverse renal effects than with conventional amphotericin.<br>**Administration:**See conventional amphotericin B.|**Pregnancy:**Not known to be harmful.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Considered safe<br>**Contraindications:**Severe hypersensitivity to penicillins and<br>other beta-lactams<br>**Use with caution:**In mild hypersensitivity to beta-lactams<br>with renal impairment.<br>Erythematous rashes common in glandular fever, lymphocytic<br>leukaemia, Epstein-Barr virus, cytomegalovirus infection.<br>**Administration:**Injection contains 2.7 mmol (62 mg) sodium/<br>gram.<br>Reduce dose in severe renal failure.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|See conventional amphotericin B. Incidence of<br>decreased renal function and infusion-related<br>events are lower than with conventional<br>amphotericin B.<br>For a patient who experiences chills, fever,<br>hypotension, nausea, or other nonanaphylactic<br>infusion-related reactions, premedicate with<br>the following drugs 30–60 minutes prior to drug<br>administration: paracetamol OR hydrocortisone<br>50–100 mg.|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Haemolytic anaemia,<br>interstitial nephritis, blood disorders, C._diff cile_<br>colitis<br>Hypersensitivity reaction (discontinue if severe),<br>anaphylaxis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection, powder for_**<br>**_reconstitution: 50 mg (contains_**<br>**_soy, sucrose 900 mg)_**<br>See Section 11.20 for dosing.<br>See Section 11.5 for dosing.|**_Injection: 500 mg vial, 1 g vial_**<br>IM/IV: 250 mg/kg daily, divided<br>every 4 hours (range 6–12 g/<br>day) AND gentamicin for 10–14<br>days AND, for septic abortion,<br>clindamycin<br>IM/IV: 250 mg/kg daily divided<br>every 4 hours (range 6–12<br>g/day) AND gentamicin  +<br>cotrimoxazole for 10–14 days<br>IM/IV: 2 g in a single dose AND<br>gentamicin 240 mg<br>IV: 2 g every 4 hours AND<br>gentamicin + metronidazole for<br>10–14 days<br>IV: 2 g IV every 4 hours for at<br>least 21 days PLUS gentamicin<br>IV: 500 mg to 1 g every 6 hours<br>for 7 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Amphotericin B (liposomal)**<br>Visceral and cutaneous<br>leishmaniasis (11.20)<br>Cryptococcal meningitis<br>in patients with renal<br>impairment (11.5)|**Ampicillin**<br>Severe infections (septic<br>abortion (10.15.6); septic<br>shock (3.1.5); upper<br>urinary tract infection<br>(pyelonephritis) (11.44)<br>Empirical therapy for<br>meningitis, if ceftriaxone<br>not available (10.10b.3)<br>Initial empirical antibiotics<br>for emergency management<br>(QC p. 19)<br>Cholangitis (10.7a.2),<br>peritonitis (10.7a.2)<br>Listerial meningitis (10.10b.3)<br>Leptospirosis (11.22)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Mastoiditis; osteomyelitis)|

**354** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**ANTIRETROVIRALS for HIV infection   Note: antiretroviral therapy must include at least 3 ARVs.**See Sections 13 and 14.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Limited safety data available, but<br>continuation of antiretroviral therapy throughout pregnancy<br>is recommended. Potential alternate if AZT and TDF are not<br>tolerated during pregnancy.<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic disease.<br>Can lead to potential life-threatening lactic acidosis. Safe to<br>use after lactic acidosis.<br>Do not use after ABC hypersensitivity reactions.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Generally safe<br>**Use with caution:**In haemophilia: possible increased<br>bleeding episodes<br>**Administration:**Take both at same time with food|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Lactic acidosis with hepatic<br>steatosis may be more frequent in pregnant women. Should<br>be used during pregnancy only if there is NO alternative.<br>**Contraindications:**Do not use with stavudine (d4T).<br>**Use with caution:**In renal or hepatic impairment; see dose<br>adjustment in Section 11.31.|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**||**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Life-threatening<br>hypersensitivity reactions, blood disorders,<br>lipodystrophy, lactic acidosis|**Common:**Diarrhoea; indirect<br>hyperbilirubinaemia, clinical jaundice,<br>hyperglycaemia, fat maldistribution;<br>nephrolithiasis; prolonged PR interval – f rst-<br>degree symptomatic AV block in some patients|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Acute pancreatitis, lactic<br>acidosis, peripheral neuropathy, lipodistrophy,<br>hyperuricaemia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**||**_Tablet: 300 mg_**<br>**_Oral liquid: 100 mg/ml_**<br>Oral: 300 mg twice daily or<br>600 mg once daily<br>If hepatic impairment (mild), 200<br>mg twice daily (maximum)|**_Capsule 300 mg atazanavir;_**<br>**_capsule ritonavir 100 mg_**<br>Oral: 300 mg ATV and 100 mg<br>ritonavir once daily|**_Capsule: 400 mg_**<br>Oral: 250 mg once daily if <60 kg;<br>400 mg once daily if >60 kg<br>Ensure suff cient antacid<br>from buffered tablets at least<br>1 hour before food or on empty<br>stomach|
|**D rug Indication**||**Abacavir (ABC)**<br>Antiretroviral<br>(13, 14)|**Atazanavir + ritonavir**<br>**(ATV/r)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|**Didanosine (ddI)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**355** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Potential risk of teratogenicity<br>in f rst trimester. Safe after f rst trimester. Provide effective<br>contraceptives after delivery.<br>Discuss risk and benef t of using EFV with women who are<br>planning to become pregnant or who may become pregnant.<br>If a woman is diagnosed as pregnant before 28 days of<br>gestation or plans to become pregnant, EFV should be<br>stopped and NVP or a PI substituted. There is no indication<br>for termination of pregnancy in women exposed to EFV in the<br>f rst trimester.<br>Provide effective contraceptive for women of reproductive<br>age who are taking EFV and choose to avoid conception.<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment (avoid if severe),<br>severe renal impairment, elderly, history of mental illness or<br>substance abuse.<br>Rash usually resolves within 3-5 days, but discontinue ART<br>and monitor AST or ALT if rash severe or if accompanied by<br>blisters, desquamation, involvement of mucous membranes,<br>or fever; seek urgent medical care. See Section 13.<br>Seek urgent medical care also in severe CNS toxicities or if<br>depression, confusion occurs.<br>**Contraindications:**History of severe psychiatric illness<br>**Administration:**Take at bedtime on an empty stomach.<br>Suggested substitution**if not tolerated:**NVP, or bPI if neither<br>NNRTI is tolerated; or triple NRTI if no other options|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**There is limited experience with<br>use during pregnancy.<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, hepatic disease<br>**Active against HBV**(see**Section 11.14**)|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Hypersensitivity reaction;<br>Rash – generally mild, often resolves within<br>3–5 days without need to change ART (but<br>discontinue if severe)<br>Often self-limiting CNS toxicities: Insomnia,<br>abnormal dreams;<br>less commonly, persistent and severe CNS<br>toxicity (depression, confusion)<br>Hepatic toxicity: Elevated liver enzymes (if<br>seropositive for hepatitis B or C)<br>Hyperlipidaemia; male gynaecomastia; potential<br>teratogenicity (f rst trimester of pregnancy)<br>Infrequent or rare:<br>Stevens-Johnsons syndrome|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain,<br>diarrhoea, headache, peripheral neuropathy|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 100 mg, 200 mg_**<br>**_Tablet: 600 mg_**<br>**_Oral solution: 150 mg/5 ml_**<br>Oral: 600 mg once daily<br>See Section 14 for use of EFV as<br>ARV prophylaxis against MTCT<br>of HIV.|**_Capsule: 200 mg_**<br>**_Oral liquid: 10 mg/ml_**<br>Oral: 200 mg once daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Efavirenz (EFV)**<br>Antiretroviral<br>(13, 14)|**Emtricitabine (FTC)**<br>Antiretroviral (13)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**356** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Favourable safety prof le<br>**Active against HBV**(see Section 11.14)|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Avoid using in pregnant women<br>with CD4 count >350. In women with CD4 count of 250–350<br>increased risks of maternal hepatotoxicity; use, with close<br>monitoring, as benef t exceeds risk in those who require ART.<br>**Contraindications:**Severe hepatic failure, pregnancy with<br>CD4 >350<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment or history of chronic<br>hepatitis; monitor liver function.<br>**Counselling:**Advise patients about the signs or symptoms of<br>hypersensitivity reactions. Seek immediate medical attention<br>if such symptoms develop.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Continuation of antiretroviral<br>therapy throughout pregnancy is recommended; close<br>monitoring of blood glucose recommended, as risk of<br>pregnancy-related hyperglycaemia may be increased.<br>**Use with caution:**Close clinical and hepatic enzyme<br>monitoring needed in patients taking ritonavir super-<br>boosting.<br>**Administration:**Possible increased bleeding episodes in<br>patients with haemophilia<br>**Suggested substitute:**ATV/r|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Well tolerated; occasional nausea and<br>diarrhoea, pancreatitis|**Common:**Nausea and vomiting, rash (including<br>Stevens-Johnson syndrome)<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Toxic epidermal necrolysis,<br>hepatotoxicity jaundice, abdominal pain,<br>diarrhoea, hypersensitivity reactions|**Common:**GI intolerance, nausea, vomiting,<br>diarrhoea, headache<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hyperlipidaemia (especially<br>hypertriglyceridaemia), elevated transaminases,<br>hyperglycaemia, fat maldistribution, PR interval<br>prolongation, QT interval prolongation, torsade<br>de pointes, lipodystrophy|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 150 mg_**<br>**_Oral solution: 50 mg/5 ml_**<br>Oral: 150 mg twice daily OR<br>300 mg once daily<br>See Section 14 for use as ARV<br>prophylaxis against MTCT of HIV.|**_Tablet: 200 mg_**<br>Oral: 200 mg once daily for f rst<br>14 days;<br>THEN (if no rash present)<br>200 mg twice daily<br>See Section 14 for use as ARV<br>prophylaxis against MTCT of<br>HIV.|**_Tablet: Fixed dose combination_**<br>**_of (LPV 200 mg + RTV 50 mg)_**<br>**_and (LPV 100 mg + RTV 25 mg)_**<br>Oral: LPV 200 mg + RTV 50 mg<br>(2 tablets) twice daily<br>In patients taking rifampicin,<br>use ritonavir super-boosting<br>(LPV 400 mg + RTV 400 mg OR<br>LPV 800 mg + RTV 200 mg) twice<br>daily.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Lamivudine (3TC)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|**Nevirapine (NVP)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|**Lopinavir + ritonavir (LPV/r)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**357** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Continuation of antiretroviral<br>therapy throughout pregnancy is recommended; close<br>monitoring of blood glucose recommended, as risk of<br>pregnancy-related hyperglycaemia may be increased.<br>**Use with caution:**In severe hepatic impairment<br>**Administration:**Take with a fatty meal or up to 2 hours after<br>meal.<br>Avoid garlic capsules, which reduce plasma saquinavir<br>concentration)|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Concern of HBV f are if HBV–HIV<br>co-infected mother stops the medication postpartum.<br>Limited data available on potential maternal and infant bone<br>toxicity.<br>**Contraindication:**Renal impairment<br>**Use with caution:**In underlying renal disease, age >40<br>years, BMI <18.5 (or body weight <50 kg), diabetes mellitus,<br>hypertension, concomitant use of a bPI or nephrotoxic drug<br>**Administration:**Do not use with ddI (levels increased).<br>**Active against HBV**(see Section 11.14)|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Well tolerated, risk of anaemia<br>**Contraindications:**Abnormally low neutrophil counts or<br>severe anaemia (Hb <7.0 g/dl and/or ANC <750 cells/mm3);<br>high risk for anaemia and neutropaenia if CD4 count <200,<br>BMI <18.5 (or body weight <50 kg), anaemia at baseline.<br>**Use with caution:**In vitamin B12 def ciency, renal or hepatic<br>impairment, elderly patients|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Diarrhoea, headache, increased<br>transaminases, dyslipidaemia, hyperglycaemia,<br>buccal and mucosal ulceration, unconjugated<br>hyperbilirubinaemia|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain, headache, dizziness<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Renal insuff ciency, Fanconi<br>syndrome, hypertriglyceridaemia, osteomalacia.<br>Severe acute exacerbation of hepatitis<br>may occur in HBV co-infected patients who<br>discontinue TDF.|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>headache, fatigue, myalgia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Bone marrow suppression<br>(macrocytic anaemia, neutropenia);<br>pancreatitis, lactic acidosis with hepatic<br>steatosis, CNS problems, insomnia,<br>pigmentation of nails, skin, oral mucosa|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 200 mg_**<br>Oral: 1 g twice daily (with 100<br>mg ritonavir booster)|**_Tablet: 300 mg_**<br>Oral: 300 mg tablet once daily|**_Capsule: 250 mg_**<br>Oral: 250–300 mg twice daily<br>See Section 14 for use as ARV<br>prophylaxis against MTCT of<br>HIV.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Saquinavir (SQV)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|**Tenofovir (TDF)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|**Zidovudine (ZDV, AZT)**<br>Antiretroviral (13, 14)|

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**358** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Both artesunate and quinine may be considered<br>as options for the treatment of severe malaria during<br>pregnancy. See note under artesunate<br>**Use with caution:**In all patients, artemether IM should only<br>be used if parenteral formulations of artesunate or quinine<br>are not available, as its absorption may be erratic.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not recommended in f rst-<br>trimester pregnancy unless no other treatment immediately<br>available.<br>**Contraindications:**History of arrhythmias, clinically relevant<br>bradycardia, or CHF with reduced LV ejection fraction; family<br>history of sudden death or prolonged QT interval.<br>**Administration:**Lumefantrine absorption is enhanced by<br>co-administration with fat.<br>**Counselling:**Take this ACT immediately after a meal or drink<br>containing at least 1.2 g fat. Dizziness may impair ability<br>to perform skilled tasks such as operating machinery and<br>driving.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Refer to artesunate.<br>Neurotoxicity has been reported in animal<br>studies, particularly with very high doses of<br>intramuscular artemether, but has not been<br>substantiated in humans.|**Common:**Abdominal pain, anorexia, diarrhoea,<br>nausea/vomiting, palpitation, cough,<br>headache, dizziness, sleep disturbances,<br>asthenia, arthralgia, myalgia, pruritus, rash, QT<br>prolongation<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Paraesthesia, ataxia,<br>hypoaesthesia, increased liver transaminases|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 80 mg/ml in 1 ml_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>Loading dose:<br>IM (in anterior thigh): 3.2 mg/kg<br>then 1.6 mg/kg daily until patient<br>can take oral.<br>Start appropriate oral treatment<br>as soon as tolerated and give<br>full course (see Section 11.25).|**_Co-formulated tablets of_**<br>**_20 mg artemether + 120 mg_**<br>**_lumefantrine_**<br>Oral: 80 mg artemether and 480<br>mg lumefantrine twice daily for<br>3 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Artemether**<br>Severe malaria<br>(note: IV artesunate is<br>preferred)<br>(QC,  p.20 and 11.25)|**Artemether + lumefantrine**<br>Uncomplicated<br>_P. falciparum_malaria, f rst-<br>line (11.25)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**359** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Both artesunate and quinine<br>may be considered as options for the treatment of severe<br>malaria during pregnancy. Treatment must not be delayed;<br>so, if only one of the drugs artesunate, artemether, or quinine<br>is available, then that should be started immediately. Give<br>quinine if possible during f rst trimester; for second and third<br>trimesters, artesunate is preferred, as it is for all adolescents<br>and adults.<br>**Use with caution:**In patients who must perform skilled<br>tasks, such as operating machinery or driving, that would be<br>impaired by dizziness.<br>**Administration:**The solution should be used immediately<br>after the powder is dissolved. It should not be used for<br>intravenous infusion if the solution appears cloudy or if<br>sediment is present.|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended in f rst-trimester pregnancy<br>unless no other treatment immediately available<br>**Breastfeeding:**No data available on the excretion of<br>artesunate/amodiaquine f xed-dose combination in breast<br>milk. Continuation can be considered while taking into<br>account safety prof le of artesunate/amodiaquine f xed-dose<br>combination tablets.<br>**Contraindications:**Previous hypersensitivity to amodiaquine<br>or artesunate, history of liver toxicity or neutropenia during<br>treatment with amodiaquine, retinopathy (in cases of<br>frequent treatment)<br>**Use with caution:**Avoid if possible in PLHIV on zidovudine<br>or efavirenz.<br>**Counselling:**Somnolence, dizziness, or weakness may occur.<br>May impair ability to perform skilled tasks such as operating<br>machinery and driving.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|Generally well tolerated<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Mild gastrointestinal<br>disturbances, dizziness, tinnitus,<br>reticulocytopenia, neutropenia, elevated liver<br>enzyme values, ECG abnormalities (bradycardia,<br>prolongation of the QT interval),<br>Type 1 hypersensitivity reactions in<br>approximately 1 in every 3000 patients.<br>Post-treatment hemolysis has been observed<br>with high cumulative doses. (Patients should<br>be monitored for signs of hemolysis after<br>parasitologic cure.)|**Common:**Anorexia, abdominal pain, nausea;<br>somnolence, insomnia; cough.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Weakness, anaemia, vertigo.<br>Amodiaquine (at higher doses and/or during<br>prolonged treatment) may lead to leukopenia<br>and neutropaenia, agranulocytosis; nervous<br>system disorders.<br>**Rare:**Neuromyopathy, transient accommodation<br>disorders, corneal opacif cation (regresses<br>once treatment stops) but, very rarely,<br>irreversible retinopathy; hepato-biliary<br>disorders: severe, sometimes fatal hepatitis;<br>slate-gray pigmentation of the skin, notably<br>affecting the f ngers and mucous membranes.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 60 mg ampoule with_**<br>**_separate ampoule of 5% sodium_**<br>**_bicarbonate_**<br>IM/IV: 2.4 mg/kg on admission<br>AND<br>repeat at 12 hours, 24 hours;<br>then once daily. Start<br>appropriate oral treatment as<br>soon as tolerated, and give full<br>course. See Section 11.25.5.|**_Co-formulated tablets of_**<br>**_artesunate + amodiaquine,_**<br>**_25/67.5 mg, 50/135 mg, or_**<br>**_100/270 mg. Blister packs of_**<br>**_separate scored tablets also_**<br>**_exist._**<br>Oral: 200 mg artesunate and<br>540 mg amodiaquine f xed-dose<br>combination once daily for 3<br>days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Artesunate**<br>Severe malaria<br>(QC p. 20, 11.25.5)|**Artesunate + amodiaquine**<br>Uncomplicated<br>_P. falciparum_malaria,<br>f rst-line<br>(11.25)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended in the f rst trimester unless no<br>other treatment immediately available.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>U**se with caution:**Do not choose this option if patient is<br>taking cotrimoxazole prophylaxis.<br>See cotrimoxazole.|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended during the f rst trimester<br>unless no other treatment immediately available.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Contraindications:**Do not give mef oquine within 60 days of<br>prior administration.<br>History of neuropsychiatric disorders, epilepsy,<br>hypersensitivity to quinine<br>**Use with caution:**Avoid in severe hepatic impairment,<br>cardiac conduction disorders.<br>**Counselling:**May impair ability to perform skilled tasks such<br>as operating machinery and driving. These effects may<br>continue up to 3 weeks after the last dose.|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended during the f rst trimester<br>unless no other treatment available.<br>**Breastfeeding:**See clindamycin.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, anorexia, diarrhoea<br>**Infrequent or rare:**See cotrimoxazole.|**Common:**Nausea/ vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain, anorexia, headache, dizziness,<br>loss of balance, sleep disorders (abnormal<br>dreams)<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Neurological and psychiatric<br>disturbances (convulsions, depression,<br>hallucinations, panic attacks, emotional<br>instability, aggression, suicidal ideation),<br>cardiac conduction problems, muscle<br>weakness/rash, disturbances in liver function<br>tests|See artesunate and clindamycin.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Co-blistered, scored tablets of_**<br>**_50 mg artesunate and tablets_**<br>**_of 500 mg sulfadoxine + 25 mg_**<br>**_pyrimethamine_**<br>Oral: 200 mg artesunate once<br>daily for 3 days AND 1500 mg/75<br>mg sulfadoxine- pyrimethamine<br>as a single dose on day 1|**_Co-blistered, scored tablets of_**<br>**_50 mg artesunate and 250 mg_**<br>**_base of mef oquine_**<br>Oral: 200 mg artesunate once<br>daily for 3 days AND 1500 mg<br>mef oquine, usually split on days<br>2 and 3 (e.g. 4 tablets on day 2<br>and 2 on day 3)|**_See clindamycin and_**<br>**_artesunate_**<br>Oral: Artesunate 2 mg/kg once<br>daily AND clindamycin 10 mg/kg<br>twice daily for 7 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Artesunate + sulfadoxine–**<br>**pyrimethamine**<br>Uncomplicated<br>_P. falciparum_malaria, f rst-<br>line (11.25)|**Artesunate + mef oquine**<br>Uncomplicated<br>_P. falciparum_malaria, f rst-<br>line (11.25)|**Artesunate + clindamycin**<br>Uncomplicated<br>_P. falciparum_malaria, f rst-<br>line (11.25)|

**361** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used at recommended doses. Can affect<br>fetal heart rate.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use with caution in breastfeeding; monitor<br>infant (e.g. drying of secretions, temperature rise). May<br>suppress milk production.<br>**Contraindications**: Angle-closure glaucoma.<br>**Use with caution:**In Down syndrome, myasthenia gravis,<br>pyloric stenosis, ileus, prostatic enlargement, cardiac<br>disorders, hypoxia, and in the elderly. Patients with pyrexia<br>and in warm environments: Monitor temperature and keep<br>patients cool.<br>Use may precipitate acute attack of angle-closure glaucoma,<br>particularly in the elderly or long-sighted.|**Precautions:**May cause sensitivity to light and blurred<br>vision.<br>**Contraindications**: Angle-closure glaucoma<br>**Counselling:**Avoid skilled tasks, such as operating machinery<br>or driving, until your vision is clear.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Dry mouth, tachycardia, blurred vision,<br>photophobia, constipation, urinary retention,<br>f ushing, delirium, fever<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Vomiting, headache,<br>paralytic ileus, rash, acute angle-closure<br>glaucoma, seizures|**Common:**Transient stinging, raised intraocular<br>pressure, local irritation, hyperaemia, oedema,<br>contact dermatitis, systemic toxicity (in very<br>young and elderly)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 1 mg (sulfate) in 1 ml_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>IM/IV: Give bolus 1–3 mg (aim<br>for clear lungs, stable blood<br>pressure and dry mucous<br>membranes) THEN double initial<br>dose if no improvement at 5<br>minutes.<br>See Section 3.8.1 for further<br>treatment and monitoring.<br>IV: 0.5–1 mg. Repeat every<br>3–5 minutes to a total dose of<br>0.04 mg/kg<br>IV: 0.6 mg<br>IV: 25–30 mcg/kg given<br>30–60 seconds before<br>neostigmine administration|**_Solution (eye drops): 0.5%, 1%_**<br>**_(sulfate)_**<br>1 drop (0.5% or 1% solution) up<br>to 4 times daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Atropine**<br>Organophosphate poisoning<br>(3.8.1)<br>Beta-blocker overdose with<br>hypotension (3.8.1)<br>Beta-blocker overdose:<br>prophylaxis before inserting<br>NG tube (3.8.1)<br>Block muscarinic effects of<br>neostigmine<br>(3.9.2)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Bradycardia; heart block<br>with hypotension)|**Atropine eye drops**<br>Iritis, uveitis; prereferral<br>treatment for bacterial<br>corneal infection (10.12.2)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Cycloplegic refraction<br>procedures)|

**362** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, in pregnancy or<br>breastfeeding, if clinically indicated. Available data on safety<br>are lmited.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to macrolides<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, hepatic impairment;<br>in combination with other medications that can prolong QT<br>interval; myasthenia gravis<br>**Administration:**Capsule should be taken on an empty<br>stomach. Oral suspension can be taken with food.<br>**Counselling:**Swallow whole at least 1 hour before or<br>2 hours after food. Do not take with aluminium or indigestion<br>remedies containing magnesium.|**Pregnancy:**Benef t of treatment greater than risk. Not known<br>to be harmful.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Considered safe<br>**Use with caution:**If active or quiescent TB possible.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/ vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain and cramps, headache,<br>dizziness, drowsiness, candida infections, taste<br>disturbances<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Constipation, hepatitis,<br>hepatic failure, syncope, insomnia, agitation,<br>anxiety, asthenia, paraesthesia, hyperactivity,<br>thrombocytopenia, haemolytic anaemia,<br>interstitial nephritis, acute renal failure,<br>photosensitivity, tooth and tongue discoloration,<br>C._diff cile_colitis; hypersensitivity reactions<br>including anaphylaxis|**Common:**Oropharyngeal candidiasis; cough,<br>dysphonia; bruising, facial skin irritation<br>following nebulisation<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Adrenal suppression,<br>growth retardation in adolescents, impaired<br>bone metabolism, cataract, glaucoma with<br>high doses or when combined with oral<br>steroids, paradoxical bronchospasm, urticaria,<br>rash, angioedema, sleep disorders, anxiety,<br>behavioural changes|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Not known to be harmful.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Considered safe (monitor infant)<br>**Contraindications:**Severe hypersensitivity to penicillins or<br>other beta-lactams<br>**Use with caution:**With history of allergy, renal failure, heart<br>failure.<br>**Administration:**Given by deep IM injection only.<br>Give doses of more than 900 mg as two injections at separate<br>sites.<br>Do not give IV. Available data on safety<br>are limited.<br>**Contraindications:**Renal impairment; hepatic impairment<br>**Administration:**Preferably after meals|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Pain/inf ammation at injection site<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hypersensitivity reactions,<br>including anaphylaxis, haemolytic anaemia,<br>interstitial nephritis, blood disorders, CNS<br>toxicity, Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction|**Common:**Dermatitis with cutaneous eruptions<br>(rash and rash erythematous); generalized<br>oedema; fever, myalgias, arthralgias;<br>gastrointestinal disorders; depression of<br>bone marrow; polyneuropathy, paresthaesia,<br>peripheral neuropathy, among others|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: benzathine_**<br>**_benzylpenicillin 1.8 g (equal to_**<br>**_2.4 million IU) in 5 ml vial_**<br>IM: 900 mg (1.2 million IU) as a<br>single dose<br>IM: 900 mg (1.2 million IU) every<br>3–4 weeks<br>IM: 1.8 g (2.4 million IU), divided<br>between 2 sites, as a single<br>dose<br>IM: 1.8 g (2.4 million IU), divided<br>between 2 sites, once weekly<br>for 3 consecutive weeks<br>IM: 900 mg (1.2 million IU) as a<br>single dose|**_Tablet: 100 mg_**<br>Oral: 5 mg/kg daily, divided<br>in 2 or 3 daily doses, over<br>60 consecutive days (maximum<br>300 mg/day)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Benzathine benzylpenicillin**<br>Streptococcal pharyngitis<br>(10.17.9)<br>Secondary prophylaxis of<br>rheumatic fever (11.32)<br>Early syphilis (11.37)<br>Late syphilis (11.37)<br>Yaws (10.2.5)|**Benznidazole**<br>Chagas disease (11.42)|

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**364** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used in pregnancy at the recommended<br>dose, but permethrin is the preferred treatment during<br>pregnancy.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at the recommended dose if it<br>is the treatment of choice; systemic absorption likely to be<br>minimal with topical use, but excess lotion should be wiped<br>from nipple areas, and infant skin contact, minimized.<br>**Use with caution:**Do not use on inf amed or broken skin.<br>Avoid contact with eyes and mucous membranes.<br>**Administration:**See Section 10.2.3. Do not bathe before<br>application. Application to the face and genitals can<br>cause irritation; an alternative agent such as permethrin is<br>preferable.<br>**Counselling:**Remember to apply also between f ngers and<br>toes, under nails, in skin folds, navel, between the buttocks,<br>and on groin area. If you wash your hands or any other parts<br>of the body during the treatment period, you should reapply<br>the lotion to the washed areas.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Use with caution:**Avoid contact with eyes, mouth, and<br>mucous membranes; avoid use of occlusive dressings.<br>**Counselling:**Before applying, wash affected area with mild<br>soap or soap substitute and warm water. Gently pat dry. Then<br>apply a thin layer to the affected area and rub in gently. May<br>bleach fabrics, hair, and skin. Avoid excessive exposure to<br>sunlight.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Local irritation, burning<br>sensation, itch dermatitis, CNS stimulation (e.g.<br>seizures with excessive use)|**Common:**Initial irritation: skin dryness or<br>peeling, feeling of warmth, mild stinging,<br>erythema, but subsiding with continued use (in<br>some cases may need to reduce frequency of<br>application or temporarily suspend use)<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Contact sensitivity<br>(occasionally, even one application can cause<br>severe irritation).|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Lotion: 25%_**<br>Topical: Follow instructions in<br>10.2.3.<br>Apply to affected area THEN<br>wash off 24 hours later (further<br>applications may be needed<br>after 7 and 14 days).|**_Cream or lotion: 2.5%, 5%_**<br>Initially apply directly to<br>clean skin on alternate days,<br>increasing frequency to<br>1–2 times daily as tolerance<br>to irritant effect develops.<br>Continue until 2 weeks after<br>lesions disappear.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Benzyl benzoate**<br>Scabies (10.2.3); empirical<br>treatment of itching papular<br>lesions (10.2.3)<br>Pediculosis (10.2.8)|**Benzoyl peroxide**<br>Acne (10.2.3)|

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**365** 

|**Special groups/comments**|See benzathine benzylpenicillin.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**May be used in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding; systemic absorption likely to be minimal with<br>topical use.<br>**Contraindications:**Untreated skin infections, broken skin,<br>rosacea, acne, perioral dermatitis<br>**Use with caution:**In psoriasis (may precipitate severe<br>pustular psoriasis on withdrawal; avoid in widespread plaque<br>psoriasis).<br>If used on a large area of the body or for a long time, risk for<br>adrenal suppression (particularly with an occlusive dressing).<br>Avoid use on the face for more than 7 days. Secondary<br>infection requires treatment with an appropriate antimicrobial.<br>**Counselling**: Apply a thin layer by smoothing gently into skin,<br>preferably after bathing.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|See benzathine benzylpenicillin.|**Common:**Folliculitis, steroid rosacea, perioral<br>dermatitis, atropy-thinning of the skin, striae,<br>depigmenatation, dilated vessels, acne at site<br>of application, exacerbation of local infection<br>or worsening of the condition; local trophic<br>changes (particularly on the face and in skin<br>folds)<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Contact dermatitis,<br>hyperaesthesia, subcutaneous tissue atrophy,<br>hypertrichosis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Intravenous: 600 mg (1 million_**<br>**_IU); 3 g (5 million IU) (sodium or_**<br>**_potassium salt)  in vial_**<br>IV: 7.2–10.8 g (12–18 million IU)<br>daily in 4 or 6 equally divided<br>doses for 4 weeks<br>IV: 1.8–2.4 g (3–4 million IU)<br>every 4 hours for 2 weeks<br>IV: 2.4–3.6 g (4–6 million IU)<br>every 6 hours for 7–10 days<br>IV: 900 mg (1.5 million IU) every<br>6 hours for 7 days|**_Cream, ointment: 0.1%_**<br>Apply sparingly 1 or 2 times<br>daily.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Benzylpenicillin (penicillin**<br>**G)**<br>Streptococcal<br>endocarditis (11.10)<br>Neurosyphilis (11.37)<br>Severe anthrax<br>(10.2.10, 10.6.2)<br>Leptospirosis (11.22)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Otitis media; gas<br>gangrene; actinomycosis;<br>osteomyelitis; brain<br>abscess)|**Betametasone (topical)**<br>Severe inf ammatory skin<br>conditions including papular<br>lesions; eosinophilic<br>folliculitis; pityrosporum<br>folliculitis; papular urticaria;<br>eczema; contact, atopic<br>dermatitis; numular eczema;<br>seborrhoeic dermatitis;<br>psoriasis; itchy papular<br>lesions (10.2)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Lichen planus)|

**366** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Appears to be safe|**Pregnancy**: Previously commenced therapy can be<br>maintained.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution in breastfeeding; poor oral<br>bioavailability, but monitor infant for opiate side-effects.<br>**Use with caution:**Must not be given while the person<br>has any signs of opioid toxicity due to risk of precipitating<br>withdrawal syndrome. Buprenorphine has both opioid<br>agonist and antagonist properties and may precipitate<br>withdrawal symptoms in people with high degree of<br>tolerance to opioids, including those prescribed opioids for<br>pain.<br>When injected, buprenorphine has a similar abuse potential<br>to injected heroin and may lead to dependence.<br>Unlike most opioid analgesics, the effects of buprenorphine<br>are only partially reversed by naloxone.<br>**Counselling:**Place the tablet under the tongue and keep in<br>place until dissolved. Do not chew or swallow the tablet.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, dry mouth,<br>constipation, dyspepsia; blurred vision,<br>mydriasis, dry eyes; urinary retention,<br>tachycardia; sedation, confusion, memory<br>disturbance especially in elderly<br>**Infrequent or rare:**arrhythmia, dizziness,<br>drowsiness, headache, hallucinations, fever,<br>anaphylaxis, acute angle-closure glaucoma,<br>myasthenia gravis, gastrointestinal obstruction|**_Opioid agonist effects_**<br>**Common:**Euphoria; constipation, anorexia,<br>nausea, vomiting, sweating, headache,<br>dizziness, vasodilatation; dry mouth, fatigue,<br>sedation, anxiety. postural hypotension, miosis,<br>decreased libido<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hallucinations, confusion,<br>spasm of urinary or biliary tract, hypotension,<br>vertigo, bradycardia, tachycardia, palpitations,<br>hypothermia, rash, facial f ushing, urticaria<br>**_Opioid antagonist effects_**(which can occur if<br>used soon after a full opioid agonist such as<br>methadone) mimic those of naltrexone in opioid<br>dependence.<br>**Common:**Vomiting, nausea, diarrhoea, anxiety,<br>agitation, restlessness, insomnia, muscle<br>aches, sweating, dilated pupils, tachycardia,<br>hypertension.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Delirium, involuntary<br>ejaculation|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 5 mg IM/IV_**<br>**_Tablet: 2 mg_**<br>IM: 5 mg (give IV if condition<br>life-threatening); maximum 20<br>mg in 24 hours<br>Oral/IV: Start with 1 mg twice<br>daily.<br>Increase to 2 mg 3 times daily to<br>a target dose of<br>3–12 mg daily.|**_Tablet given sublingually:_**<br>**_2 mg, 8 mg_**<br>SL: 2–16 mg/day for 3–14 days<br>SL: Start at 2–8 mg and increase<br>by up to 8 mg daily as needed up<br>to a maximum of 32 mg. Average<br>dose is usually 12–16 mg daily.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Biperiden**<br>Acute dystonic reaction<br>(QC p. 29)<br>Antipsychotics causing<br>extrapyramidal side-effects<br>such as parkinsonism or<br>dystonia (10.11.4)|**Buprenorphine**<br>Opioid withdrawal (3.6.2)<br>Opioid substitution<br>treatment (17.4)|

**367** 

|**Special groups/comments**||**Pregnancy:**Use only when indicated (no controlled trials or<br>animal studies).<br>**Breastfeeding:**Calcium in breastmilk is normal nutritional<br>component.<br>**Contraindications:**Conditions associated with<br>hypercalcaemia and hypercalciuria (for example, some forms<br>of malignant disease)<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, sarcoidosis, history of<br>nephrolithiasis<br>**Administration:**Monitor ECG (and plasma calcium if feasible)<br>with IV administration.<br>Do not give by subcutaneous or IM route, as it will cause<br>tissue necrosis.<br>For infusion, dilute 100 ml of calcium gluconate 10% in 1 litre<br>of glucose 5% or sodium chloride 0.9%, and give at an initial<br>rate of 50 ml/hour, adjusted according to response.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**||**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, constipation;<br>injection site reactions; fall in blood pressure<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Bradycardia/arrhythmia,<br>peripheral vasodilation, renal calculi, severe<br>tissue damage with extravasation|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Lotion: calamine 15%, zinc_**<br>**_oxide 5%, bentonite 3%, sodium_**<br>**_citrate 0.5%, liquid phenol 0.5%,_**<br>**_glycerine 5% + water to 100 ml_**|**_IV: 100 mg/ml in 10 ml ampoule_**<br>**_(10%)_**<br>IV: 0.6 ml/kg (600 mg/kg) to<br>a maximum of 30 ml over 5<br>minutes; can be repeated every<br>10–20 minutes, up to 4 doses<br>IV: 1 g (10 ml of 10% solution)<br>over 10 minutes<br>IV: 10 ml of calcium gluconate<br>10% slowly, given over 2–5<br>minutes; may repeat after<br>5 minutes titrated and adjusted<br>to ECG improvement|
|**D rug Indication**|**Calamine lotion**<br>Mild pruritus; mild drug<br>reactions (10.2)|**Calcium gluconate**<br>Hypotension with calcium<br>channel blocker overdose<br>(3.8.1)<br>Antidote for magnesium<br>sulfate toxicity (QC p. 28)<br>Hyperkalemia with ECG<br>changes or K >6.5 mmol/l<br>(5.2.2)<br>(**Other indication:**<br>Hypocalcaemic tetany)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**368** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Avoid in pregnancy. May be used<br>in breastfeeding (monitor infant for drowsiness).<br>**Contraindications:**Atrioventricular conduction abnormalities,<br>history of bone marrow depression, porphyria; history of<br>sensitivity to tricyclic antidepressants, oxcarbazepine, or<br>other structurally related drugs; use of MAO-Is within past<br>2 weeks; on antiretrovirals for HIV (lowers levels)<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment, renal impairment,<br>cardiac disease, skin reactions, history of blood disorders,<br>glaucoma, elderly.<br>Monitor blood counts before and during treatment.<br>Avoid sudden withdrawal.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions. Monitor for drug<br>interactions when starting any new medication.<br>**Counselling:**Take with food to help prevent stomach upset.<br>This medicine may cause drowsiness, dizziness, or blurred<br>vision, especially at the start of treatment or when the dose<br>is increased. If affected, do not drive or operate machinery.<br>May increase the effects of alcohol.<br>Tell your health care provider that you are taking<br>carbamazepine before starting any new medicine including<br>herbal and OTC products.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Contraindications:**Cephalosporin hypersensitivity<br>**Use with caution:**In sensitivities to beta-lactam<br>antibacterials (avoid if history of immediate hypersensitivity<br>reaction); renal impairment<br>**Note:**Can cause false positive urinary glucose (if tested for<br>reducing substances) and false positive Coombs’ test.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Drowsiness, dizziness, ataxia,<br>headache, diplopia (may be associated with<br>high plasma levels); dry mouth; mild transient<br>generalized erythematous rash (withdraw if<br>worsens or other symptoms); diarrhoea or<br>constipation; leukopenia, thrombocytopenia;<br>increased liver enzymes (usually not clinically<br>signif cant)<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Antibody def ciency,<br>exfoliative dermatitis, Stevens-Johnson<br>syndrome, systemic lupus erythematosus,<br>agranulocytosis; aplastic anaemia, multiorgan<br>hypersensitivity syndrome (including fever,<br>severe skin disease, lymphadenopathy,<br>haematologic abnormalities, hepatitis);<br>psychiatric disorders, or facial dyskinesia,<br>jaundice/hepatitis, acute renal failure,<br>cardiovascular problems (arrhythmias, heart<br>block heart failure), neuropsychiatric problems,<br>impotence/male infertility, photosensitivity,<br>pulmonary hypersensitivity, confusion/agitation<br>(elderly), SIADH|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal discomfort; headache<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Allergic reactions<br>(including anaphylaxis), erythema multiforme;<br>transient hepatitis, jaundice; leukopenia,<br>thrombocytopenia, agranulocytosis, aplastic<br>anaemia, haemolytic anaemia; interstitial<br>nephritis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Oral liquid: 100 mg/5 ml_**<br>**_Tablet (chewable): 100 mg,_**<br>**_200 mg_**<br>**_Tablet (scored): 100 mg, 200 mg_**<br>Oral: initially 100–200 mg<br>1–2 times daily (tablets) OR<br>50–100 mg every 6 hours<br>as oral suspension THEN<br>Increase gradually by ~200 mg<br>every week as needed (usual<br>maintenance dose is 400–1400<br>mg daily in divided doses)<br>Oral: initially 200 mg daily<br>at bedtime THEN gradually<br>increase to 400–600 mg daily<br>(divided doses); in severe cases<br>may need 1000 mg<br>(In elderly or medically ill:<br>see Section 10.11.5)<br>100 mg once or twice daily, THE<br>increase to 200 mg 3 or 4 times<br>daily (up to 1.6 g daily in some<br>patients)|**_Capsule: 400 mg_**<br>Oral: 400 mg as a single dose<br>Oral: 400 mg twice daily to<br>complete total 7–10 days, after<br>ceftriaxone for 3 days.<br>Oral: 400 mg twice daily to<br>complete 14–21 days, after<br>ceftriaxone for 3 days.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Carbamazepine**<br>Generalized tonic-clonic<br>seizures, partial seizures<br>(3.5, 10.10c)<br>Bipolar disorders<br>(10.11.5)<br>Peripheral neuropathy<br>(10.10a.6); neuropathic pain<br>(20.3)|**Cef xime**<br>Gonorrhoea, uncomplicated<br>(10.15.4)<br>Gonococcal dermatitis-<br>arthritis syndrome (10.13.2)<br>Gonococcal septic arthritis<br>(10.13.2)|

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**369** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for side-effects)<br>**Contraindications:**Cephalosporin hypersensitivity<br>(anaphylaxis, hives), porphyria, hypoalbuminaemia or<br>impaired bilirubin binding<br>**Use with caution:**In history of allergy to beta-lactams (minor<br>rash), pre-existing gallbladder disease<br>**Administration:**Incompatible with calcium; do not give via<br>calcium-containing solutions.<br>Divide IM dose over 1 g between 2 sites.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Diarrhoea, nausea; rash, electrolyte<br>disturbances, pain and inf ammation at injection<br>site<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Antibiotic-associated colitis<br>(particularly with higher doses), hypersensitivity<br>reactions (including anaphylaxis), erythema<br>multiforme, transient hepatitis/jaundice, blood<br>disorders (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia,<br>agranulocytosis, aplastic anaemia, haemolytic<br>anaemia), interstitial nephritis, pancreatitis,<br>cholecystitis, pseudolithiasis (dose-dependent,<br>asymptomatic, and reversible biliary sludge<br>formation due to calcium–ceftriaxone complex,<br>which usually resolves after treatment<br>stopped), nephrolithiasis (formation of calcium–<br>ceftriaxone renal stones, sometimes requiring<br>treatment, usually reversible) pain, tenderness<br>at IM injection site (can reconstitute with<br>1% lidocaine for patient comfort)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 250 mg, 1 g (as_**<br>**_sodium salt) in vial_**<br>IV: 1 g daily for 7–14 days for<br>severe infection<br>IV: 2 g daily for 4 weeks<br>IV: 2 g twice daily for 5–14 days<br>IV: 1 g daily AND metronidazole<br>for 10–14 days<br>IV: 2 g daily for 5–10 days<br>IM: 250 mg as a single dose<br>IM: 250 mg as a single dose<br>IV: 1 g daily, continuing 1–2<br>days after improvement; THEN<br>cef xime to complete 14–21 days<br>treatment<br>IM: 1 g as single dose pre-<br>referral|
|**D rug Indication**|**Ceftriaxone**<br>First-line empirical antibiotic<br>coverage for emergency<br>management (QC p. 19);<br>septic shock (3.1.5); severe<br>pneumonia (3.2.3); acute<br>pyelonephritis (11.44); septic<br>abortion (10.15.6)<br>Endocarditis - viridans<br>streptococci (11.10)<br>Meningitis (10.10b.3)<br>Cholangitis (10.7a.2)<br>Spontaneous bacterial<br>peritonitis (10.9.2)<br>Gonorrhoea, uncomplicated<br>(10.15.4, 11.13)<br>Prophylaxis of contacts of<br>meningococcal meningitis<br>(10.10b.2)<br>Gonorrhoea, arthritis<br>(10.13.5)<br>Gonorrhoea, conjunctivitis<br>(10.12.2)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding<br>**Contraindications:**Poisoning by hydrocarbons, with high<br>potential for harm if aspirated; poisoning by corrosive<br>substances – may prevent visualization of lesions caused<br>by poison<br>**Use with caution:**In drowsy or unconscious patients – risk<br>of aspiration (intubate via nasogastric or gastric tube before<br>administration)<br>Not effective for poisoning with alkalis, acids, heavy metals,<br>iron, lithium, toxic alcohols, glycols, or hydrocarbons such<br>as kerosene<br>**Administration:**Improve palatability by chilling. It may be<br>easier for some patients to take it in a covered container with<br>a large straw or with eyes shut.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, constipation, black<br>stools, colicky abdominal pain<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Diarrhoea, aspiration<br>pneumonitis, dehydration and electrolyte<br>imbalances, gastrointestinal obstruction/faecal<br>impaction in dehydrated patients|

**372** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use with caution in pregnancy; risk of neonatal<br>“grey syndrome” with high doses close to term.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Not recommended with systemic use<br>**Use with caution:**In porphyria reduce high doses as soon as<br>clinically indicated; avoid repeated courses and prolonged<br>use.<br>Reduce dose in hepatic and severe renal impairment.<br>Blood counts required before and during treatment.<br>Plasma concentration monitoring required in the elderly<br>and in hepatic or renal impairment; renal and hepatic dose<br>adjustment required.<br>**Counselling:**Tell your doctor if you get pale skin, sore throat,<br>fever, tiredness or weakness, or unusual bleeding or bruising<br>in the months after you stop taking the medicine.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, headache,<br>reversible bone marrow suppression<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Diarrhoea, stomatitis/<br>glossitis, depression, hypersensitivity reactions<br>(including anaphylaxis), aplastic anaemia,<br>peripheral/optic neuritis, minor disulf ram-like<br>reactions, grey baby syndrome (premature and<br>newborn infants), C._diff cile_colitis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 250 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 1 g (sodium_**<br>**_succinate) vial_**<br>**_Oily injection: 500 mg/ml, 2 ml_**<br>**_ampoule (for IM use only)_**<br>IV: 1 gram every 6 hours<br>IV: 1 gram every 6 hours AND<br>cotrimoxazole<br>IM (oily): 100 mg/kg (maximum<br>3 g) as a single dose; repeat<br>after 24–48 hours if necessary<br>Oral: 2 to 3 g in 4 divided doses<br>for 14 days<br>Oral: 500 mg 4 times daily for<br>5–7 days<br>IV: 1 gram every 6 hours|
|**D rug Indication**|**Chloramphenicol**<br>Severe infections such<br>as sensitive H. inf uenzae<br>meningitis (10.10b.3);<br>H. inf uenzae epiglottitis<br>(3.2.2)<br>Empirical treatment of<br>bacterial meningitis if<br>anaphylaxis to penicillin<br>(10.10b.3)<br>Epidemics of<br>meningococcal meningitis<br>(10.10b)<br>Typhoid fever if known<br>antibiotic sensitivity (11.43)<br>Rickettsial diseases in<br>pregnant women (11.33)<br>Tetanus (11.39)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Cerebral abscess;<br>mastoiditis; relapsing<br>fever; plague; psittacosis;<br>tularemia; Whipple disease)|

**373** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Contraindications:**Chloramphenicol hypersensitivity<br>**Maximum duration of use:**7 days; prolonged use may lead to<br>overgrowth of non-susceptible organisms|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**May be used in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding. Systemic absorption likely to be minimal with<br>topical use.<br>**Use with caution:**Not for use in body cavities. Avoid contact<br>with middle ear, eyes, brain and meninges.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Transient stinging<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Unpleasant taste,<br>hypersensitivity reactions (local allergy,<br>angioedema, anaphylaxis), dermatitis.<br>(Large, population-based studies have found no<br>association between use of chloramphenicol<br>eye drops and aplastic anaemia.)|**Infrequent or rare:**Skin irritation/local contact<br>dermatitis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**Drops: 0.5%**<br>**Ointment: 1%**<br>Eye drops:<br>1 or 2 drops, every 2 hours<br>for the f rst 24 hours; THEN<br>decrease to every 6 hours until<br>48 hours after resolution<br>Ointment: May be used at<br>bedtime (if eye drops used<br>during day) or 3–4 times daily (if<br>eye ointment used alone).|**Solution: concentrate for**<br>**solution, 5%**<br>0.05% aqueous solution applied<br>to affected areas|
|**D rug Indication**|**Chloramphenicol – eye**<br>**drops and ointment**<br>Superf cial bacterial<br>infections of the eye;<br>prophylaxis against<br>bacterial infection following<br>minor ocular trauma<br>(10.12.2)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>blepharitis)|**Chlorhexidine**<br>Multiple skin infections,<br>itching (10.2.8)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Disinfection of clean<br>instruments; pre-operative<br>skin disinfection and hand<br>washing)|
