Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**374** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Benef t exceeds risk when indicated for<br>treatment of acute malaria.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use with caution.<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic and renal impairment,<br>neurological disorders (avoid for prophylaxis if epilepsy<br>history), severe gastrointestinal disorders, G6PD def ciency,<br>pre-existing auditory damage, elderly.<br>May exacerbate psoriasis or myasthenia gravis.<br>If patient continues to deteriorate after chloroquine,<br>administer quinine intravenously (suspect resistance).<br>**Counselling:**Take with food to minimize nausea and vomiting.<br>If part or all of a dose is vomited, the same amount should be<br>taken again immediately.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended<br>doses in pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for<br>drowsiness).<br>**Use with caution:**In the elderly; may cause a paradoxical<br>stimulation. Also, use with caution in prostate enlargement,<br>urinary retention, ileus or pyloroduodenal obstruction,<br>glaucoma, renal impairment, hepatic impairment, epilepsy.<br>**Counselling:**Drowsiness may impair ability to perform skilled<br>tasks such as operating machinery or driving. Drowsiness<br>may diminish after a few days of treatment.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain or cramps; headache; rash,<br>pruritus<br>Infrequent or rare (with prolonged treatment):<br>Psychotic episodes, anxiety, personality<br>changes, visual disturbances (dose-<br>related retinopathy), hair loss, blue-black<br>pigmentation of mucous membranes and skin,<br>photosensitivity, tinnitus, hearing loss, bone<br>marrow suppression, hypersensitivity reactions,<br>atrioventricular block, porphyria, exacerbation<br>of psoriasis, neuromyopathy|**Common:**Drowsiness, headache, psychomotor<br>impairment, antimuscarinic effects such as<br>urinary retention, dry mouth, blurred vision, and<br>gastro-intestinal disturbances<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hypotension, palpitations,<br>arrhythmias, extrapyramidal effects, dizziness,<br>confusion, depression, sleep disturbances,<br>tremor, convulsions, hypersensitivity reactions<br>(including bronchospasm, angioedema, and<br>anaphylaxis, rashes, and photosensitivity<br>reactions), blood disorders, liver dysfunction,<br>angle-closure glaucoma.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**Tablet: chloroquine base**<br>**(as phosphate or sulfate)**<br>**100 mg, 150 mg**<br>Oral: Initially 10 mg/kg (600 mg)<br>as a single dose THEN 5 mg/kg<br>(300 mg) 6–8 hours later THEN<br>5 mg/kg (300 mg) daily on next<br>2 days (24 and 48 hours after<br>initial dose) THEN full anti-<br>relapse treatment of 14 days<br>of primaquine in P. vivax and P.<br>ovale infections, except during<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding|**Tablet: 4 mg**<br>**Injection: 10 mg/ml, 1 ml**<br>**ampoule; (if necessary,**<br>**injection solution can be**<br>**diluted with sodium chloride**<br>**0.9% injection)**<br>Oral: 4 mg every 4–6 hours<br>(maximum 24 mg daily)<br>SC/IM/IV: 10–20 mg (maximum<br>40 mg in 24 hours); give IV over<br>1 minute|
|**D rug Indication**|**Chloroquine**<br>Malaria caused by<br>_P. malariae, P. vivax_, or<br>_P. ovale_(11.25.3)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Malaria prophylaxis;<br>rheumatoid arthritis)|**Chlorphenamine**<br>(chlorpheniramine)<br>Itching (10.2.8)<br>Allergic reactions,<br>anaphylaxis (adjunct) (3.1.3)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**375** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use, with caution, in pregnancy, if benef t is<br>greater than risk.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use caution (monitor infant for drowsiness).<br>**Contraindications:**CNS depression/impaired consciousness;<br>bone marrow depression; phaeochromocytoma, porphyria,<br>basal ganglia disease, parkinsonism<br>**Use with caution:**In cardiovascular and cerebrovascular<br>disorders, dementia, respiratory disease, epilepsy, acute<br>infections, renal and hepatic impairment (avoid if severe),<br>history of jaundice, leukopenia (blood count required if<br>unexplained fever or Infection), hypothyroidism, myasthenia<br>gravis, prostatic hypertrophy, angle-closure glaucoma,<br>organophosphate poisoning; subarachnoid haemorrhage,<br>metabolic disturbances (hypokalaemia, hypocalcaemia,<br>hypomagnesaemia), seizure disorders; in patients on other<br>QT-prolonging medications<br>**Elderly/debilitated (including HIV stage 3 or 4):**Haloperidol<br>preferred; alternative is chlorpromazine at one-third to half<br>adult dose.<br>Avoid abrupt withdrawal.<br>Check blood counts if unexplained fever or infection.<br>**Administration:**Avoid skin contact with injection solution or<br>oral liquid, as there is a risk of contact dermatitis.<br>Oral hygiene is very important with regular use of oral liquid.<br>**Counselling:**Warn patients that this medication may impair<br>ability to perform skilled tasks such as operating machinery<br>or driving. If taking liquid form, daily oral hygiene is very<br>important.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**_Extrapyramidal side-effects:_Acute<br>dystonic reaction or severe muscle spasm,<br>stiffness, tremor, motor restlessness, agitation,<br>Parkinson’s syndrome; with prolonged<br>administration, potentially irreversible<br>involuntary movements (tardive dyskinesia)<br>_Autonomic side-effects:_Drowsiness; orthostatic<br>hypotension, dizziness; tachycardia; dry mouth;<br>blurred vision; constipation; urinary retention<br>_Other:_Nausea, anorexia, dyspepsia; headache;<br>apathy, confusion, depression, nightmares,<br>insomnia; weight gain; photosensitivity;<br>rash; galactorrhoea, gynaecomastia, sexual<br>dysfunction, impotence, menstrual irregularities;<br>jaundice, altered liver enzymes<br>**Infrequent or rare, serious:**Neuroleptic<br>malignant syndrome; hyperthermia,<br>hyperpyrexia, heat stroke; blood disorders,<br>including leukopenia, thrombocytopenia,<br>aplastic anaemia; lupus erythematosus-like<br>syndrome; exfoliative dermatitis; seizures;<br>cholestatic jaundice; arrhythmias; respiratory<br>depression at high doses<br>**Other infrequent or rare:**ECG changes;<br>hypothermia; corneal and lens opacities;<br>purplish pigmentation of the skin, cornea and<br>retina (with prolonged high doses)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 100 mg_**<br>**_Oral liquid: 25 mg/5 ml_**<br>**_Injection: 25 mg/ml, 2 ml_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>Oral: Initially 75 mg at night<br>Typical effective dose is<br>75–300 mg daily, but up to 1000<br>mg may be necessary in severe<br>cases.<br>Oral/IM/IV: 25–50 mg 4 times<br>daily until vomiting stops<br>IM: 50–150 mg every 4–8 hours|
|**D rug Indication**|**Chlorpromazine**<br>Psychosis (including<br>schizophrenia) (10.11.4)<br>Vomiting<br>(10.7c.3, 14.1.11)<br>Tetanus spasms (11.39)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**376** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Contraindicated in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding due to theoretical risk of injury to developing<br>cartilage; consider alternatives where possible; use only if no<br>available alternatives and benef t is greater than risk.<br>**Contraindications:**History of quinolone-associated tendon<br>disorder<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly patients and those on<br>corticosteroids (higher risk of tendonitis/rupture); in history<br>of epilepsy or seizure, renal impairment, G6PD def ciency,<br>myasthenia gravis (risk of exacerbation).<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions.<br>If TB infection is suspected, limit use if there are alternate<br>antibiotics available.<br>**Administration:**Give IV infusion over at least 60 minutes.<br>**Counselling:**Oral: Take 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals.<br>Drink plenty of f uids while taking it.<br>Dairy products; antacids; and iron, zinc, or calcium<br>supplements may reduce absorption; do not take within<br>2 hours of a ciprof oxacin dose.<br>This medication may impair ability to perform skilled tasks<br>such as operating machinery or driving. Avoid extended<br>exposure to sunlight (discontinue if photosensitivity occurs;<br>report to clinician).|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>dyspepsia, abdominal pain<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hepatitis, jaundice,<br>pancreatitis; dizziness, sleep disorders;<br>convulsions, paraesthesia, movement disorders<br>(discontinue use); hypersensitivity reactions<br>(if severe rash, discontinue), petechiae,<br>haemorrhagic bullae, erythema nodosum;<br>photosensitivity reaction; psychiatric symptoms<br>(depression, confusion, hallucinations –<br>discontinue if occurs); haemolytic anaemia;<br>C. diff cile colitis.<br>Tendon damage (including rupture) has<br>been reported rarely in patients receiving<br>quinolones. Tendon rupture may occur within<br>48 hours of starting treatment; cases have also<br>been reported several months after stopping<br>a quinolone. If tendinitis is suspected, the<br>quinolone should be discontinued immediately.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**Tablet: 250 mg**<br>**Solution for IV infusion: 2 mg/ml**<br>Oral: 500 mg twice daily OR IV:<br>400 mg twice daily for 10–14<br>days<br>Oral: 500 mg  twice daily for<br>5–7 days AND metronidazole<br>IV: 400 mg twice daily AND<br>metronidazole for 10-14 days<br>Oral: 500 mg twice daily for 7<br>days<br>Prophylaxis:<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times a week<br>See table in Section 10.15.5.<br>Oral: 500 mg as a single dose<br>Oral: 500 mg twice daily for<br>7-10 days<br>IV: 400 mg twice daily for 7-10<br>days<br>Oral: 500 mg as a single dose<br>Oral: 1 g as a single dose|
|**D rug Indication**|**Ciprof oxacin**<br>Severe infections: acute<br>pyelonephritis (11.44);<br>typhoid fever (11.43)<br>Cholecystitis (10.7a.2)<br>Cholangitis (10.7a.2)<br>Isosporiasis with<br>intolerance to<br>cotrimoxazole or<br>resistance to ampicillin and<br>cotrimoxazole) (11.18)<br>PID (10.15.5)<br>Uncomplicated gonorrhoea<br>if susceptible (10.15.4);<br>gonococcal conjunctivitis<br>(10.12.2)<br>Anthrax, cutaneous<br>(10.2.10)<br>Anthrax, severe (10.2.10)<br>Prophylaxis, meningococcal<br>meningitis in non-epidemic<br>situations (10.10b.3)<br>Cholera (10.7d.2)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use alternative macrolides in pregnancy where<br>possible.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution in breastfeeding (monitor infant for<br>side-effects)<br>**Contraindications:**Sensitivity to macrolide antibiotics.<br>Known interactions with many drugs.<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment<br>**Counselling:**Before starting or stopping any other medicines,<br>tell your doctor that you are taking this medication.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Dyspepsia.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Tooth and tongue<br>discoloration; smell and taste disturbances,<br>stomatitis, glossitis; headache, arthralgia,<br>myalgia, hepatitis, pancreatitis; tinnitus,<br>dizziness, insomnia, nightmares, anxiety,<br>confusion, psychosis, paraesthesia; convulsions;<br>hypoglycaemia; interstitial nephritis, renal failure;<br>leukopenia, thrombocytopenia; pulmonary<br>inf ltration with eosinophilia; prolonged QT<br>interval, torsade de pointes; on IV infusion, local<br>tenderness, phlebitis.<br>See also erythromycin adverse effects.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 250 mg, 500 mg_**<br>**_Oral suspension: 125 mg/5 ml,_**<br>**_250 mg/5 ml_**<br>Oral: 500 mg twice daily AND<br>ethambutol 15 mg/kg daily for<br>6 months<br>Oral: 500 mg twice daily AND<br>amoxicillin + omeprazole for<br>7 days<br>Oral: 7.5 mg/kg twice daily (not<br>to exceed 500 mg twice daily)<br>AND rifampicin for 8 weeks|
|**D rug Indication**|**Clarithromycin**<br>MAC treatment in PLHIV<br>(11.27)<br>Helicobacter pylori infection<br>eradication<br>(10.7a.2)<br>Buruli ulcer (10.2.10)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Not known to be harmful<br>**Breastfeeding:**Unknown safety; amount in milk probably too<br>small to be harmful.<br>Discontinue immediately if diarrhoea or colitis develops.<br>**Contraindications:**Diarrhoeal states, porphyria<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment.<br>Monitor liver function during prolonged therapy.<br>**Administration:**Dilute in glucose 5% or normal saline to<br>concentration not more than 12 mg/ml and infuse slowly (not<br>more than 30 mg/minute) to reduce risk of adverse cardiac<br>effects. Single doses over 600 mg by intravenous infusion<br>only and should not exceed 1.2 g over 1 hour.<br>**Counselling:**Take with a full glass of water. Stop taking this<br>medication and tell your doctor immediately if you develop<br>diarrhoea.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Counselling:**Noticeable improvement usually seen in<br>6 weeks; however, 8–12 weeks of treatment may be required<br>before maximum benef t is seen. Before applying, wash<br>affected area with a mild soap and warm water, rinse<br>thoroughly, and pat dry. Avoid contact with eyes, lips, and<br>inside of your nose or mouth.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain or cramps; rash, contact<br>dermatitis<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Unpleasant taste in the<br>mouth; oesophagitis; altered liver enzymes,<br>jaundice; blood disorders (leukopenia,<br>granulocytosis, eosinophilia, thrombocytopenia);<br>pain/induration/abscess after intramuscular<br>injection, thrombophlebitis after intravenous<br>injection, erythema multiforme, polyarthritis; C.<br>_diff cile_colitis (see Section 10.7d)|**Common:**Dry, scaly, or peeling skin<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Contact dermatitis, irritation,<br>burning sensation, itch|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 150 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 150 mg/ml, 2 ml_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>Oral: 300-450 mg every 8 hours<br>(maximum 450 mg every 6 hours)<br>IM/IV: 900 mg  every 8 hours<br>AND ampicillin or cloxacillin<br>IV: 900 mg every 8 hours<br>AND ampicillin/penicillin +<br>gentamicin for 14 days<br>See table in Sectoin 10.15.5.<br>Oral: 600 mg twice daily AND<br>quinine (600 mg every 8 hours)<br>for 7 days|**_Gel/lotion: clindamycin 1% (as_**<br>**_phosphate)_**<br>Gel: Apply to lesions once daily.<br>Lotion: Apply to the lesions<br>twice daily. Continue until 2<br>weeks after lesions disappear.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Clindamycin**<br>Cellulitis (10.2.2)<br>Complicated soft tissue<br>infection including<br>necrotizing fasciitis (10.2.2)<br>Septic abortion (10.15.6)<br>PID (10.15.5)<br>Uncomplicated<br>_P. falciparum_malaria in f rst<br>trimester pregnant woman<br>(11.25)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Staphylococcal bone and<br>joint infections; endocarditis<br>prophylaxis)|**Clindamycin, topical**<br>Moderate acne (10.2.3)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**No information<br>**Breastfeeding:**May cause reversible skin discoloration in<br>nursing infants<br>**Use with caution:**In liver and renal impairment and pre<br>existing GI symptoms<br>**Counselling:**This medication is absorbed best if taken with<br>food.<br>Your skin may become pink to brownish-black while you are<br>taking this medicine, but this is reversible. Avoid exposure<br>to sunlight.<br>Tears and urine may turn reddish-brown.|**Pregnancy:**Use, with caution, in pregnancy if clinically<br>indicated and drug of choice.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution in breastfeeding (monitor infant for<br>drowsiness)<br>**Contraindications:**See amitriptyline.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for side-effects)<br>**Contraindications:**Known severe hypersensitivity to<br>penicillins or other beta-lactams<br>**Use with caution:**In patients with history of mild<br>hypersensitivity to beta-lactams, renal/hepatic impairment<br>**Counselling:**This medicine is absorbed best if taken on an<br>empty stomach at least half an hour before food or 2 hours<br>after food.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/ vomiting (hospitalize<br>if persistent), abdominal pain,  headache,<br>tiredness, brownish-black discoloration of<br>lesions, pink to brownish-black discoloration of<br>skin including areas exposed to light; reversible<br>hair discoloration; dry skin; red discoloration<br>of faeces, urine, and other body f uids; rash,<br>pruritus.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Photosensitivity, acne-like<br>eruptions, anorexia, eosinophilic enteropathy,<br>bowel obstruction, dry eyes, dimmed vision,<br>macular and subepithelial corneal pigmentation;<br>elevation of blood sugar, weight loss, splenic<br>infarction, lymphadenopathy, GI bleeding,<br>constipation, taste disorder, dizziness,<br>drowsiness|See amitriptyline.|See benzylpenicillin.<br>**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, transient increases<br>in liver enzymes and bilirubin<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Cholestatic hepatitis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 50 mg, 100 mg_**<br>See Section 11.21 for dosing of<br>clofazimine as part of multidrug<br>treatment for multibacillary<br>leprosy<br>200-300 mg daily in 2-3 divided<br>doses for maximum 3 months|**_Capsule: 10 mg, 25 mg_**<br>Oral: see instructions in Section<br>10.11.7.|**_Capsule: 500 mg, 1 g_**<br>**_Powder for injection: 500 mg_**<br>**_in vial_**<br>Oral: 500 mg every 6 hours<br>IV: 1-2 g every 6 hours<br>IV: 2 g every 4 hours or 3 g every<br>6 hours for 6 weeks<br>IV: 2 g every 6 hours for<br>2-4 weeks|
|**D rug Indication**|**Clofazimine**<br>Leprosy (multibacillary)<br>(11.21)<br>Type 2 lepra reaction if<br>inadequate response to<br>prednisolone or if steroids<br>contraindicated<br>(11.21)|**Clomipramine**<br>Obsessive-compulsive<br>disorders; phobic states,<br>panic attacks (10.11.7)|**Cloxacillin**<br>Cellulitis (10.2.2)<br>S. aureus endocarditis<br>(11.10)<br>Septic arthritis due to<br>S. aureus (10.13.2)|

**380** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Contraindications:**inf amed, broken or infected skin.<br>**May be used with salicylic acid in psoriasis**<br>**Administration:**Skin protection possibly required to reduce<br>photosensitivity reactions.<br>Bathing can be alternated with exposure to ultraviolet (UVB)<br>rays, allowing at least 24 hours between exposure and<br>treatment with coal tar.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe at recommended<br>doses in both pregnancy and breastfeeding<br>**Contraindications:**Conditions where inhibition of peristalsis<br>should be avoided; abdominal distension; acute diarrhoeal<br>conditions such as ulcerative colitis or antibiotic-associated<br>colitis; acute respiratory depression<br>**Use with caution:**With prolonged use, tolerance or<br>dependence may occur in elderly and debilitated patients<br>and patients with hepatic or renal impairment.<br>6–10% of Caucasians and 1-2% of Asians lack the enzyme<br>CYP2D6 (necessary to metabolise codeine to morphine) and<br>are, therefore, unlikely to obtain analgesia with codeine.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**irritation, photosensitivity reactions;<br>skin, hair, and fabrics discoloured.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**hypersensitivity|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, constipation;<br>dizziness, headache, miosis, diff culty with<br>micturition, urinary retention, dry mouth,<br>dyspepsia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Dependence, biliary<br>spasm, palpitations, bradycardia, tachycardia,<br>hypothermia, hallucinations/mood changes,<br>rash; in large doses, convulsions, respiratory<br>depression, hypotension|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Solution: 5%._**<br>Topical: apply directly to the<br>affected area 1–3 times daily,<br>preferably starting with lower<br>strength preparation or add<br>100 ml to bath of tepid water<br>and soak affected area for<br>10–20 minutes, once daily to<br>once every 3 days for at least<br>10 baths.|**_Tablet: 30 mg (phosphate)_**<br>Oral: 30 mg every 4 hours;<br>maximum dose for pain 240 mg;<br>consider switch to morphine<br>when a dose of 180 mg is<br>reached.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Coal tar**<br>Chronic psoriasis, either<br>alone or in combination<br>with exposure to ultraviolet<br>light (10.2.7)|**Codeine**<br>Acute or chronic pain<br>(20.2, 20.4)|

**381** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Avoid in f rst trimester. However, if a<br>pregnant woman with HIV requires cotrimoxazole<br>prophylaxis, it should be started regardless of the stage<br>of pregnancy. Pregnant women in malarial areas who are<br>taking cotrimoxazole should not be given sulfadoxine-<br>pyrimethamine-based intermittent preventive therapy for<br>malaria. See Sections 13 and 14 for PMTCT and management<br>of cotrimoxazole side-effects.<br>Breastfeeding women living with HIV should continue to<br>receive cotrimoxazole prophylaxis.<br>**Contraindications:**Severe renal or hepatic impairment;<br>previous severe reactions to sulfa-containing drugs.<br>**Use with caution:**In predisposition to folate def ciency,<br>patients with a sulfa allergy, elderly, and G6PD def ciency.<br>See Section 13.3 for response to rash. Discontinue<br>immediately if anaemia or new jaundice appears.<br>Renal dose adjustment required.<br>Avoid in blood disorders except with specialist supervision.<br>**Administration: IV:**Dilute each 5 ml in 100–125 ml f uid,<br>preferably glucose 5%, and infuse over 60–90 minutes.<br>Maintain adequate f uid intake.<br>**Counselling:**Take this medicine with food to reduce stomach<br>upset.<br>Tell your doctor if you get a sore throat, fever, troublesome<br>rash, cough, diff culty breathing, joint pain, dark urine, or<br>pale stools.<br>If taking prolonged high dose treatment: Drink a lot of f uid –<br>at least 2–3 litres daily.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea;<br>headache; hyperkalaemia; rash; anorexia, sore<br>mouth, fever<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Stevens-Johnson<br>syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis,<br>photosensitivity – discontinue immediately;<br>drowsiness; liver damage (including jaundice<br>and hepatic necrosis), pancreatitis, C._diff cile_<br>colitis, myocarditis, cough and shortness of<br>breath, pulmonary inf ltrates, aseptic meningitis,<br>depression, convulsions, peripheral neuropathy,<br>ataxia, tinnitus, vertigo, hallucinations,<br>hypoglycaemia, blood disorders (including<br>leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, megaloblastic<br>anaemia, eosinophilia), hyponatraemia, renal<br>disorders (including interstitial nephritis),<br>arthralgia, myalgia, vasculitis, systemic lupus<br>erythematosus; rhabdomyolysis reported in HIV-<br>infected patients; crystalluria|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: sulfamethoxa-zole_**<br>**_100 mg + trimethoprim 20 mg;_**<br>**_sulfamethox-azole 400 mg +_**<br>**_trimethoprim 80 mg (single-_**<br>**_strength=SS); sulfamethoxa-_**<br>**_zole 800 mg + trimethoprim_**<br>**_160 mg (double-strength=DS);_**<br>**_Injection: sulfamethoxa-zole_**<br>**_80 mg + trimethoprim 16 mg/ml,_**<br>**_5 ml and 10 ml ampoules_**<br>IV: 5 mg/kg (based on the<br>trimethoprim component)<br>4 times daily for 21 days<br>1 DS tablet or 2 SS tablets once<br>daily. Total daily dose is 960 mg<br>(800 mg SMX + 160 mg TMP)<br>IV: 10–20 mg/kg (based on the<br>trimethoprim component) daily<br>divided into 2–4 doses AND<br>ampicillin<br>IV: 10–20 mg/kg (based on the<br>trimethoprim component) daily<br>divided into 2–4 doses AND<br>chloramphenicol|
|**D rug Indication**|**Cotrimoxazole-**<br>trimethoprim with<br>sulfamethoxazole<br>(TMP-SMX)<br>(doses based on the<br>trimethoprim component)<br>_P. jirovecii (P. carinii)_<br>pneumonia (PCP) (10.6.3)<br>Primary prophylaxis<br>for PLHIV (see 13.3 for<br>indications for primary<br>prophylaxis); secondary<br>prophylaxis after PCP<br>pneumonia (10.6.3),<br>isosporiasis (11.18),<br>toxoplasmosis (11.40)<br>Acute bacterial meningitis-<br>empirical treatment in<br>absence of ceftriaxone<br>(10.10b.3)<br>Acute bacterial meningitis<br>with anaphylaxis to<br>penicillin (empirical<br>or for conf rmed_N._<br>_meningitidis, H. inf uenzae,_<br>_S. pneumoniae_) (10.10b.3)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Third trimester use can result in neonatal<br>haemolysis and methaemoglobinaemia; folic acid 5 mg daily<br>should be given to mother.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Continue breastfeeding, monitor infant for<br>jaundice.<br>**Use with caution:**In anaemia, susceptibility to haemolysis<br>**Administration:**Obtain full blood count before starting<br>treatment; then again each week for the f rst month, and then<br>each month during treatment.<br>**Counselling:**Take dapsone with food to reduce stomach<br>upsets.<br>Stop medication and inform your doctor if troublesome rash<br>occurs.|**Pregnancy:**Use only if benef t greater than risk (risk of<br>teratogenicity).<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution in breastfeeding; not recommended,<br>but low oral bioavailability so unlikely to cause adverse<br>effects<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, aluminium<br>encephalopathy (may exacerbate neurological dysfunction)<br>**Administration:**Perform eye and ear examinations before<br>treatment and at 3-month intervals during treatment.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain or cramps, hepatitis, rash, fever,<br>jaundice, headache, nervousness, blurred vision<br>**Infrequent or rare**(dose-related and uncommon<br>at doses used for leprosy): haemolysis,<br>methaemoglobinaemia, allergic dermatitis<br>including Stevens-Johnson syndrome|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal cramps or pain; injection-site<br>reactions (including redness, pain, swelling<br>rashes and itch); hypotension (especially when<br>given too rapidly by intravenous injection);<br>asthma; fever, headache, arthralgia and<br>myalgia; growth retardation; bone deformities<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Disturbances of hearing and<br>vision (including lens opacity and retinopathy);<br>acute respiratory distress syndrome,<br>neurological disturbances (including dizziness,<br>neuropathy and paraesthesia), Yersinia<br>and mucormycosis infections, rash, renal<br>impairment, blood dyscrasias, anaphylaxis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 25 mg, 50 mg, 100 mg_**<br>Oral: 100 mg daily for 6 months<br>AND rifampicin on day 1 of each<br>month<br>Oral: 100 mg daily for 12 months<br>AND rifampicin + clofazimine<br>(see Section 11.21)<br>Oral: 100 mg daily|**_Powder for injection: 500 mg_**<br>**_(mesilate) in vial_**<br>IV (slow): Initially 15 mg/kg per<br>hour THEN  reduce after<br>4–6 hours so that total dose<br>does not exceed 80 mg/kg in<br>24 hours.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Dapsone**<br>Leprosy, paucibacillary<br>(11.21)<br>Leprosy, multibacillary<br>(11.21)<br>Alternative to cotrimoxazole<br>prophylaxis for PLHIV<br>allergic to sulfa-based<br>medicines (13.3)|**Deferoxamine**<br>**(desferrioxamine mesilate)**<br>Acute iron poisoning<br>(3.8.1)<br>(Other indications: Chronic<br>iron overload including<br>hemoglobinopathies;<br>aluminium overload in<br>end-stage renal disease;<br>diagnosis of iron or<br>aluminium overload)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**384** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Use in pregnancy only if drug<br>of choice and benef t greater than risk (risk of intrauterine<br>growth retardation); unlikely to be harmful at low doses (<40<br>mg daily prednisolone equivalent)<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution in breastfeeding; prednisolone<br>preferred<br>Contraindications: Systemic infection (unless life-threatening<br>or specif c antimicrobial therapy given); avoid live virus<br>vaccines in those receiving immunosuppressive doses.<br>**Use with caution:**In patients with infections (symptoms<br>may be masked until advanced stage; clinical presentation<br>may be atypical); can activate or exacerbate TB,<br>amoebiasis, strongyloidiasis; in the elderly and adolescents,<br>hypertension, recent myocardial infarction, congestive heart<br>failure, liver failure, renal impairment, diabetes mellitus<br>(including family history), osteoporosis, glaucoma (including<br>family history), severe affective disorder (particularly if<br>history of steroid-induced psychosis), epilepsy, psoriasis,<br>peptic ulcer, hypothyroidism, history of steroid myopathy.<br>Adrenal suppression can occur during prolonged treatment<br>and persist for years after stopping treatment.<br>**Administration:**Monitor weight, blood pressure, f uid and<br>electrolyte balance, and blood glucose throughout prolonged<br>treatment.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|See prednisolone. In addition: Burning and<br>tingling in perineal area (high dose IV treatment)<br>Dexamethasone has a long duration of action<br>and very high glucocorticoid activity in<br>conjunction with insignif cant mineralocorticoid<br>activity. This makes it particularly suitable for<br>high-dose therapy in conditions where f uid<br>retention would be a disadvantage.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 500 mg, 4 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 4 mg/ml, 1 ml ampoule_**<br>IV: 8 mg; then repeat every 8<br>hours<br>IV/IM: Initially 24 mg daily;<br>THEN reduce by 2 mg/day to<br>lowest effective maintenance<br>dose.<br>IV: 10 mg every 6 hours for<br>4 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Dexamethasone**<br>Also, see steroid<br>equivalents table in Section<br>8.2.<br>Addison’s disease (adrenal<br>insuff ciency) (3.4.3)<br>Cerebral oedema<br>associated with malignancy<br>(20.3)<br>Bacterial meningitis<br>(consider before antibiotics<br>but do not delay antibiotics)<br>(10.10b.3)<br>(**Other indications:**Some<br>malignant neoplasms, to<br>help prevent chemotherapy-<br>induced emesis)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**385** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Avoid regular use (use only if necessary, e.g.<br>seizure control). Use minimum effective dose for shortest<br>duration; associated with increased risk of cleft palate;<br>withdrawal symptoms in newborns have been reported<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use with caution; short-acting<br>benzodiazepines preferred if needed. Adverse effects<br>possible (monitor infant for drowsiness and poor feeding)<br>**Contraindications:**Respiratory disease, acute pulmonary<br>insuff ciency, sleep apnoea, severe hepatic impairment,<br>myasthenia gravis, acute angle closure glaucoma<br>**Use with caution:**In muscle weakness, history of alcohol<br>or drug abuse, marked personality disorder, elderly or<br>debilitated, porphyria, hepatic impairment (avoid if severe),<br>renal impairment.<br>Repeated use even in therapeutic doses can lead to a<br>dependence syndrome. This is unlikely when it is prescribed<br>in standard doses for up to 4 weeks, but beyond this the risk<br>increases in proportion to the duration of treatment and daily<br>dose. Avoid abrupt withdrawal.<br>**Counselling:**This medication may impair ability to perform<br>skilled tasks such as operating machinery or driving.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not for use in pregnancy<br>**Contraindications:**DEC should not be used in areas where<br>onchocerciasis or loiasis is co-endemic, due to possible<br>severe adverse reactions. Patients should be examined for<br>co-infection before using DEC.<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, cardiac disorders|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Sedation and other side-effects that<br>appear similar to alcohol intoxication<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain or cramps, respiratory<br>depression (usually due to an excessive<br>dose), withdrawal syndrome, hypotension,<br>bradycardia, dependence and abuse,<br>drowsiness and light-headedness the next day,<br>confusion and ataxia (especially in elderly),<br>amnesia, dependence, muscle weakness,<br>paradoxical increase in aggression, visual<br>disturbances, changes in libido, incontinence or<br>urinary retention, blood disorders, raised liver<br>enzymes/jaundice, hypotension with rapid IV<br>administration|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting; headache, dizziness<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Immunological reaction|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 2 mg, 5 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 5 mg/ml_**<br>**_rectal solution: 10 mg/2 ml_**<br>Oral: see Section 10.11.7<br>10 mg IV slowly, or rectally if<br>no IV access; can be repeated<br>after 10 minutes if convulsion<br>does not stop. DO NOT give IM.<br>Up to 20 mg, according to the<br>severity of alcohol withdrawal,<br>every 1-2 hours until patient is<br>calm and mildly sedated (See<br>Section 3.7.1 for how to titrate<br>diazepam dose.)<br>See Section 3.6.3.<br>See Section 20.7.|**_Tablet: 50 mg, 100 mg_**<br>Oral: 6 mg/kg in 3 divided doses<br>for 12 days OR 6 mg/kg as a<br>single dose AND albendazole<br>400 mg in a single dose.<br>Repeat every 6 months if<br>microf laraemic or symptomatic.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Diazepam**<br>Anxiety (10.11.7); adjuvant<br>analgesia for muscle<br>spasms and anxiety-related<br>pain (20.3)<br>Convulsions (QC<br>pages 6 and 19, 3.5);<br>organophosphate or<br>chloroquine poisoning (3.8)<br>Acute alcohol withdrawal<br>(3.7.1)<br>Amphetamine or cocaine<br>acute intoxication with<br>severe agitation or anxiety<br>(3.6)<br>Insomnia|**Diethylcarbamazine (DEC)**<br>Filariasis (11.12)|

**386** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended in the f rst trimester of<br>pregnancy<br>**Administration:**Take with water on an empty stomach.<br>If a patient vomits within 30 minutes of taking drug, the whole<br>dose should be re-administered; if a patient vomits within<br>30-60 minutes, half the dose should be re-administered.|**Pregnancy:**Defer treatment until after the f rst trimester.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Manufacturer advises to avoid.|**Precautions:**irritant (avoid contact with eyes, mucous<br>membranes and healthy skin).<br>**Contraindications:**hypersensitivity; avoid use on face, groin,<br>acute eruptions, excessively inf amed areas.<br>**Administration:**Localize application to plaques by<br>application in Lassar's paste (zinc paste 96%, salicylic acid<br>2%, liquid paraff n 2) or white soft paraff n to surrounding<br>skin. Dithranol must be protected from light and should be<br>supplied in appropriate light-occlusive containers.<br>**Counselling:**Wash hands thoroughly after use|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Headache, eosinophilia, cough,<br>potential for QTc prolongation|**Common:**Flatulence<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Vomiting, pruritus, urticaria|**Common:**Local irritation; excessive erythema<br>or spread of lesions (discontinue use);<br>conjunctivitis following contact with eyes;<br>staining of skin, hair, and fabrics.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Co-formulated tablet of 40 mg_**<br>**_dihydroartemisinin and 320 mg_**<br>**_piperaquine_**<br>See dosing in table in 11.25.3.|**_Tablet: 500 mg (furoate)_**<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily for<br>5 days|**_Ointment: 0.1-2%_**<br>Topical: start with 0.1%,<br>carefully apply directly to<br>lesions only, leave in contact<br>for 30 minutes, then wash off<br>thoroughly; repeat application<br>daily, gradually increasing<br>strength to 2% and contact<br>time to 60 minutes at weekly<br>intervals; some 0.1–0.5%<br>strength preparations are<br>suitable for overnight use|
|**D rug Indication**|**Dihydro-artemisinin +**<br>**piperaquine**<br>A f rst-line antimalarial<br>regimen for symptomatic,<br>uncomplicated P. falciparum<br>malaria (11.25.3)|**Diloxanide**<br>Luminal amoebicide (11.1.1)|**Dithranol**<br>Moderate to severe<br>psoriasis<br>(10.2.7)<br>Note: Stability is a problem<br>with dithranol preparations,<br>especially those of low<br>strength. Addition of<br>salicylic acid, ascorbic<br>acid or oxalic acid as<br>an antioxidant stabilises<br>dithranol products and<br>prevents discolouration<br>and inactivation. Stability<br>appears to be best in soft<br>paraff n and least in cream<br>bases.|

**387** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use only if benef t is greater than risk and no<br>alternatives are available.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended doses<br>if benef t is greater than risk; short half-life and rapidly<br>destroyed in the GI tract.<br>**Contraindications:**Tachyarrhythmia, ventricular<br>f brillation, ischaemic heart disease, phaeochromocytoma,<br>hyperthyroidism.<br>Correct hypovolaemia before administration; maintain blood<br>volume during treatment.<br>Correct hypoxia before or at same time as starting treatment.<br>See Section 3.1.<br>**Use with caution:**If history of peripheral vascular disease<br>**Administration:**Dilute before use in glucose 5% or normal<br>saline and infuse into a large vein. Do not add to sodium<br>bicarbonate or other strongly alkaline solutions.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, tachycardia, ectopic<br>beats, palpitations, anginal pain;<br>hypotension with dizziness, hypertension (in<br>overdosage), headache, dyspnoea<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Allergic reaction including<br>anaphylaxis (due to sodium metabisulphite in<br>products), abnormal ventricular conduction,<br>bradycardia, piloerction, uraemia, mydriasis,<br>peripheral vasoconstriction, asthma<br>exacerbation, necrosis/gangrene at injection<br>site|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Infusion, intravenous: 200 mg in_**<br>**_5 ml ampoule_**<br>IV: See vasopressor dosing<br>table and instructions in Section<br>3.1.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Dopamine**<br>Shock not responding to<br>f uid boluses (3.1)|

**388** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not indicated after week 8 due<br>to effects on fetal bone growth and dental discolouration;<br>short courses can be used if alternative not appropriate;<br>implicated in causing maternal hepatotoxicity, especially in<br>third trimester (dose-related). May be used for a single short<br>course of 7-10 days (monitor infant for side-effects).<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment, porphyria, systemic<br>lupus erythematosus<br>**Counselling:**Advise patients to take capsules whole,<br>with plenty of f uid, while sitting or standing to prevent<br>oesophageal irritation; may give with milk/food to counter<br>gastric irritation. Avoid exposure to sunlight or sunlamps.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea, anorexia,<br>epigastric burning, staining of growing teeth and<br>occasional dental hypoplasia, photosensitivity<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Tinnitus, hypersensitivity<br>reactions, visual disturbances, hepatotoxicity,<br>blood disorders, C._diff cile_colitis, oesophagitis<br>and oesophageal ulceration|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 100 mg_**<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily for<br>5- 7 days<br>See Section 11.25.<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily for<br>14 days<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily for<br>30 days<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily for 7<br>days<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily for 14<br>days<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily until<br>healed<br>Oral/IV: See table in Section<br>10.15.5.<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily for<br>4-6 weeks|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Contraindicated in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding<br>U**se with caution:**In renal impairment hospitalize and<br>supervise closely during treatment.<br>**Administration:**Monitor blood count for bone marrow<br>suppression.|**Pregnancy:**Avoid in pregnancy.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Amount excreted probably too small to be<br>harmful.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to ACE inhibitors<br>(including angioedema)<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, peripheral vascular<br>disease, aortic stenosis, hepatic impairment<br>**Administration:**When starting, stop diuretics for 24 hours<br>and start with a low dose at bedtime.<br>Check renal function and electrolytes before starting.<br>**Counselling:**While taking this medicine, you may feel dizzy<br>on standing. Get up gradually from sitting or lying to minimize<br>this; sit or lie down if you feel dizzy.<br>Do not take potassium supplements while you are taking this<br>medicine unless prescribed by your clinician.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Anaemia, leukopenia,<br>thrombocytopenia, convulsions, impaired<br>hearing, vomiting, abdominal pain, headache,<br>facial oedema|**Common:**Hypotension if diuretics co-<br>prescribed, cough, hyperkalaemia, headache,<br>dizziness, fatigue, nausea, renal impairment,<br>stomatitis, glossitis<br>**Infrequent or rare:**See current formulary such<br>as BNF|

See<br>Section 11.31.5 for dose<br>adjustment.|

**391** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used at the recommended doses<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution in breastfeeding. Monitor infant for<br>side-effects such as irritability, restlessness, tremor.<br>**Use with caution:**In hyperthyroidism, hypertension, diabetes<br>mellitus, heart disease, arrhythmias, cerebrovascular<br>disease, second stage of labour, and the elderly<br>**Administration:**Intravenous epinephrine should be given<br>only by those experienced in its use and in a setting where<br>patients can be carefully monitored.|**Pregnancy:**Contraindicated at induction of labour or f rst<br>and second stages of labour as can cause premature uterine<br>contractions and decrease uterine blood f ow (possible fetal<br>hypoxia and death)<br>**Breastfeeding:**Single dose post-partum compatible with<br>breastfeeding; avoid prolonged or multiple dosing<br>**Contraindications:**Vascular disease, severe cardiac disease<br>(angina pectoris), severe hypertension, severe renal or<br>hepatic impairment, sepsis, pre-eclampsia or eclampsia<br>Use with caution: In cardiac disease, hypertension, renal<br>impairment, multiple pregnancy, porphyria|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**anxiety, headache, fear, palpitations,<br>tachycardia, tremor, dizziness, sweating, pallor,<br>nausea, vomiting, hyperglycaemia.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**hypertension, arrhythmias,<br>angina, pulmonary oedema (a sign of excessive<br>dosage or extreme sensitivity), tissue necrosis<br>(if injected IM or SC)|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Headache, dizziness,<br>tinnitus, abdominal pain, chest pain/palpitations,<br>dyspnoea, bradycardia, transient hypertension,<br>stroke, myocardial infarction, pulmonary<br>oedema|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 1 mg in 1 ml ampoule_**<br>**_(1:1000)_**<br>IM (1:1000): 0.5 ml in a single<br>dose if 50 kg (0.3 ml if 30 kg, 0.4<br>ml if 40 kg) THEN may repeat at<br>5-minute intervals<br>IV infusion: See vasopressor<br>dosing table and instructions in<br>Section 3.1<br>IM: 0.5 mg (0.5 ml of 1:1000)|**Injection: 200 mcg (0.2 mg)**<br>**(hydrogen maleate) in 1 ml**<br>**ampoule**<br>IM: 0.2 mg in a single dose AND<br>repeat IM or IV if bleeding<br>continues<br>IV (slow) or IM: 0.2 mg|
|**D rug Indication**|**Epinephrine (adrenaline)**<br>Anaphylaxis (QC p. 11, 3.1.3)<br>Septic shock not<br>responding to f uid boluses<br>(3.1.5); cardiogenic shock<br>(3.1.4)<br>Severe bronchospasm<br>if no inhaled salbutamol<br>available (3.2.4)|**Ergometrine**<br>Haemorrhage in early<br>pregnancy (QC p. 24)<br>Postpartum haemorrhage:<br>heavy bleeding continues<br>with soft uterus after<br>oxytocin given and placenta<br>delivered or after manual<br>removal of placenta (QC<br>p. 25, 27)|

**392** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not known to be harmful in<br>pregnancy or breastfeeding<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to erythromycin or other<br>macrolides, porphyria<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment, predisposition to QT<br>interval prolongation (including electrolyte disturbances and<br>concomitant use of drugs that prolong the QT interval).<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions.<br>**Administration:**Reconstitute injection with water for<br>injection only. Dilute further for administration.<br>Parenteral erythromycin is an irritant and may cause<br>thrombophlebitis.<br>Infuse at a rate of 1-5 mg/ml over 60 minutes or slower, via a<br>central vein where possible. Avoid extravasation.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain or cramps<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Allergic reactions, reversible<br>hearing loss, jaundice, cardiac effects,<br>myasthenia-like syndrome, erythema multiforme<br>(Stevens-Johnson syndrome)/ toxic epidermal<br>necrolysis, QT prolongation, C._diff cile_colitis|

|**D rug Indication**|**Erythromycin**<br>Early syphilis (11.37)<br>Late latent syphilis (11.37)<br>Uncomplicated genital<br>Chlamydia; non-gonococcal<br>urethritis (10.15.4)<br>Lympogranuloma venereum<br>(10.14.3)<br>Granuloma inguinale<br>(donovaniasis) (10.14.3)<br>Chancroid (10.1.3)<br>Severe pneumonia (3.2.3) or<br>non-severe pneumonia not<br>responding to oral therapy<br>after 3 days (10.6.3)<br>Non-severe pneumonia<br>(10.6)<br>Rheumatic fever, primary<br>treatment if allergic to<br>penicillin (11.32)|

**394** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Counselling:**Before applying, wash affected area with a<br>mild soap and warm water, rinse thoroughly, and pat dry.<br>Avoid contact with eyes, lips, and inside of your nose or<br>mouth. Noticeable improvement may be seen in 3–4 weeks.<br>However, 6–12 weeks of treatment may be required before<br>maximum benef t is seen.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for side-effects,<br>including jaundice)<br>**Contraindications:**History of optic neuritis and severe renal<br>impairment<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly with ocular defects, renal<br>impairment<br>**Administration:**Ocular examination recommended<br>before and during treatment; patients should report visual<br>disturbances immediately and discontinue treatment. Renal<br>dose adjustment required.<br>**Counselling:**If you see less clearly or colour vision is<br>affected, stop the medication and tell your clinician.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Dry skin, itch, stinging, burning feeling<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Desquamation, erythema<br>If irritation occurs, apply less frequently; if it<br>persists, stop treatment.|**Common:**Optic neuritis<br>**Infrequent or rare:**peripheral neuritis<br>(especially in legs), rash, pruritus, urticaria,<br>thrombocytopenia, gout, jaundice|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Gel/lotion: erythromycin 2%_**<br>Gel: Apply once a day in a thin<br>f lm to affected area.<br>Lotion: Apply twice daily in a<br>thin f lm to affected area.<br>See Section 10.2.3|**_Tablets: 100 mg, 400 mg_**<br>Oral: 15 mg/kg daily or 30 mg/kg<br>3 times a week<br>15 mg/kg/day AND<br>clarithromycin or azithromycin<br>for 6 months|
|**D rug Indication**|**Erythromycin, topical**<br>Mild to moderate acne<br>(10.2.3)|**Ethambutol**<br>Tuberculosis, initial phase<br>of combination therapy<br>(15.3)<br>MAC (in HIV-infected<br>patients) (11.27)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**395** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Judgement needed; however,<br>fetus is more likely to be at risk from metabolic derangements<br>from ethylene glycol/methanol poisoning than from ethanol<br>as antidote. Avoid breastfeeding during ethanol treatment<br>since ethanol passes into breast milk.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to ethanol.<br>**Use with caution:**Causes CNS depression, and effects are<br>additive with other CNS depressants, e.g. benzodiazepines.<br>Increased risk of hypoglycaemia in those with alcohol<br>dependence. In patients taking drugs that inhibit aldehyde<br>dehydrogenase, e.g. disulf ram, metronidazole, griseofulvin,<br>use can result in acetaldehyde syndrome (nausea, f ushing,<br>autonomic instability).|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Contraindications:**Haemosiderosis, haemochromatosis;<br>any form of anaemia not caused by iron def ciency; patients<br>receiving repeated blood transfusions; parenteral iron<br>therapy<br>**Administration:**If side-effects occur, the dose may be<br>reduced; alternatively, another iron salt may be used, but an<br>improvement in tolerance may simply be a result of a lower<br>content of elemental iron.<br>See Section 10.18.3 for monitoring, duration of treatment.<br>**Counselling:**Although iron preparations are best absorbed<br>on an empty stomach, they may be taken after food to reduce<br>gastrointestinal adverse effects. Keep out of children's<br>reach.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Signs of intoxication, confusion,<br>drowsiness, coma, respiratory depression,<br>hypoglycaemia|**Common:**Constipation (particularly in<br>older patients; occasionally leads to faecal<br>impaction), diarrhoea, dark stools, nausea,<br>epigastric pain, gastrointestinal irritation<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Haemosiderosis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Oral liquid:  40–43% alcohol_**<br>**_(vodka or whisky)_**<br>Oral (or by NG):<br>Loading: 1.8 ml/kg over 15–30<br>minutes (diluted).<br>Maintenance dose: 0.2 ml/kg/<br>hour (non-drinker) or 0.46 ml/kg/<br>hour (heavy alcohol user)|**_Tablet: equivalent to 60 mg iron_**<br>Elemental iron, 60 mg for mild<br>anaemia, 120 mg (plus 400<br>mcg folic acid) for moderate to<br>severe anaemia daily<br>Elemental iron 100 mg AND<br>400 mcg folic acid daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Ethanol**<br>Ethylene glycol or methanol<br>poisoning (3.8.1)|**Ferrous sulfate**<br>See elemental iron<br>equivalents in Section 8.3.<br>Iron-def ciency anaemia<br>(10.18.3)<br>Preventive iron<br>supplementation in<br>pregnant women without<br>anaemia (14.1.1)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Single dose unlikely to pose a risk to fetus, but<br>avoid high dose or prolonged treatment.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Safe in usual dosage for short-term treatment<br>(monitor infant for side-effects)<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, sensitivity to other<br>azoles.<br>Monitor liver function (discontinue if signs or symptoms of<br>hepatic disease).<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions. Renal dose adjustment<br>required.<br>**Counselling:**Tell your doctor if you feel unusually tired,<br>nauseous, or are not eating, or if you notice dark urine, pale<br>faeces, or yellowing of the white of your eyes or skin.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea/vomiting, abdominal<br>pain, diarrhoea, headache, hepatic disorders,<br>dizziness, seizures, alopecia, rash (withdraw<br>treatment), hypersensitivity reactions, blood<br>disorders, hypokalaemia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 50 mg_**<br>**_Infusion, intravenous: 2 mg/ml_**<br>**_Oral liquid: 50 mg/5 ml_**<br>Oral: 150 mg as a single dose<br>Oral: 100-200 mg daily for<br>7-14 days<br>IV/oral: 100-200 mg daily for<br>14-21 days<br>IV/oral: 400 mg daily, continued<br>for 14 days after last fever<br>See options in Section 11.5<br>Oral: 200 mg daily until the<br>patient is on successful ART<br>and CD4 count is maintained<br>above 200 for 6 months<br>Oral: 400 mg as a single dose<br>Oral: 150-300 mg weekly until<br>cure (6–12 months)<br>Oral: 200 mg daily for 6 weeks|
|**D rug Indication**|**Fluconazole**<br>Vaginal candidiasis<br>(10.15.4, 11.4)<br>Recurrent oral candidiasis<br>(10.17.3, 11.4)<br>Oesophageal candidiasis<br>(10.7b.3, 11.4)<br>Invasive candida disease<br>and candidemia (11.4)<br>Cryptococcal meningitis,<br>with or without<br>amphotericin B (11.5)<br>Cryptococcal meningitis<br>(secondary prophylaxis)<br>(11.5)<br>Pityriasis versicolor (10.2.8)<br>Dermatophytosis (10.2.7)<br>Leishmaniasis, cutaneous<br>(11.20.1)|

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**397** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Avoid in pregnancy (teratogenic in animal studies);<br>consider alternatives; use only if benef t greater than risk.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Not recommended until more information<br>available; use only if benef t greater than risk; consider<br>alternatives.<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly, renal impairment, pre-existing<br>bone marrow suppression<br>**Administration:**Monitor liver function, kidney function, and<br>blood counts when use with amphotericin B (check weekly<br>in renal impairment or in blood disorders). Renal dose<br>adjustment required.<br>**Storage:**Keep at 15–25 0C (forms f uorouracil above 25 0C<br>and can precipitate below 15 0C).<br>**Counselling:**Take the capsule with food.|**Pregnancy:**Use with caution; self-limiting withdrawal<br>symptoms have been reported in newborns (e.g. distress,<br>poor feeding, sleep disturbances).<br>**Breastfeeding:**Not recommended; long half-life, may<br>accumulate in breast milk; if required, use lowest effective<br>dose.<br>**Contraindications:**In combination with MAO-Is<br>**Use with caution:**Renal or hepatic failure (consider dose<br>reduction), diabetes mellitus.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions.<br>**Administration:**Although symptomatic relief may be<br>apparent within the f rst 1–3 weeks, optimum antidepressant<br>effect usually requires at least 4 weeks or more of therapy.<br>Watch for agitation and suicidal ideation and behaviour (see<br>Section 10.11). If history of mania or bipolar disorder, use a<br>mood stabilizer f rst (see Section 10.11.5 on bipolar disorder<br>for details).|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, rash, diarrhoea<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Cardiotoxicity, confusion,<br>hallucinations, convulsions, headache, sedation,<br>vertigo, alterations in liver function tests<br>(hepatitis and hepatic necrosis reported), toxic<br>epidermal necrolysis; blood disorders including<br>thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, aplastic anaemia|**Common:**Restlessness, nervousness,<br>insomnia, anorexia and other gastrointestinal<br>disturbances, headache, sweating, decreased<br>libido<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Marked akathisia (inner<br>restlessness), bleeding abnormalities in patients<br>taking aspirin or non-steroidal anti-inf ammatory<br>drugs|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 250 mg_**<br>**_Infusion: 2.5 g in 250 ml_**<br>IV/oral: 100 mg/kg daily in 4<br>divided doses for 14 days|**_Capsule/tablet: 20 mg_**<br>Oral: Initiate treatment with<br>20 mg daily (to reduce risk of<br>side-effects that undermine<br>adherence, may start at 10 mg<br>(e.g. half a tablet) once daily<br>and increase to 20 mg if the<br>medication is tolerated); THEN<br>if no response in 4–6 weeks<br>or partial response in 6<br>weeks, increase dose by 20<br>mg (maximum dose 60 mg)<br>according to tolerability and<br>symptom response.<br>For elderly and medically ill, see<br>Section 10.11.6|
|**D rug Indication**|**Flucytosine (5-FC)**<br>Cryptococcal meningitis (as<br>an adjunct to amphotericin)<br>(11.5)|**Fluoxetine**<br>Moderate or severe<br>depression (10.11.6);<br>chronic anxiety disorders<br>(10.11.7)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use with caution; monitor infant for reversible<br>extrapyramidal side-effects.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, if drug of choice (monitor<br>infant for side-effects such as sedation).<br>**Contraindications:**See chlorpromazine.<br>**Use with caution:**See chlorpromazine.<br>**Elderly/debilitated**(**including HIV stage 3 or 4**): See<br>chlorpromazine.<br>**Counselling:**See chlorpromazine. Warn patients that this<br>medication may impair ability to perform skilled tasks such as<br>operating machinery or driving.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended<br>dose in pregnancy and breastfeeding<br>**Contraindications:**Folate-dependent malignant disease<br>Women receiving antiepileptic therapy need counselling<br>before starting folic acid.<br>Should never be given without vitamin B12 in undiagnosed<br>megaloblastic anaemia or other vitamin B12 def ciency<br>states due to risk of precipitating subacute combined<br>degeneration of the spinal cord.|

|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 25 mg, 1 ml ampoule_**<br>Deep IM injection:  Initially<br>12.5 mg in gluteal region THEN<br>repeat every 2–4 weeks.<br>Typical effective dose is 12.5-100<br>mg IM every 2-5 weeks.<br>In elderly or medically ill<br>patients: IM: Initially 6.25 mg<br>deep injection in gluteal region<br>AND repeat IM injections<br>every 2-5 weeks using lowest<br>effective dose.|**_Tablet: 1 mg, 5 mg_**<br>Oral: 5 mg daily for 4 months (in<br>pregnancy, continue to term); up<br>to 15 mg daily for malabsorption<br>states if needed<br>AND vitamin B12<br>Oral: 1 mg daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Fluphenazine**<br>Antipsychotic: maintenance<br>treatment of schizophrenia<br>and other psychosis<br>(10.11.4)|**Folic acid**<br>Folate-def ciency<br>megaloblastic anaemia<br>(10.18.3)<br>Sickle-cell disease<br>prophylaxis (10.18.3)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Prevention of neural tube<br>defect in pregnancy;<br>prevention of recurrence of<br>neural tube defect)|

**399** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not recommended during<br>pregnancy or breastfeeding. Manufacturer advises use only<br>if potential benef t outweighs risk.<br>**Use with caution**and at lower doses if renal or hepatic<br>impairment.<br>Does not alter the CNS effects of drinking alcohol or<br>withdrawal symptoms|**Pregnancy:**Consider alternatives; loop diuretics not<br>recommended unless absolutely necessary (e.g. cardiac<br>failure). Neonatal ototoxicity has been reported.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Limited data. Not recommended;<br>theoretically, may suppress lactation due to diuresis<br>**Contraindications:**Anuria due to renal failure, precomatose<br>states associated with liver cirrhosis, electrolyte depletion<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly (reduce dose), hypotensive<br>patients, renal impairment, hepatic impairment, prostatic<br>enlargement<br>**Administration:**Dose to be diluted in suitable amount of<br>infusion f uid (saline or LR; glucose solutions are unsuitable),<br>depending on hydration of patient, at rate not to exceed<br>4 mg/minute.<br>Monitor electrolytes, particularly potassium and sodium.<br>Correct hypovolaemia before using in oliguria.<br>IV to PO conversion is 1:2 (e.g. 20 mg IV is equal to 40 mg PO)|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Hypersensitivity reactions<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Pyrexia after parenteral use|**Common:**Electrolyte imbalance (hypokalaemia,<br>hyponatraemia, hypomagnesaemia),<br>hyperuricaemia and gout, orthostatic<br>hypotension, hypovolaemia, syncope, dizziness<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hypochloraemic alkalosis,<br>hypocalcaemia, hyperglycaemia (less than<br>with thiazide diuretics), paraesthesia, blood<br>disorders (including thrombocytopenia,<br>leukopenia, agranulocytosis, aplastic anaemia,<br>haemolytic anaemia), bone marrow depression<br>(withdraw treatment); deafness (with rapid<br>administration of large parenteral doses and in<br>renal impairment), hypotension, hypersensitivity<br>reaction (including anaphylaxis), temporary<br>increase in plasma cholesterol and triglyceride<br>concentration|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection, powder for_**<br>**_reconstitution, folinic acid (as_**<br>**_calcium salt) 3 mg/ml in 10 ml_**<br>**_ampoule Tablet: 15 mg_**<br>IV: 50 mg every 4 hours for 6 doses<br>Oral: 10-25 mg with each dose of<br>pyrimethamine|**_Tablet: 40 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 10 mg/ml_**<br>Oral: Initially 20–80 mg daily in<br>morning (higher starting doses<br>recommended when patient<br>not naive to drug or renal<br>impairment)<br>Maintenance dose: 20–40 mg<br>daily (may be increased to<br>80 mg daily or more in resistant<br>oedema)<br>IV (slow): 20–50 mg (if<br>necessary, increase by 20 mg<br>steps every 2 hours)<br>(see f owchart in Section 3.2.5)<br>IV: Initially give 20 mg;<br>monitor urinary response.<br>(see dosing in Section 11.31.3)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Folinic acid (calcium**<br>**folinate, calcium**<br>**leucovorin)**<br>Methanol poisoning (3.8.1)<br>Toxoplasmosis (11.40)<br>(**Other indications:**Given<br>with methotrexate and 5 FU<br>chemotherapy)|**Furosemide**<br>Oedema (not lymphoedema)<br>(10.4.3)<br>Acute pulmonary oedema<br>(3.2.5)<br>Oliguria in acute kidney<br>injury (11.31.3)|

**400** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use with extreme caution; has been associated<br>with fetal abnormalities such as hypospadias, unilateral renal<br>agenesis.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, in breastfeeding; monitor<br>infant for sedation and lethargy.<br>**Caution:**Monitor for depression, suicidal ideation, unusual<br>mood, behaviour change; mixed seizure disorder (including<br>absence seizure); renal impairment; encephalopathy<br>**Administration:**Renal dose adjustment required.<br>Avoid stopping abruptly, which may cause anxiety, insomnia,<br>nausea, pain and sweating. Gradually reduce dose over at<br>least 1 week.<br>**Counselling:**This medicine may cause drowsiness or<br>dizziness. If affected, do not drive or operate heavy<br>machinery.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Avoid during pregnancy or<br>breastfeeding unless need justif es the risk. To avoid<br>pregnancy, advise use of an effective contraceptive.<br>**Contraindications:**Neutropenia (ANC <500) or<br>thrombocytopenia (platelets <25 000); concurrent use with<br>zidovudine<br>**Use with caution:**Ganciclovir is toxic. Personnel should be<br>adequately protected during handling and administration.<br>If solution comes into contact with skin or mucosa, wash<br>off immediately with soap and water. In patients with<br>renal impairment, renal dose adjustment required. Affects<br>spermatogenesis and fertility.<br>**Administration:**To avoid phlebitis at the injection site (related<br>to high pH of solution), administer in veins with good f ow.<br>Maintain adequate hydration.<br>Monitor blood counts 2–3 times per week.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, dry<br>mouth, dyspepsia, constipation, abdominal pain,<br>f atulence; appetite changes, gingivitis, weight<br>gain; hypertension, vasodilation, oedema;<br>dyspnoea, cough, rhinitis<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Confusion, depression,<br>hostility, sleep disturbances, headache,<br>dizziness, anxiety, amnesia, ataxia, dysarthria,<br>nystagmus, tremor, asthenia, paraesthesia,<br>hyperkinesia; inf uenza-like symptoms;<br>impotence, urinary incontinence; leukopenia;<br>myalgia, arthralgia; diplopia, amblyopia; rash,<br>purpura, pruritus, acne; rarely, pancreatitis,<br>hepatitis, jaundice, palpitation, hallucinations,<br>movement disorders, thrombocytopenia, blood-<br>glucose f uctuations in patients with diabetes,<br>tinnitus, acute renal failure, Stevens-Johnson<br>syndrome, alopecia|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>dyspepsia, abdominal pain, constipation,<br>f atulence, dysphagia; hepatic dysfunction;<br>dyspnoea, chest pain, cough; headache,<br>insomnia, convulsions, dizziness, neuropathy,<br>depression, anxiety, confusion, abnormal<br>thinking, fatigue; weight loss, anorexia;<br>infection, fever, night sweats; anaemia,<br>leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, pancytopenia,<br>renal impairment; myalgia, arthralgia; macular<br>oedema, retinal detachment, vitreous f oaters,<br>eye pain; ear pain, taste disturbance; dermatitis,<br>pruritus; injection-site reactions<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Chest pain, chills, mouth<br>ulceration, cough, dry mouth, drowsiness,<br>arthralgia, pancreatitis, arrhythmias,<br>hypotension, anaphylactic reactions, psychosis,<br>tremor, male infertility, haematuria, disturbances<br>in hearing and vision, alopecia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 300 mg_**<br>Oral: Initial dose of 300 mg daily;<br>THEN increase to 300 mg twice<br>daily; THEN 300 mg 3 times daily<br>as needed THEN titrate with 100<br>mg increments every 3 days  to<br>a maximum of 3.6 g daily (given<br>as 1200 mg 3 times daily or 900<br>mg 4 times daily|**_Injection: 500 mg vial_**<br>Induction dose:<br>IV: 5 mg/kg twice daily for<br>3-4 weeks<br>IV: 5 mg/kg twice daily for<br>3- 6 weeks<br>See Section 11.8 for<br>maintenance with<br>valganciclovir, if available.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Gabapentin**<br>Neuropathic pain (if poor<br>response to amitryptiline<br>in HIV patient on ART)<br>(10.10a.6)|**Ganciclovir**<br>Cytomegalovirus (CMV)<br>retinochoroiditis in HIV-<br>infected patients (11.8)<br>CMV oesophagitis, gastritis,<br>neurologic, in HIV-infected<br>patients (11.8)|

**401** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Avoid unless essential for serious infections;<br>monitor levels to minimize potential for ototoxicity and<br>nephrotoxicity.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended doses (monitor<br>infant for thrush, diarrhoea)<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to aminoglycoside group<br>of antibiotics<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, pre-existing tinnitus/<br>hearing loss, conditions with muscular weakness, obesity,<br>the elderly (dosage adjustment required).<br>Monitor renal, auditory, vestibular function, serum-<br>gentamicin concentration.<br>Avoid prolonged use.<br>One-hour (peak) concentration not to exceed 5–10 mg/l<br>(3–5 mg/l for endocarditis) and pre-dose (_trough_)<br>concentration less than 2 mg/l (less than 1 mg/l for<br>endocarditis).<br>Avoid use with neuromuscular blocking agents (additive<br>toxicity) and other renal/ototoxic agents.<br>Avoid monotherapy with gentamicin especially for severe<br>infections of unclear etiology.<br>**Administration:**Renal dose adjustment required.<br>Can be dosed daily (4.5 mg/kg every 24 hours) or 1.5 mg/kg<br>every 8 hours.<br>The empirical antibiotic dose for emergency management<br>of 240 mg (QC p. 19) represents the full daily dose for a 60 kg<br>person.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Neuromuscular blockade,<br>renal electrolyte wasting (Mg, K, Ca),<br>antibiotic-associated colitis, nausea/vomiting,<br>hypersensitivity reactions|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 10 mg, 40 mg (as_**<br>**_sulfate)/ml in 2 ml vial_**<br>IM/IV (slow): 4–6 mg/kg daily in<br>divided doses every 8 hours for<br>2 weeks AND ampicillin<br>IV: 240 mg in a single dose<br>AND ampicillin 2 g IV/IM<br>IV/IM: 3 mg/kg per 24  hours<br>in 1 dose or in 2 or 3 equally<br>divided doses for 2 weeks<br>AND benzylpenicillin or<br>ceftriaxone or vancomycin<br>IV: 1.5 mg/kg every 8 hours AND<br>clindamycin AND metronidazole<br>IV: 1.5 mg/kg every 8 hours AND<br>ampicillin AND metronidazole<br>IV: 5 mg/kg daily in divided<br>doses every 8 hours AND<br>doxycycline for 15 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Gentamicin**<br>General dosing for<br>infections, due to<br>susceptible organisms,<br>such as upper UTI (11.44);<br>septic shock (3.1.5); severe<br>pneumonia (3.2.3)<br>Initial empirical antibiotics<br>for emergency management<br>(QC p. 19)<br>Endocarditis from viridans<br>streptococci- non-<br>complicated cases (as part<br>of combination therapy)<br>(11.10)<br>PID (10.15.5)<br>Cholangitis and peritonitis<br>(10.7a.2)<br>Brucellosis (11.3)|

**402** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Use with caution:**Prolonged use may lead to sensitization<br>and emergence of resistant organisms including fungi;<br>discontinue if there is purulent discharge, inf ammation,<br>exacerbation of pain.|**Pregnancy:**Avoid pregnancy during and for 1 month after<br>treatment; men should not father children within 6 months of<br>treatment. Consider alternative treatments.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Not recommended; use only if benef t is<br>greater than risk; consider alternatives.<br>Contraindications: Severe liver disease, porphyria, systemic<br>lupus erythematosus<br>**Use with caution:**In pre-existing hepatic insuff ciency<br>(closely monitor hepatic function throughout treatment),<br>blood disorders (monitor blood count weekly during f rst<br>month of treatment), penicillin allergy (cross-sensitivity may<br>occur)<br>**Counselling:**Take with milk or food. May impair ability to<br>perform skilled tasks such as operating machinery or driving.<br>Avoid sun exposure. During treatment and for 4 weeks after,<br>drinking alcohol may cause increased heart rate and skin<br>f ushing.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Burning, stinging, itching, dermatitis|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, anorexia,<br>headache<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Leukopenia; hepatotoxicity;<br>sleep disturbances; photosensitivity; systemic<br>lupus erythematosus; rash, toxic epidermal<br>necrolysis, erythema multiforme; peripheral<br>neuropathy; confusion, impaired coordination|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Solution (eye drops): 0.3%_**<br>1 drop every 2 hours, reducing<br>frequency as infection is<br>controlled THEN continue for 48<br>hours after healing is complete.|**_Tablets: 125 mg, 250 mg_**<br>**_Capsules: 250 mg_**<br>Oral: 500 mg to 1 g daily but not<br>less than 10 mg/kg<br>Duration of treatment depends<br>on the infection and thickness<br>of keratin at site of infection:<br>at least 4 weeks for skin and<br>hair, at least 6 weeks for<br>scalp ringworm and, in severe<br>infection, up to 3 months;<br>6 months for f ngernails,<br>12 months or more for toe nails.|

**403** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use with caution and at lowest effective dose;<br>reversible respiratory depression, extrapyramidal effects,<br>diff culty feeding have been reported in newborn.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution in breastfeeding (monitor infant for<br>drowsiness)<br>**Contraindications:**See chlorpromazine.<br>**Use with caution:**See chlorpromazine.<br>**Elderly/debilitated**(**including HIV stage 3 or 4**): See<br>chlorpromazine.<br>**Administration:**Monitor blood pressure and maintain supine<br>position for 30 minutes after intramuscular injection.<br>**Counselling:**This medication may impair your ability to<br>perform skilled tasks such as operating machinery or driving.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**See chlorpromazine.**<br>With the exception of tardive dyskinesia,<br>haloperidol has more prominent extrapyramidal<br>side-effects but fewer autonomic side-effects<br>than chlorpromazine.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 2 mg; 5 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 5 mg in 1 ml ampoule_**<br>Oral: Initially 1.5-3 mg once daily<br>(typical effective dose is 3-20<br>mg daily)<br>In elderly or medically ill<br>patients:  Oral: Initially<br>0.5–1 mg once daily (use the<br>lowest effective dose)<br>IM/oral: 2 mg every hour up to 5<br>doses (maximum dose 10 mg).<br>In elderly and patients with<br>complicating medical illness:<br>IM/oral: 0.5-1 mg every hour up<br>to 3 doses maximum dose 3 mg)<br>See dosing in Section 3.7.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Haloperidol**<br>Psychoses (including<br>schizophrenia) (10.11)<br>Short-term adjunctive<br>management of agitation,<br>violent behaviour, severe<br>anxiety (QC p. 29) or severe<br>symptoms of acute mania<br>with agitation (10.11.5)<br>Alcohol withdrawal delirium<br>that persists after the stage<br>of tremor and sweating has<br>subsided (3.7)<br>Antiemetic when other<br>agents not available (10.7c)|
