Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**404** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use only in acute treatment of hypertensive<br>emergencies; avoid large boluses, as fetal distress and<br>arrhythmias have been reported.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, if benef ts are greater<br>than risks; monitor infant for effects such as hypotension,<br>bradycardia, fatigue.<br>**Contraindications:**Idiopathic systemic lupus erythematosus,<br>severe tachycardia, high output heart failure, myocardial<br>insuff ciency due to mechanical obstruction, corpulmonale,<br>dissecting aortic aneurysm, porphyria<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment, renal impairment,<br>coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular disease.<br>May provoke angina (avoid after myocardial infarction until<br>stabilized).<br>**Administration:**Renal dose adjustment required.<br>**Counselling:**This medicine may cause dizziness, especially<br>at the start of treatment. If affected, do not drive or operate<br>heavy machinery.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Flushing, headache, dizziness,<br>tachycardia, palpitation, oedema<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea/vomiting, ischaemia,<br>postural hypotension, abnormal liver function,<br>systemic lupus erythematosus-like syndrome,<br>blood disorders (haemolytic anaemia,<br>leukopenia, thrombocytopenia)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Powder for injection: 20 mg_**<br>**_(hydrochloride) in ampoule_**<br>IV (slow): 5 mg diluted with<br>10 ml sodium chloride 0.9%; may<br>repeat after 30 minutes|
|**D rug Indication**|**Hydralazine IV**<br>Acute pulmonary oedema<br>with severe hypertension<br>if isosorbide dinitrate not<br>available (3.2.5); severe<br>hypertension in pre-<br>eclampsia and eclampsia<br>(3.2.5)-  see IMPAC MCPC<br>(**Other indications:**Heart<br>failure; hypertension (oral);<br>hypertensive crisis)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**405** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Avoid use; may cause electrolyte disturbances<br>or neonatal thrombocytopenia. Reduction in maternal blood<br>volume may diminish uteroplacental perfusion.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use with caution but unlikely to suppress<br>lactation.<br>**Contraindications:**Severe renal or severe hepatic<br>impairment; hyponatraemia, hypercalcaemia, refractory<br>hypokalaemia, symptomatic hyperuricaemia; Addison’s<br>disease<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly; electrolytes may need to be<br>monitored with high doses; may aggravate diabetes mellitus<br>and gout; may exacerbate systemic lupus erythematosus;<br>porphyria<br>**Counselling:**Take the medicine once daily in the morning.<br>While taking this medicine, you may feel dizzy on standing.<br>Get up gradually from sitting or lying to minimize this effect.<br>Sit or lie down if you become dizzy.|**Pregnancy:**May be used at the recommended doses; caution<br>in f rst trimester due to possibility of oral cleft; limited fetal<br>exposure due to inactivation by placenta<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended doses; caution<br>with high parenteral/oral doses<br>**Contraindications:**Not relevant to emergency use. For<br>contraindications related to long-term use, see prednisolone.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Dizziness, dry mouth, weakness,<br>muscle cramps, polyuria, orthostatic<br>hypotension, hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia,<br>hypochloraemic alkalosis, hypomagnesaemia<br>hyperuricaemia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea/vomiting, weakness,<br>lethargy, drowsiness, seizures, headache,<br>oliguria, arrhythmias, hypercalcaemia,<br>hyperglycaemia, rash, photosensitivity, altered<br>plasma lipid concentration; rarely, impotence<br>(reversible); blood disorders (including<br>neutropenia, thrombocytopenia); pancreatitis,<br>intrahepatic cholestasis, acute renal<br>failure, hypersensitivity reactions (including<br>pneumonitis, severe skin reactions|Associated with long-term treatment, which is<br>not recommended with IV hydrocortisone. See<br>prednisolone.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 25 mg_**<br>25 mg once daily (12.5 mg in<br>elderly).<br>Increase to 50 mg as needed.<br>See Section 11.31.5 for addition<br>hydro-chlorthiazide to enalapril|**_Injection: 100 mg_**<br>**_(as sodium succinate) in vial_**<br>IV (slow): 100 mg in a single<br>dose<br>IV: 100 mg initially AND repeat<br>every 8 hours.<br>Convert to oral dose once<br>patient stable.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Hydrochlorothiazide**<br>Oedema (10.4.3); mild<br>hyperkalaemia (5.2.2)<br>HIV-associated<br>nephropathy (11.31.5)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Hypertension; heart failure)|**Hydrocortisone**<br>Also see Section 8.2 steroid<br>equivalents table.<br>Anaphylaxis (3.1.3);<br>moderate or severe<br>bronchospasm, if suspect<br>asthma or COPD or unable<br>to take oral medication<br>(3.2.4)<br>Addison’s syndrome (acute<br>adrenal insuff ciency)<br>(3.4.3); urticaria (10.2.9)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**406** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Topical preparations unlikely to cause any<br>adverse effects in pregnancy or breastfeeding, as systemic<br>absorption is expected to be minimal.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Wipe excess cream from nipple area before<br>feeding.<br>**Contraindications:**Untreated skin infections, broken skin,<br>rosacea, acne, perioral dermatitis. Occlusive dressings<br>increase penetration into keratinized lesions.<br>Treat secondary infection with an appropriate antimicrobial.||**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Avoid unless potential benef t<br>greater than risk; consider alternatives such as paracetamol<br>or opioids; regular use in third trimester may cause closure<br>of fetal ductus arteriosus_in utero_, possibly persistent<br>pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, delayed onset and<br>increased duration of labour.<br>**Use with caution:**/;In renal and hepatic disorders,<br>hypersensitivity, and in the elderly. Ibuprofen can reduce<br>the antiplatelet activity of low-dose aspirin and potentially<br>reduce or negate the cardioprotective effect.<br>**Counselling:**Take with or after food.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Exacerbation of local<br>infection, contact dermatitis, perioral dermatitis|**Common:**Eye irritation|**Common:**Dyspepsia, nausea, diarrhoea; GI<br>ulceration and haemorrhage, raised liver<br>enzymes, headache, dizziness, salt and f uid<br>retention, hypertension<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Heart failure; hypersensitivity<br>reactions; bronchospasm; renal failure; rarely,<br>hepatic failure; erythaema multiforme, toxic<br>dermal necrolysis; oesophageal ulceration,<br>hyperkalaemia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Cream: 1%_**<br>Topical: Apply a small quantity<br>to the affected area 1–2 times<br>daily until improvement occurs.|**_Drops: 0.5%_**<br>Instill frequently (e.g. hourly) for<br>adequate relief.|**_Tablet: 200 mg, 400 mg_**<br>200–400 mg 3–4 times daily.<br>Maximum 2.4 g daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Hydrocortisone cream**<br>Severe inf ammatory skin<br>conditions including papular<br>lesions; eosinophilic<br>folliculitis; pityrosporum<br>folliculitis; papular urticaria;<br>eczema; contact, atopic<br>dermatitis; numular eczema;<br>seborrhoeic dermatitis;<br>psoriasis (10.2)|**Hydroxypropyl**<br>**methylcellulose (tears**<br>**naturale)**<br>Chronic soreness of the<br>eyes associated with<br>reduced or abnormal tear<br>secretion; acute viral<br>conjunctivitis (10.12)|**Ibuprofen**<br>Mild to moderate pain<br>(20.2, 20.4); musculoskeletal<br>disorders; arthritis (10.13);<br>dysmenorrhoea (10.15);<br>fever (10.1); erythema<br>nodosum (10.2)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**407** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Generally accepted as safe (insulin requirements<br>should be assessed in each trimester)<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended doses<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, hepatic impairment,<br>hypokalaemia|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe in pregnancy.<br>**Use with caution:**In prostatic hypertrophy. Medical<br>supervision with f rst dose due to risk of paradoxical<br>bronchospasm.<br>**Counselling:**Do not use for immediate relief of symptoms.|**Administration:**Desirable to give also pyridoxine 10 mg daily<br>to prevent peripheral neuropathy in PLHIV.<br>Defer IPT in the presence of active hepatitis (acute or<br>chronic), regular and heavy alcohol consumption, and<br>symptoms of peripheral neuropathy.<br>Stop INH if jaundice or skin rash with or without itching. Stop<br>IPT, and start treatment regimen if active TB develops.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Hypoglycaemia, weight gain;<br>hypersensitivity reactions; hypokalaemia,<br>transient oedema; local reactions including<br>erythema, itching, lipodystrophy, lipoatrophy|**Common:**Dry mouth, throat irritation<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Constipation, tachycardia,<br>atrial f brillation, urinary retention, acute angle-<br>closure glaucoma|**Common:**Burning, numbness or tingling<br>sensation in hands and feet; drowsiness;<br>anorexia, nausea, abdominal pain; jaundice<br>(other causes excluded), hepatitis; skin rash<br>with or without itching (rarely, but can be<br>serious, e.g. Stevens-Johnson syndrome)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 40 IU/ml in 10 ml vial;_**<br>**_100 IU/ml in 10 ml vial_**<br>See Section 3.4.1<br>IV: 10–15 units in 50 ml D50<br>(50% dextrose water) infused<br>over 2 hours THEN dextrose<br>infusion + regular blood glucose<br>monitoring<br>See Table in 3.8.1|**_Inhalation (aerosol): 20 mcg /_**<br>**_metered dose_**<br>20 to 40 mcg 4 times daily<br>(2 puffs) AND inhaled<br>salbutamol|**Tablet: 300 mg**<br>Oral: INH 300 mg daily for at<br>least 6 months is recommended,<br>and up to 36 months in HIV-<br>prevalent settings with a high<br>prevalence and transmission of<br>TB AND pyridoxine|
|**D rug Indication**|**Insulin (soluble)**<br>Diabetic ketoacidosis (3.4.1)<br>Hyperkalaemia (5.2.2)<br>Overdose of calcium-<br>channel blockers and<br>beta- blockers, given in<br>combination with dextrose<br>(3.8.1)<br>(**Other indications:**Diabetes<br>mellitus type I; type II after<br>failing oral therapy)|**Ipratropium bromide**<br>Acute wheezing (QC p. 17);<br>COPD, moderate (10.6)|**Isoniazid (INH) (H)**<br>TB prevention for PLHIV<br>(13.3)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**408** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use with caution. Consider alternatives where<br>possible; use minimum effective dose if required in acute<br>situation.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, if benef t is greater than<br>risk; monitor infant for side-effects.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to nitrates, hypotension,<br>hypovolaemia, hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy,<br>aortic stenosis, cardiac tamponade, constrictive pericarditis,<br>mitral stenosis; marked anaemia, head trauma, cerebral<br>haemorrhage, angle-closure glaucoma<br>**Use with caution:**In severe hepatic or renal impairment,<br>hypothyroidism, malnutrition, hypothermia, elderly, or recent<br>MI.<br>Prone to multiple signif cant drug interaction through CYP3A4<br>enzyme.|**Pregnancy:**Contraindicated in f rst trimester; use in second<br>or third trimesters only if drug of choice and no alternatives.<br>Breastfeeding: Not recommended – effects unknown. Use<br>f uconazole if available and indicated.<br>**Use with caution:**In patients with heart failure or risk factors<br>for heart failure, pre-existing hearing loss, hypersensitivity to<br>other azoles.<br>Monitor liver enzymes in patients with liver disease.<br>**Administration:**Prone to multiple signif cant drug<br>interactions (many leading to severe cardiovascular<br>compromise). (An alternative for candida oesophagitis is the<br>itraconazole solution).<br>**Counselling:**Take capsule with food for best absorption (oral<br>solution should be taken on empty stomach).<br>Do not take antacids within 2 hours of taking this medicine.<br>Tell your doctor if you feel unusually tired or have loss of<br>appetite, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or dark urine.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Throbbing headache, f ushing,<br>dizziness, fainting, postural hypotension,<br>tachycardia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Paradoxical bradycardia|**Common:**Dyspepsia, anorexia, fatigue, itch<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea, vomiting, abdominal<br>pain, diarrhoea, constipation, jaundice,<br>hepatitis; heart failure, pulmonary oedema,<br>headache, dizziness, peripheral neuropathy<br>(discontinue<br>treatment); menstrual disorders; hypokalaemia,<br>rash, pruritus, Stevens-Johnson syndrome,<br>alopecia<br>Potentially life-threatening hepatotoxicity<br>reported very rarely; discontinue if signs of<br>hepatitis develop.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet (sublingual): 5 mg_**<br>SL: 5 mg sublingual, repeat in<br>10–15 minutes, not to exceed<br>10 mg every 2–3 hours|**_Capsule: 100 mg;_**<br>**_Oral solution 10 mg/ml_**<br>Oral: 100–200 mg twice daily  for<br>10–14 days (may be increased to<br>a maximum of 400 mg daily)<br>200 mg 3 times daily for 3 days.<br>THEN 200 mg twice daily for<br>6–12 months<br>200 mg twice daily for 8weeks<br>THEN, in PLHIV, 200 mg daily<br>until 6 months after CD4> 100<br>Amphotericin B for 14 days THEN<br>itraconazole 200 mg daily for 10<br>weeks, continuing in PLHIV until<br>6 months after CD4>100<br>Pulsed monthly treatment for<br>3 months|
|**D rug Indication**|**Isosorbide dinitrate**<br>Pulmonary oedema with<br>severe hypertension (3.2.5)|**Itraconazole**<br>Candidal oesophagitis when<br>f uconazole not available<br>(11.4)<br>Histoplasmosis (11.16)<br>Penicilliosis, mild disease<br>(11.29)<br>Severe disseminated<br>penicilliosis (11.29)<br>Pityriasis versicolor,<br>recurrence (10.2.7)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**409** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Delay treatment until after delivery<br>and infant is 1 week old.<br>**Administration/counselling:**Avoid food or alcohol for at least<br>2 hours before and after a dose.<br>**Contraindications:**Do not give ivermectin for onchocerciasis<br>in loaiasis co-endemic areas.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Strongyloidiasis: Diarrhoea, dizziness,<br>nausea; mild ocular irritation; somnolence<br>**Onchocerciasis:**Mild Mazzotti reaction<br>within 3 days of treatment (fever, headache,<br>cough, pruritus, conjunctivitis, arthralgia,<br>lymphadenopathy, diarrhoea)<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Cutaneous or systemic<br>reactions|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 3 mg, 6 mg_**<br>200 mcg/kg as a single dose<br>OR 200 mcg/kg daily for 2 days<br>and then maintenance therapy 6<br>mg monthly<br>200–400 mcg/kg as a single dose<br>AND albendazole 400 mg twice<br>daily for 2 weeks<br>150 mcg/kg as a single dose<br>every 6 or 12 months<br>200 mcg/kg as a single dose and<br>repeat in 2 weeks.<br>Combine ivermectin with topical<br>scabicide (benzyl benzoate or<br>permethrin)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Ivermectin**<br>Strongyloidiasis (11.36)<br>Filariasis (11.12)<br>Onchocerciasis (in non Loa<br>Loa endemic areas) (11.28)<br>Scabies (10.2.4)<br>Norwegian (crusted)<br>scabies (10.2.4)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**410** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Safe to use<br>**Breastfeeding:**Limited data; avoid use.<br>**Contraindications:**Where elevation of blood pressure<br>would constitute a serious hazard, including eclampsia or<br>pre-eclampsia, severe coronary or myocardial disease,<br>cerebrovascular accident or cerebral trauma<br>**Use with caution:**In increased cerebrospinal f uid pressure,<br>predisposition to hallucinations or nightmares.<br>Ketamine has abuse potential and can itself cause<br>dependence.<br>**Administration:**For intravenous injection, dilute 100 mg/ml<br>strength to a concentration of not more than 50 mg/ml with<br>dextrose 5% or sodium chloride 0.9% or water.<br>Give IV slowly; rapid administration may result in respiratory<br>depression and enhanced hypertensive response.<br>Emergence reactions can be eliminated by co-administrating<br>benzodiazepine such as diazepam or midazolam and by<br>minimizing stimulation during the recovery period.|**Pregnancy:**No evidence of harm<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use caution.<br>**Use with caution:**In patients with electrolyte imbalance,<br>diabetes mellitus; solution contains galactose and lactose.<br>**Administration:**May mix with fruit juice, water, or milk. Onset<br>of action is 1–3 days.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Raised BP and pulse rate, increased<br>muscle tone (sometimes tonic-clonic and<br>resembling seizures), lacrimation, nausea,<br>vomiting, nystagmus, raised intracranial<br>pressure, diplopia; emergence reactions (may<br>occur after recovery and up to 24 hours), which<br>vary in severity between pleasant dream-<br>like states to vivid imagery, hallucinations,<br>nightmares and emergence delirium<br>(often consisting of dissociative or f oating<br>sensations), confusion, excitement, irrational<br>behaviour<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Raised intraocular pressure,<br>arrhythmias, hypotension, bradycardia,<br>laryngospasm; anxiety, insomnia; increased<br>salivation, apnoea, rashes, injection-site<br>reactions, anaphylaxis|**Common:**Flatulence, abdominal discomfort,<br>cramps<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Diarrhoea, dehydration,<br>hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 50 mg, 100 mg (as_**<br>**_hydrochloride) /ml in 10 ml vial_**<br>IV: 1–2 mg/kg IV over 2 minutes<br>May repeat 0.5 mg/kg IV every<br>10 minutes as needed OR IM:<br>4 mg/kg|**_Lactulose solution: 3.1–3.7_**<br>**_g/5 ml_**<br>20–30 g (30–45 ml) 3–4 times<br>daily; adjust dose every 1–2<br>days to produce 2–3 soft stools<br>daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Ketamine**<br>Sedation during short<br>procedures; induction for<br>intubation (QC p. 28 and 31)|**Lactulose**<br>Hepatic encephalopathy in<br>cirrhosis (10.9.2)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**411** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**No harm to fetus if pregnancy should occur<br>Breastfeeding: Unknown safety in breastfeeding but duration<br>of use of ECPs is brief.<br>**Administration:**The duration of use of ECP is brief; thus<br>less clinical impact is expected in severe cardiovascular<br>complications, angina, migraine, severe liver disease.<br>**Counselling:**If vomiting occurs within 2 hours after taking<br>levonorgestrel, a replacement dose should be taken. An<br>antiemetic can be taken one-half to one hour before the<br>replacement dose.<br>No protection against STI/HIV|**Pregnancy:**Avoid in third trimester<br>**Breastfeeding:**Amount too small to be harmful<br>**Contraindications:**Adjacent skin infection, inf amed skin,<br>severe anaemia, heart disease<br>**Use with caution:**In CHF (lower dosage) and following<br>cardiac surgery, bradycardia, hepatic impairment, severe<br>respiratory depression, and in the elderly|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, breast tenderness,<br>headache, dizziness, abdominal pain<br>Irregular vaginal bleeding for 1–2 days; next<br>menstrual bleeding starts earlier or later than<br>expected|**Common:**Dizziness, paraesthesia, drowsiness,<br>confusion; apnoea, respiratory  depression;<br>coma, seizures; hypotension, arrhythmias, heart,<br>block, bradycardia (may lead to cardiac arrest);<br>nystagmus (early sign of overdose)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: .75 mg; 1.5 mg_**<br>1.5 mg taken as a single dose<br>within 120 hours of unprotected<br>sex, sexual assault OR 0.75 mg<br>followed by another dose of 0.75<br>mg 12 hours later|**_Injection: 1%, 2% in vial_**<br>**_Topical: 2–4%_**<br>Local inf ltration and nerve<br>block, using 0.5% solution, up to<br>250 mg (50 ml) in adults<br>Local inf ltration and nerve<br>block, using 1% solution, up to<br>250 mg (25 ml) in adults<br>With epinephrine: 0.5% solution<br>up to 400 mg (80 ml) or 1%<br>solution up to 400 mg (40 ml)<br>in adults|
|**D rug Indication**|**Levonorgestrel**<br>For emergency<br>contraception (ECP) (14.5.2)|**Lidocaine**<br>(with or without<br>epinephrine)<br>Local anaesthesia<br>(7.1.2); nerve block (20.3)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Arrhythmias; spinal and<br>other anaesthesia)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**412** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Safe for short-term use in third trimester<br>(neonatal respiratory depression if excessive dose)<br>**Breastfeeding:**Mother treated with parenteral magnesium<br>for pre-eclampsia can breastfeed.<br>**Contraindications:**Bradycardia or AV block, pre-existing<br>hypermagnesaemia, renal insuff ciency/failure<br>**Use with caution:**In myasthenia gravis, liver or renal<br>impairment<br>**Administration:**For intravenous injection, dilute 1 part of<br>magnesium sulfate injection 50% with at least 1.5 parts of<br>water for injection.<br>For intramuscular injection, mix magnesium sulfate injection<br>50% with 1 ml lidocaine injection 2%.<br>Monitor urine output. Before giving next dose, ensure that:<br>• knee jerk is present<br>• urine output >100 ml/4 hours<br>• respiratory rate >16/minute.<br>Otherwise, do not give magnesium and consider calcium<br>gluconate for toxicity.<br>Note: Magnesium sulfate 1 g is approximately equivalent to<br>Mg 4 mmol.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, thirst, f ushing<br>of skin, hypotension, arrhythmias, coma,<br>respiratory depression, drowsiness, confusion,<br>loss of tendon ref exes, muscle weakness|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 500 mg/ml in 2 ml_**<br>**_ampoule; 500 mg/ml in 10 ml_**<br>**_ampoule (50%)_**<br>See instructions in QC p. 28.<br>IV: 2 g over 20 minutes<br>IV: 1 g every 6 hours<br>IM: 5 g OR IV: 75 mg/kg loading<br>dose THEN 2–3 g hourly as<br>needed until spasms controlled<br>(with or without diazepam)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Magnesium sulfate**<br>Prevention of seizures in<br>severe pre-eclampsia;<br>eclampsia convulsions and<br>prevention of recurrence<br>(QC p. 28)<br>Severe bronchospasm not<br>responding to salbutamol<br>(3.2.4); torsade de pointes/<br>VF/ pulseless VT from<br>toxicity due to quinine (3.8.1)<br>Aluminium/zinc phosphide<br>toxicity (3.8.1)<br>Tetanus (11.39)|

**413** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Avoid use; permethrin preferred.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Safe to use<br>**Counselling:**Avoid contact with eyes. Do not use on broken<br>or secondarily infected skin. Use lotion no more than once a<br>week for 3 consecutive weeks.|**Pregnancy:**Contraindicated in f rst trimester; consider<br>alternatives before using in second or third trimester.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used; 2–10% of oral dose absorbed,<br>and some excretion into breast milk expected.<br>**Counselling:**Take dose between meals.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Skin irritation, contact<br>dermatitis, allergy|**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain or cramps; headache, dizziness;<br>hypersensitivity reaction; with high doses;<br>increased liver enzymes; alopecia; bone marrow<br>depression|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_0.5% in an aqueous basis_**<br>Rub 0.5% preparation into<br>dry hair and scalp, allow to<br>dry naturally; THEN remove<br>by washing after 12 hours.<br>THENRepeat application after<br>7 days.<br>Apply 0.5% aqueous preparation<br>over whole body, allow to dry<br>naturally, THEN wash off after<br>12 hours or overnight. THEN<br>repeat application after 7 days|**_Tablet: 500 mg, 100 mg_**<br>500 mg orally in a single dose<br>OR 100 mg twice daily for 3 days<br>(Repeat after 3–4 weeks if eggs<br>persist in stool)<br>500 mg as a single dose every 6<br>months as prophylaxis<br>500 mg twice daily for 5 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Malathion**<br>Head lice<br>(pediculosis capitis) (10.2.8)<br>Crab lice (pediculosis pubis)<br>(10.2.8)|**Mebendazole**<br>Hookworm (10.18); ascaris<br>(10.7)<br>Ascaris, hookworm<br>prophylaxis every 6 months<br>in adolescent girls and<br>women of childbearing age<br>(19.1)<br>Persistent diarrhoea in<br>immunocompromised<br>patients (10.7d.2)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Echinocococcus infections<br>prior to surgery or not<br>amenable to surgery,<br>nematode infections<br>including enterobiasis,<br>trichuriasis, capillariasis)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**414** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Uncertain safety<br>**Breastfeeding:**Contraindicated<br>**Contraindications:**Severe cardiac, liver and kidney<br>disorders. Use local therapy if lesion is close to the eyes,<br>multiple (≥3), large (>3 cm diameter), sporotrichoid forms,<br>on the joint, super-infected, or produced by L. brazilensis,<br>L. guyanensis, or L. tropica.<br>**Use with caution:**The risk of serious, even fatal, toxicity<br>is increased in patients who concomitantly present with:<br>cardiac disease (particularly arrhythmia), renal failure, liver<br>disease, severe malnutrition, severely impaired general<br>condition, advanced HIV infection; pregnancy.<br>**Administration:**If any of the above cautions is present,<br>provide protein-rich diet throughout treatment and correct<br>iron and other nutritional def ciencies. Monitor cardiac, renal<br>and hepatic function.<br>Successful treatment of mucocutaneous leishmaniasis<br>may induce severe inf ammation around lesions (may be<br>life-threatening if pharyngeal or tracheal involvement); may<br>require corticosteroids.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain,<br>anorexia; ECG changes (possibly requiring dose<br>reduction or withdrawal); cough; arthralgia,<br>myalgia; elevated liver enzymes,jaundice, renal<br>function impairment; lethargy|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 30%, equivalent to_**<br>**_approximately 8.1% antimony in_**<br>**_5 ml ampoule_**<br>See table in Section 11.20.2.<br>IM/IV: 20 mg/kg every 3 or 4<br>weeks<br>Intralesional injection: 1 to 5<br>intralesional injections, every<br>few days or weekly, with or<br>without cryotherapy<br>IV/IM: 20 mg/kg for 21 days<br>IM: 20 mg/kg daily until slit-skin<br>smears are negative and for<br>at least 4 weeks thereafter<br>THEN, if inadequate response,<br>10–15 mg/kg every 12 hours<br>for same period if inadequate<br>response.<br>Repeat for at least twice as long<br>if relapse. (If unresponsive<br>to this treatment, treat with<br>pentamidine or amphotericin B.)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Meglumine antimoniate**<br>Visceral leishmaniasis<br>(11.20.2)<br>Secondary prophylaxis<br>of leishmaniasis in HIV-<br>infected patient (11.20.3)<br>Cutaneous leishmaniasis,<br>local treatment (11.20.1)<br>Cutaneous leishmaniasis,<br>systemic treatment (11.20.1)<br>Mucocutaneous<br>leishmaniasis<br>(L. braziliensis) (11.20.1)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**415** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Contraindicated in pregnancy<br>**Use with caution:**Hospitalization and close medical<br>supervision (intensive care) required during treatment.<br>Suspend treatment if reactive encephalopathy. Treat<br>intercurrent infections such as pneumonia and malaria<br>before treatment with melarsoprol. Use with caution in<br>malnutrition (if possible, correct with a protein-rich diet);<br>G6PD def ciency; leprosy (may precipitate erythema<br>nodosum).<br>**Administration:**Avoid extravasation–injection is very<br>irritating. Patients should be supine and fast for at least 5<br>hours after injection.|**Pregnancy:**May be used; not associated with birth defects;<br>caution in third trimester as chronic use is associated with<br>neonatal opioid withdrawal symptoms.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Monitor adverse effects such as sedation<br>(no adverse effects reported with 20 mg/day or less); infant<br>withdrawal reported with sudden cessation.<br>**Contraindications:**Acute bronchial asthma or hypercarbia,<br>respiratory depression in absence of appropriate airway<br>equipment, paralytic ileus<br>**Do not give to patients showing signs of intoxication from**<br>**alcohol or depressant drugs (such as diazepam).**<br>**Use with caution:**In patients with renal or hepatic<br>impairment, hypothyroidism, convulsive disorders, decreased<br>respiratory reserve as in asthma, hypotension, elderly,<br>prostatic hyperplasia, adrenal insuff ciency, head trauma.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions.<br>Dose adjustment recommended if severe renal and/or<br>hepatic impairment.<br>**Counselling:**This medication may impair your ability to<br>perform skilled tasks such as operating machinery and driving.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Jarisch-Herxheimer-like reaction<br>(chills; fever; general feeling of illness or<br>discomfort); headache; rigidity; sweating,<br>peripheral neuropathy, reactive encephalopathy<br>in 5-10% patients.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**myocardial damage,<br>hypertension, hypersensitivity, blood dyscrasias<br>such as agranulocytosis; hepatic and renal<br>dysfunction.|**Common:**Drowsiness, dizziness, respiratory<br>depression, QT interval prolongation,<br>dysmenorrhoea, hyperprolactinaemia, dry eyes,<br>dry mouth<br>**Rare:**torsade de pointes, hypothermia,<br>restlessness, raised intracranial pressure,<br>agitation/confusion (especially in the elderly),<br>urinary retention with high doses (especially in<br>the elderly)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 3.6% solution, 5_**<br>**_ml ampoule (180 mg active_**<br>**_compound)_**<br>2.2 mg/kg slow IV injection per<br>day for 7–10 days|**_Concentrate for oral_**<br>**_liquid: 5 mg/ml, 10 mg/ml_**<br>**_(hydrochloride)_**<br>**_Oral liquid: 5 mg/5ml, 10 mg/5ml_**<br>Oral: Initially 15–20 mg.<br>Gradually increase to 40 mg/day.<br>Taper off over 3–28 days. (See<br>Section 3.6.2.)<br>Oral: Initially 20 mg, with an<br>additional 10 mg after 4 hours.<br>If tolerance is low or uncertain,<br>start with a dose of 10 mg (see<br>Section 17.4).<br>(Optimal dosing range is<br>60–120 mg.)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Melarsoprol**<br>Trypanosomiasis,<br>meningoencephalitic stage<br>(11.41)|**Methadone**<br>Acute opioid withdrawal<br>(3.6.2)<br>Opioid substitution therapy<br>(OST) (17.4)|

**416** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Uncertain safety in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding. Use with caution.<br>**Contraindications:**Severe renal impairment,<br>methaemoglobinaemia due to chlorate or induced by sodium<br>nitrite in treatment of cyanide poisoning, G6PD def ciency<br>(may cause haemolytic anaemia)<br>**Use with caution:**Monitor blood methaemoglobin throughout<br>treatment|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for side-effects)<br>**Contraindications:**Gastrointestinal obstruction (often used<br>to empty the stomach of blood prior to EGD); within 3–4<br>days after gastrointestinal surgery, convulsive disorders,<br>phaeochromocytoma<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly, children, and young adults;<br>hepatic or renal impairment; Parkinson´s disease, epilepsy,<br>depression, porphyria.<br>May mask underlying disorders such as cerebral irritation.<br>Renal dosage adjustment required.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain,<br>chest pain, headache, dizziness, confusion,<br>profuse sweating, hypertension, hypotension,<br>haemolytic anaemia (in G6PD def ciency);<br>methaemoglobinaemia with high dosage; bluish<br>skin discoloration; blue saliva, urine, and faeces|**Common:**Drowsiness, headache restlessness,<br>dizziness<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Extrapyramidal symptoms<br>(especially children/young adults),<br>hyperprolactinaemia, depression, diarrhoea,<br>hypotension, hypertension, neuroleptic<br>malignant syndrome (rare), rash, cardiac<br>conduction abnormalities following IV<br>administration (rare)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 10 mg/ml in 10 ml_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>IV (loading dose): 2 mg/kg over<br>5 minutes THEN further dose of<br>1 mg/kg if no improvement. See<br>Section 3.8.1 for for monitoring<br>and further dosing.|**_Tablet: 10 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 5 mg (hydrochloride)/_**<br>**_ml in 2 ml ampoule_**<br>Oral/IV: 10 mg every 8 hours<br>15–19 years (<60 kg):<br>5 mg 3 times daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Methylthioninium chloride**<br>**(methylene blue)**<br>Acute methaemo-<br>globinaemia (propanil<br>poisoning) (3.8.1)|**Metoclopramide**<br>Nausea, vomiting<br>(10.7.3, 14.1.11)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used if drug of choice. Use has not been<br>associated with increased risk of adverse outcomes.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at usual doses, but avoid high<br>single-dose therapy or else withhold feeds for<br>12–24 hours; monitor infant for side-effects; may cause<br>temporary changes to milk taste.<br>**Contraindications:**Chronic alcohol dependence – disulf ram-<br>like reaction with alcohol occurs.<br>**Precautions:**Hepatic disease.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions through CYP enzyme<br>system. Check for interactions with current and new<br>medications.<br>**Administration:**Tablets should be swallowed whole with<br>water, with or after food.<br>IV: Give over 15–30 minutes.<br>**Counselling:**Take tablets with food to reduce stomach upset.<br>This medicine may make you feel dizzy or confused. Avoid<br>driving if you are affected.<br>Avoid alcohol during treatment, and for 24 hours after<br>stopping the drug, to prevent nausea, vomiting, f ushing,<br>headache, and palpitation.<br>Stop the medicine and inform your doctor if you have any<br>numbness, tingling, pain, or weakness in hands or feet|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>unpleasant metallic taste, dizziness, headache<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Furred tongue, glossitis<br>stomatitis paraesthesia, increased liver<br>enzymes, blood disorders, myalgia/arthralgia,<br>peripheral neuropathy, epileptiform seizures,<br>disulf ram-like reaction, bone marrow<br>depression, alopecia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 200 mg, 500 mg_**<br>**_Suppositories: 0.5 g, 1 g_**<br>**_Gel Injection: 500 mg in_**<br>**_100 ml vial_**<br>500 mg twice daily for 5 –7 days<br>Oral: 400–500 mg twice daily<br>for 14 days AND ceftriaxone +<br>doxycycline or tetracycline<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily for<br>7 days AND cotrimoxazole<br>Oral: 400 mg every 8 hours for<br>7 days<br>Oral: 200 mg 3 times daily for<br>7–10 days<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily AND<br>phenoxymethyl penicillin or<br>amoxicillin<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily AND<br>amoxicillin<br>Oral: 500 mg 3 times daily for<br>10–14 days; IV form effective<br>if patient cannot take oral pills<br>(same dose)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Metronidazole**<br>Bacterial vaginosis (10.15.4)<br>Pelvic inf ammatory disease<br>(10.15.5)<br>Persistent or<br>chronic diarrhoea in<br>immunocompromised<br>patients (empirical) (10.7d.3)<br>Leg ulcers/pressure sores<br>(10.2.10)<br>Acute necrotizing ulcerative<br>gingivitis or periodontitis<br>(10.17.6)<br>Dental abscess (10.17.5)<br>Peritonsillar abscess<br>(10.17.9)<br>Antibiotic-associated<br>colitis;_Clostridium diff cile_<br>colitis (10.7d.2)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended<br>doses in pregnancy and breastfeeding.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Remove excess cream from nipple areas<br>before feeding.<br>**Use with caution:**Contact with eyes and mucous<br>membranes should be avoided.<br>**Counselling:**Gum patch: Place rounded side of tablet on<br>upper gum above an incisor tooth and hold upper lip f rmly<br>over the gum for 30 seconds using a f nger. If tablet detaches<br>within 6 hours, replace with a new tablet. With each dose,<br>alternate sides of the gum.|**Pregnancy:**Avoid use if possible. High doses during late<br>pregnancy or labour may cause neonatal hypothermia,<br>hypotonia, respiratory depression.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Present in milk; manufacturer advises<br>avoiding breastfeeding for 24 hours after administration.<br>**Contraindications:**Marked neuromuscular respiratory<br>weakness including unstable myasthenia gravis; severe<br>respiratory depression; acute pulmonary insuff ciency<br>**Use with caution:**In cardiac disease; respiratory disease;<br>myasthenia gravis. Midazolam is associated with profound<br>sedation when high doses are given IV or when used with<br>certain other drugs.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Local irritation, contact<br>dermatitis (discontinue if sensitization occurs)|**Common:**hypotension, hiccup, cough<br>**Infrequent or rare:**GI disturbances, heart<br>rate changes, laryngospasm, bronchospasm,<br>respiratory depression and respiratory arrest<br>(particularly with high doses or on rapid<br>injection); drowsiness, confusion, ataxia,<br>amnesia, headache, euphoria, hallucinations,<br>paradoxical excitement and aggression (in<br>elderly); skin reactions, injection-site reactions,<br>anaphylaxis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Cream: 2% (nitrate)_**<br>**_Suppository: 200 mg_**<br>**_Oral gel_**<br>**_Gum patch_**<br>Topical: Apply cream twice daily<br>to clean, dry lesions for 5–7<br>days; continue at least 10 days<br>after the condition clears.<br>Vaginal suppository: 200 mg<br>inserted daily for 3 days<br>Oral: gel 60 mg 4 times daily for<br>7 days OR gum patch once daily<br>for 7 days|**_Injection: (as hydrochloride)_**<br>**_1 mg/ml_**<br>IV: 0.05 mg/kg over 2 minutes<br>just prior to giving ketamine<br>IV: 0.2 mg/kg<br>Infusion: 0.02–0.1 mg/kg/hour)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Miconazole**<br>Candida skin infection (11.4)<br>Vulvovaginal candidiasis<br>(10.15.4, 11.4)<br>Oral candidiasis (11.4)|**Midazolam**<br>Pretreatment to prevent<br>emergence reaction with<br>ketamine (QC p. 28)<br>Sedation for intubation if<br>not comatose (QC p. 31)<br>Sedation after intubation<br>(QC p. 34)|

**420** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Do not use in pregnancy or<br>during breastfeeding and assure adequate contraception<br>for women of childbearing age during treatment and for 3<br>months afterwards.|**Use with caution:**In conditions where hypotension might<br>precipitate severe complications (cardiovascular or cerebral<br>disease).<br>See other sources for contraindications to use for induction<br>of labour (such as placenta praevia, cephalopelvic distortion,<br>history caesarean section or major uterine surgery, etc.)|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, diarrhoea<br>– usually brief and resolve as treatment<br>continues. Occasionally severe, requiring<br>treatment interruption.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Skin allergy, elevated hepatis<br>transaminases, renal insuff ciency|**Common:**Diarrhoea (may occasionally be<br>severe and require withdrawal; reduced by<br>giving single doses not exceeding 200 mcg and<br>by avoiding magnesium-containing antacids);<br>abdominal pain, dyspepsia, f atulence, nausea,<br>vomiting; abnormal vaginal bleeding (including<br>intermenstrual bleeding, menorrhagia,<br>postmenopausal bleeding); rash; shivering and<br>fever.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Uterine rupture|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 10 mg, 50 mg_**<br>See table on treatment options<br>in Section 11.20.2.<br>Repeat 28-day courses|**_Oral tablet: 200 mcg_**<br>Oral: Single dose of 400 mcg<br>sublingually or 600 mcg by<br>mouth<br>SL: 800 mcg|

**421** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used if drug of choice. Not associated<br>with birth defects; high doses or prolonged use near term<br>can cause neonatal respiratory depression and withdrawal<br>symptoms.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use caution; monitor adverse effects such as<br>sedation; therapeutic concentrations in breastfeeding infant<br>may be reached with repeated dosing or long-term use.<br>**Contraindications:**Acute respiratory depression, acute<br>alcoholism, risk of paralytic ileus, raised intracranial<br>pressure or head injury (affects pupillary responses vital for<br>neurological assessment), injection in phaeochromocytoma<br>**Use with caution:**In renal and hepatic impairment,<br>dependence (severe withdrawal symptoms if withdrawn<br>abruptly), hypothyroidism, convulsive disorders, decreased<br>respiratory reserve and acute asthma, hypotension, prostatic<br>hypertrophy.<br>Reduce dose or avoid in elderly and debilitated.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions through CPY enzymes;<br>check for interactions with new or current medications.<br>**Administration:**SC dosing not suitable for oedematous<br>patients.<br>Sustained-release tablets should be taken at regular<br>intervals and not on an as-needed basis for episodic or<br>breakthrough pain.<br>Tablets can be crushed|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Safe in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding<br>**Use with caution:**Avoid contact with eyes and mouth.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting (particularly in initial<br>stages), constipation, dry mouth, drowsiness,<br>anorexia, spasm of urinary and biliary tract,<br>bradycardia,  tachycardia, palpitation, euphoria,<br>hallucinations, confusion, hypersensitivity<br>reaction, postural hypotension, dependence,<br>miosis. Larger doses produce respiratory<br>depression, hypotension, muscle rigidity.|**Infrequent or rare:**Local reactions including<br>urticaria, pruritus, burning sensation, rash|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 10 mg (morphine_**<br>**_hydrochloride or morphine_**<br>**_sulfate) in 1 ml ampoule_**<br>**_Oral liquid: 10 mg (morphine_**<br>**_hydrochloride or morphine_**<br>**_sulfate) in 5 ml_**<br>**_Tablet: 10 mg (morphine sulfate)_**<br>**_Tablet (prolonged-release): 10_**<br>**_mg, 30 mg, 60 mg (morphine_**<br>**_sulfate)_**<br>See dosing and precautions in<br>Section 20.4.<br>See dosing in Section 20.2.<br>IV (slow, 2 mg/min): 10 mg<br>THEN 5–10 mg if necessary<br>2.5 mg every 4–6 hours if not<br>on morphine for pain. If on<br>morphine for pain, increase<br>dose by 25%.|**_Cream (as mupirocin calcium):_**<br>**_2%_**<br>**_Ointment: 2%_**<br>Apply up to 3 times daily for up<br>to 10 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Morphine**<br>Severe acute pain (20.4)<br>Chronic pain (20.2)<br>Myocardial infarction<br>(QC p. 8)<br>Diff cult breathing in<br>terminal illness<br>(20.5)|**Mupirocin**<br>Impetigo (10.2.2); papular<br>urticaria with secondary<br>bacterial infection (10.2.3)|

**422** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use only if potential benef t is greater than risk;<br>may precipitate withdrawal in fetus of an opioid dependant<br>mother<br>**Breastfeeding:**Unknown safety; currently not recommended.<br>**Use with caution:**In physical dependence on opioids,<br>other situations where acute withdrawal syndrome may be<br>precipitated; cardiovascular disease<br>**Administration:**Naloxone effects last only 40 minutes.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not recommended during<br>pregnancy or breastfeeding<br>**Use with caution:**In individuals with hepatic and renal<br>impairment. If feasible, liver function tests should be<br>routinely carried out.<br>No ingestion of other opioid drugs for previous 5 days.<br>Will block effects of other opioid drugs (if analgesia required)|**Pregnancy:**Use with caution; no reports of malformation, but<br>neonatal myasthenia gravis and bradycardia are possible in<br>newborns exposed during pregnancy.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used in breastfeeding  (monitor infant<br>for adverse effects)<br>**Use with caution:**In asthma, urinary and intestinal surgery,<br>infection|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Hypotension, hypertension, ventricular<br>tachycardia and f brillation, cardiac arrest;<br>hyperventilation, dyspnoea, pulmonary oedema<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Agitation, excitement,<br>paraesthesia|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain,<br>diarrhoea, constipation, reduced appetite,<br>increased thirst; chest pain; anxiety, sleep<br>disorders, headache, reduced or increased<br>energy, irritability, emotional lability, dizziness;<br>chills; urinary retention; delayed ejaculation,<br>decreased potency; arthralgia, myalgia;<br>increased lacrimation; rash, increased sweating<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hepatic dysfunction; suicidal<br>ideation, speech disorders, hallucinations,<br>tremor; idiopathic thrombocytopenia|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, increased<br>salivation, diarrhoea, abdominal cramps.<br>Signs of overdosage: Bronchoconstriction,<br>increased bronchial secretions, lacrimation,<br>excessive sweating, involuntary defaecation<br>and micturition, miosis, nystagmus, bradycardia,<br>heart block, arrhythmias, hypotension, agitation,<br>excessive dreaming, weakness eventually<br>leading to fasciculation and paralysis|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 400 mcg/ml_**<br>**_(hydrochloride) in 1 ml ampoule_**<br>IV: 100 mcg in a single dose<br>OR IM: 400 mcg in a single dose<br>OR SC: 800 mcg in a single dose.<br>May repeat every 5 minutes up<br>to 3 times (maximum 10 mg)<br>If response (i.e. respiratory rate<br>>10/minute), start IV infusion:<br>0.4 mg/hour for 12 hours|**_Tablet: 50 mg_**<br>Start with 50 mg daily after<br>withdrawal from alcohol or<br>whilst still drinking some<br>alcohol.<br>Maintenance dose: 50–100 mg<br>daily.<br>Important that the patient has<br>not taken any opioid drugs for<br>previous 5 days|**_Injection: 500 mcg in 1 ml_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>**_Tablet: 15 mg_**<br>See Section 3.9|
|**D rug Indication**|**Naloxone**<br>Opioid overdose (QC p 18)|**Naltrexone**<br>Alcohol dependence (16.5)|**Neostigmine**<br>Snake bite neurotoxicity<br>(3.9)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Myasthenia gravis, to<br>reverse non-depolarizing<br>muscle relaxants; post-<br>operative non-obstructive<br>urinary retention)|

**423** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Uncertain safety in pregnancy, no human data;<br>animal studies have not shown evidence of birth defects.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Unknown safety in breastfeeding; not<br>currently recommended; use only if potential benef t is<br>greater than risk.<br>**Contraindications:**Psychiatric or neurological disorders<br>**Counselling:**Take after meals.|**Pregnancy:**May be used at recommended doses. Due to risk<br>of fetal haemolysis, avoid use at or near term in patients with<br>G6PD def ciency.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended dose except<br>with neonates and infants who are G6PD-def cient; monitor<br>infants for adverse effects.<br>**Contraindications:**Impaired renal function, G6PD def ciency,<br>porphyria<br>**Use with caution:**In pulmonary disorders or hepatic<br>impairment, neurological or allergic disorders, anaemia,<br>diabetes mellitus, elderly and debilitated, vitamin B and folate<br>def ciency<br>**Counselling:**Take with food or milk to reduce nausea<br>and improve absorption. This drug can make your urine a<br>brownish colour.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain, anorexia, central nervous<br>system alterations (sleep disturbances,<br>excitatory states, seizures, psychotic<br>behaviour), tremors, muscle weakness,<br>paraesthesia and polyneuritis|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, diarrhoea,<br>abdominal pain; allergic skin reactions;<br>headache<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hepatitis, jaundice; erythema<br>multiforme; pancreatitis; blood disorders; with<br>long-term use, pulmonary f brosis; possible<br>association with lupus erythematosus-like<br>syndrome; peripheral neuropathy|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 30 mg,120 mg, 250 mg_**<br>Oral: 8–10 mg/kg daily divided in<br>2 or 3  doses, for 60 consecutive<br>days|**_Tablet: 100 mg_**<br>Oral: 100 mg twice daily for 5<br>days|

**424** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Caution in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding. Use only when treatments with antacids and<br>H2 antagonists have failed.<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment, hepatic impairment,<br>and the elderly.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions.<br>Do not administer in combination with clopidogrel.<br>**Counselling:**Swallow the tablet whole. Do not crush or<br>chew it.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**No increased risks found in<br>pregnancy or breastfeeding<br>**Administration:**Give IV doses≤8 mg over at least 5 minutes<br>and doses >8 mg over at least 15 minutes.<br>No dose adjustment in the elderly or if renal impairment. In<br>severe liver impairment, do not exceed maximum dose of 8<br>mg.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain,<br>f atulence, diarrhoea, constipation; headache.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Dry mouth, peripheral<br>oedema, dizziness, sleep disturbances, fatigue,<br>paraesthesia, arthralgia, myalgia, rash, pruritus,<br>taste disturbance, stomatitis, hepatitis, jaundice,<br>agitation, impotence, fever, depression,<br>hallucinations, confusion, gynaecomastia,<br>interstitial nephritis, hyponatraemia, blood<br>disorders (including leukopenia, leucocytosis,<br>pancytopenia, thrombocytopenia), visual<br>disturbances, sweating, photosensitivity,<br>alopecia, Stevens- Johnson syndrome, toxic<br>epidermal necrolysis.<br>By decreasing gastric acidity, proton pump<br>inhibitors may increase the risk of gastro-<br>intestinal infections (including C._diff cile_<br>infection).|**Common:**Constipation, headache, f ushing;<br>injection-site reactions; transient rise in hepatic<br>enzymes<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hiccups, hypotension,<br>bradycardia, chest pain, arrhythmias, movement<br>disorders, seizures; on IV administration, rarely,<br>dizziness, transient visual disturbances (very<br>rarely, transient blindness)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 20 mg_**<br>Oral: 20 mg daily for 4–8 weeks<br>Oral: 20 mg twice daily<br>AND clarithromycin +<br>amoxicillin|**_Tablet: 4 mg, 8 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 2 mg/ml in 2ml_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>**_Liquid: 4 mg in 5 ml_**<br>Oral: 4 mg every 12 hours-<br>increase to 8 mg if this dose not<br>effective.<br>IV: 8 mg over 15 minutes every<br>12 hours OR 1 mg/hour infused<br>continuously for up to 24 hours.<br>Oral: Up to 24 mg daily oral or IV|
|**D rug Indication**|**Omeprazole**<br>Gastritis/peptic ulcer<br>disease (PUD) (10.7a.2)<br>_Helicobacter pylori_gastritis<br>(10.7a.2)|**Ondansetron**<br>Hyperemesis gravidarum<br>(14.1.11); moderate nausea<br>or vomiting or after<br>chemotherapy for 1–2 days<br>(10.7c.3, 20.2)<br>Severe vomiting<br>(**Other indications:**Prior<br>to chemotherapy causing<br>severe vomiting)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**425** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Unknown safety in pregnancy<br>**Breastfeeding:**Not recommended until more known; use only<br>if potential benef t is greater than risk.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Not known to be harmful in<br>breastfeeding, as rapidly inactivated in GI tract<br>**Contraindications:**Hypertonic uterine contractions,<br>mechanical obstruction to delivery, fetal distress, any<br>condition where spontaneous labour or vaginal delivery<br>inadvisable.<br>Avoid prolonged administration in oxytocin-resistant<br>uterine inertia, severe pre-eclamptic toxaemia, severe<br>cardiovascular disease.<br>**Use with caution:**Monitor fetal heart rate and uterine<br>motility (discontinue immediately if uterine hyperactivity/fetal<br>distress).<br>To avoid water intoxication with hyponatraemia: (1) use<br>electrolyte-containing diluent (not glucose); (2) increase<br>oxytocin concentration to reduce f uid; (3) restrict f uid intake<br>by mouth; (4) monitor f uid and electrolytes.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain,<br>diarrhoea; headache; conjunctivitis<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Rash, hepatitis, arrhythmias,<br>neuropsychiatric disorders (in children and<br>adolescents), visual disturbances, Stevens-<br>Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, arrhythmia,<br>headache<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Disseminated intravascular<br>coagulation, rash; anaphylactoid reactions<br>(with dyspnoea, hypotension, or shock); uterine<br>spasm (may occur at low doses), uterine<br>hyperstimulation (usually with excessive<br>doses; may cause fetal distress, asphyxia, and<br>death, or may lead to hypertonicity, tetanic<br>contractions, soft-tissue damage or uterine<br>rupture); water intoxication and hyponatraemia<br>associated with high doses with large infusion<br>volumes of electrolyte-free f uid; in overdose,<br>placental abruption, amniotic f uid embolism|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 30 mg, 45 mg, 75 mg_**<br>**_Suspension: 12 mg/ml_**<br>>40 kg: 75 mg twice daily for<br>10 days<br>(Note: In severe illness may use<br>150 mg twice daily)|**_Injection: 10 IU in 1 ml ampoule_**<br>IM: 10 IU AND<br>Start IV f uids with 20 IU<br>oxytocin at 60 drops/minute<br>See QC p. 25.<br>Continue oxytocin at 20 IU at<br>20 drops/minute for at least 1<br>hour after bleeding stops.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Oseltamivir**<br>Pandemic H1N1;  patients<br>with severe inf uenza-like<br>illness or at risk for severe<br>ILI (QC p 20, 11.17)|**Oxytocin**<br>Treatment of postpartum<br>and post-abortion<br>haemorrhage<br>(QC pp. 25–26)<br>(Other indictions:<br>prevention of postpartum<br>haemorrhage- when<br>the anterior shoulder is<br>delivered or immediately<br>after delivery)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**426** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for side-effects)<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment, renal impairment,<br>alcohol dependence<br>**Counselling:**Do not exceed 4 grams in 24 hours.|**Pregnancy:**Unknown safety in pregnancy; no human data.<br>Use only if potential benef t is greater than risk.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Unknown safety in breastfeeding, but poorly<br>absorbed from GI tract so excretion into breast milk likely to<br>be minimal.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to aminoglycosides,<br>course of paromomycin treatment in preceding 3 months,<br>concurrent administration of nephrotoxic or ototoxic drugs<br>including aminoglycosides, renal impairment|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Increased transaminases<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Urticarial or erythematous<br>rash, blood disorders, liver damage following<br>overdosage|**Common:**Injection site reactions, elevated liver<br>enzymes<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Ototoxicity (reversible<br>at recommended dosage), nephrotoxicity,<br>neurotoxicity (numbness, skin tingling, muscle<br>twitching, convulsions; neuromuscular<br>blockage, respiratory paralysis reported<br>following high doses)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 250 mg, 500 mg_**<br>**_Dispersible tablets: 500 mg_**<br>**_Suppositories: 250 mg, 500 mg_**<br>Oral: 0.5–1 g every 4–6 hours<br>(max 4 g daily; max 2 g daily if<br>hepatic impairment, cirrhosis)<br>Rectally: 0.5–1 g every 4–6 hours|**_Injection: 750 mg base (11 mg_**<br>**_base = 15 mg paramomycin_**<br>**_sulfate)_**<br>**_Ointment: 15% paramomycin_**<br>**_+ 12% methyl benzethonium_**<br>**_chloride_**<br>Ointment twice daily for up to<br>20 days<br>See table in Section 11.20.2|
|**D rug Indication**|**Paracetamol**<br>**(acetaminophen)**<br>Mild to moderate pain (20.2,<br>20.4); fever (10.1)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Acute migraine attacks;<br>tension headache)|**Paromomycin**<br>Local treatment of<br>cutaneous leishmaniasis<br>(11.20.1)<br>Visceral leishmaniasis<br>caused by certain species|

**427** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Potentially fatal visceral leishmaniasis and PCP<br>pneumonia (cotrimoxazole is preferred) should be treated in<br>pregnancy.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Manufacturer advises avoiding unless<br>essential; not known to be harmful, as rapidly inactivated in<br>GI tract.<br>**Contraindications:**In severe renal impairment<br>**Administration:**Risk of severe hypotension following<br>administration. Establish baseline blood pressure and<br>administer with patient lying down; monitor blood pressure<br>closely during administration and at regular intervals until<br>treatment concluded.<br>Avoid direct intravenous injection whenever possible and<br>never give rapidly.<br>Reconstitute with 3–5 ml of water for injection; dilute further<br>to<br>50–250 ml with glucose 5% or normal saline; give over at<br>least 60 minutes.<br>IM injections should be deep and preferably given into the<br>buttock.<br>**Pentamidine is toxic**– protect health workers during<br>handling and administration.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, taste<br>disturbances; severe reactions, sometimes<br>fatal (hypotension, hypoglycaemia, pancreatitis,<br>arrhythmias); leukopenia, thrombocytopenia;<br>acute renal failure, hypocalcaemia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Azotaemia, abnormal<br>liver-function tests, anaemia, hyperkalaemia,<br>dizziness, syncope, f ushing, hyperglycaemia,<br>rash, Stevens-Johnson syndrome; on inhalation,<br>bronchoconstriction (may be prevented by prior<br>use of bronchodilators), cough, shortness of<br>breath; discomfort, pain, induration, abscess<br>formation, muscle necrosis at injection site|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Powder for injection: 200 mg,_**<br>**_300 mg (isetionate) in vial_**<br>IM/IV: 2–3 mg/kg once daily or<br>every second day for 4–7 doses<br>4 mg/kg (300 mg) every 3–4<br>weeks<br>Slow IV/deep IM: 4 mg/kg daily<br>for 5 days; then reduce dose<br>to 2 mg/kg daily to complete<br>21 days<br>IM (deep): 4 mg/kg daily for 7<br>consecutive days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Pentamidine**<br>Cutaneous leishmaniasis<br>(due to severe side-effects,<br>recommended only if no<br>other treatment available)<br>(11.20.1)<br>Leishmaniasis, secondary<br>prophylaxis in HIV-infected<br>patients (11.20.3)<br>Severe_Pneumocystis_<br>_jirovecii_(PCP) pneumonia,<br>treatment if not able to<br>tolerate or unresponsive to<br>cotrimoxazole (10.6.3)<br>Human African<br>trypanosomiasis (_T. b._<br>_gambiense_)- haemo-<br>lymphatic stage (11.41)|

**428** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used; systemic absorption expected to<br>be minimal.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used; systemic absorption expected<br>to be minimal, but avoid application to nipple areas (or<br>withhold breastfeeding during treatment).<br>**Contraindications:**Do not use on inf amed or broken skin.<br>**Counselling:**Avoid contact with eyes, mouth, and inside the<br>nose.<br>Itch may persist for 2–3 weeks after scabies treatment or<br>7–10 days after lice treatment. This may not indicate ongoing<br>infection.<br>Scabies: Remember to apply also between f ngers and toes,<br>under nails, in skin folds, navel, between the buttocks, and<br>in groin area.<br>If you wash your hands or any other treated parts of the body<br>during the treatment period, you should reapply the lotion to<br>the washed areas.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Temporary increase in itch, redness,<br>swelling that usually accompany lice or mite<br>infestation<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Local irritation, rash, oedema|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Cream: 5%_**<br>Topical: Apply cream over whole<br>body AND Wash off after<br>8–12 hours (if hands washed<br>with soap within 8 hours after<br>application, treat again)<br>THEN Repeat after 7 days as<br>necessary.<br>Apply to damp hair and leave for<br>10 minutes before washing off.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Permethrin**<br>Scabies (10.2.3); pediculosis<br>(body lice) (10.2.8)<br>Pediculosis capitis (head<br>lice) (10.2.8)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**429** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended; adverse effects on<br>neurobehavioural development have been reported.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use with caution in breastfeeding; avoid large<br>doses and monitor for adverse effects; may accumulate in<br>breast milk.<br>**Contraindications:**Porphyria, absence seizures<br>**Use with caution:**In the elderly, impaired renal or hepatic<br>function, respiratory depression<br>Avoid sudden withdrawal.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions through CYP enzymes;<br>check for interactions with all new and current medications.<br>**Counselling:**Take once daily at bedtime.<br>May cause drowsiness and affect your ability to drive or<br>operate machinery. Avoid these activities at least until you<br>know how this medicine affects you.<br>Do not stop taking this medicine suddenly without your<br>clinician’s advice. Avoid drinking alcohol, as the medicine<br>may increase the effects of alcohol.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Drowsiness, incoordination,<br>restlessness and confusion (in elderly),<br>impaired memory and cognition, hyperactivity<br>(particularly in the elderly), allergic skin<br>reactions, paradoxical excitement, sleep<br>disorders.<br>With IV: hypotension, respiratory depression,<br>laryngospasm<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hepatitis, cholestasis;<br>behavioural disturbances, nystagmus, irritability,<br>lethargy, depression, ataxia, hallucinations;<br>osteomalacia; megaloblastic anaemia (may<br>be treated with folic acid), agranulocytosis,<br>thrombocytopenia; allergic skin reactions; very<br>rarely Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic<br>epidermal necrolysis; status epilepticus (on<br>treatment withdrawal); Dupuytren’s contracture;<br>lymphadenopathy|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 200 mg/ml_**<br>**_(phenobarbital sodium)_**<br>**_Tablet: 15–100 mg_**<br>**_(phenobarbital)_**<br>Loading dose:<br>IV: 5–15 mg/kg bolus over 1 hour<br>Oral: Initiate at 60 mg daily; then<br>maintain at 60–180 mg daily.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Phenobarbital**<br>Status epilepticus (3.5)<br>Epilepsy (10.10c.2)|

**430** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for side-effects)<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to penicillins<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment.<br>Oral penicillin should not be used for the treatment of severe<br>infections.<br>**Counselling:**Take 1 hour before meals or on an empty<br>stomach.|

|**Adverse effects**|See benzylpenicillin.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 500 mg_**<br>Oral: 500 mg twice daily for<br>10 days<br>Oral: 500 mg every 6 hours for<br>7–10 days<br>Oral: 250 mg every 6 hours for<br>5 days<br>Oral: 500 mg twice daily<br>Oral: 500 mg every 6 hours for<br>5–10 days|

Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**431** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Risk of teratogenicity; use only if benef t<br>is greater than risk (provide adequate folic acid<br>supplementation to mother); monitor for neonatal bleeding if<br>vitamin K not given at birth.<br>Breastfeeding: May be used in breastfeeding; monitor for<br>sedation and decreased sucking; consider infant serum level<br>monitoring.<br>**Contraindications:**Porphyria. Avoid parenteral use in sinus<br>bradycardia, sinoatrial block, second- and third-degree heart<br>block, Adams-Stokes syndrome.<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic impairment, diabetes mellitus,<br>hypotension, heart failure.<br>Resuscitation facilities must be available for intravenous<br>administration.<br>**Administration:**Administer phenytoin IV in normal saline<br>and not in same line as diazepam. IV line should be running<br>well, as the drug is caustic and will cause local damage if it<br>extravasates.<br>Monitor blood counts. When decision is made to withdraw<br>treatment, do so preferably over 6 months at a rate not<br>greater than 25 mg each week or 100 mg each month.<br>**Counselling:**Seek immediate medical attention if you have<br>symptoms such as sore throat, rash, mouth ulcers, bruising,<br>or bleeding. This medication may impair ability to perform<br>skilled tasks such as operating machinery or driving; avoid<br>these activities at least until you know how this medicine<br>affects you.<br>Take with or after food.<br>Good dental hygiene can help to prevent gum enlargement.<br>Do not stop this medicine suddenly without your clinician’s<br>advice.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, constipation;<br>insomnia, transient nervousness, tremor,<br>paraesthesia, dizziness, headache, anorexia;<br>gingival hypertrophy and tenderness; rash<br>(discontinue; if mild, re-introduce cautiously but<br>discontinue immediately if recurrence), acne,<br>hirsutism, coarse facies<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hepatotoxicity, peripheral<br>neuropathy, dyskinesia, lymphadenopathy,<br>osteomalacia; blood disorders (including<br>megaloblastic anaemia (may be treated<br>with folic acid)), leukopenia (if severe,<br>progressive, or clinically apparent leukopenia<br>develops, withdraw drug, replacing with<br>suitable alternative), thrombocytopenia,<br>aplastic anaemia; polyarteritis nodosa, lupus<br>erythematosus, Stevens-Johnson syndrome,<br>toxic epidermal necrolysis; pneumonitis,<br>interstitial nephritis; with excessive dosage,<br>nystagmus, diplopia, slurred speech, ataxia,<br>confusion, hyperglycaemia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule: 25 mg, 50 mg, 100 mg_**<br>**_Injection: 50 mg/ml in, 5 ml vial_**<br>**_Tablet: 25 mg, 50 mg, 100 mg_**<br>**_Tablet (chewable): 50 mg_**<br>Oral: Start at 150–200 mg daily,<br>increase by increments of 25–30<br>mg to reach maintenance at<br>200–400 mg daily<br>Loading dose: IV (slowly over<br>60 ): 15 mg/kg in normal saline,<br>at a rate of not more than 50 mg/<br>minute (monitor BP and ECG).<br>Then oral maintenance dose<br>as above.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Phenytoin**<br>Generalized tonic-clonic<br>seizures; partial seizures<br>(10.10c.2)<br>Status epilepticus (3.5)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**432** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Contraindications:**Acute iritis, acute uveitis, anterior uveitis,<br>some forms of secondary glaucoma; acute inf ammation<br>of anterior segment. Use not advisable after angle-closure<br>surgery (risk of posterior synechiae).<br>**Use with caution:**In retinal disease, conjunctival or corneal<br>damage (monitor intraocular pressure in chronic open-angle<br>glaucoma and in long-term treatment); cardiac disease,<br>hypertension, asthma, peptic ulceration, urinary tract<br>obstruction, Parkinson’s disease. Withdraw treatment if<br>symptoms of systemic toxicity develop.<br>**Administration:**A darkly pigmented iris may require higher<br>concentration of the miotic or more frequent administration;<br>care should be taken to avoid overdosage.<br>**Counselling:**If you are using more than one type of eye drop,<br>put in pilocarpine drops last.<br>Causes diff culty with adapting to the dark and may cause<br>accommodation spasm.<br>Avoid skilled tasks, for example, operating machinery or<br>driving, until vision is clear.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Contraindicated in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding<br>**Use with caution:**Avoid use on large areas; very irritant to<br>eyes (keep away from face); avoid contact with normal skin,<br>mucus membranes, open wounds.<br>**Administration:**Must be applied by a trained health worker<br>**Counselling:**Avoid contact with face and other sensitive<br>areas.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Eye pain, blurred vision, ciliary spasm;<br>ciliary spasm leads to headache and brow ache,<br>which may be most severe in the initial 2–4<br>weeks of treatment.<br>I**nfrequent or rare:**Lacrimation, myopia,<br>conjunctival vascular congestion, superf cial<br>keratitis, vitreous haemorrhage, increased<br>pupillary block; lens opacities (following<br>prolonged use); rarely, systemic effects<br>including hypertension, tachycardia, bronchial<br>spasm, pulmonary oedema, salivation, sweating,<br>nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea|**Common:**Irritation, staining of the skin<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Systemic effects resulting<br>from cutaneous absorption include nausea,<br>vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhoea; also,<br>transient leukopenia and thrombocytopenia;<br>renal failure; delayed neurotoxicity including<br>visual and auditory hallucinations, delusions,<br>disorientation, confusion, delirium following<br>excessive application|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Drops: 2%, 4% (hydrochloride_**<br>**_or nitrate)_**<br>1 drop (2% or 4% solution) up to<br>4 times daily<br>1 drop (2% solution) every<br>10 minutes for 30–60 minutes;<br>then 1 drop every 1–3 hours until<br>intraocular pressure subsides|**_Solution: 10–25%_**<br>Apply carefully, avoiding<br>contact with normal tissue;<br>rinse off after 1–6 hours.<br>May be repeated at weekly<br>intervals but no more than 4<br>times in all.<br>Only a few warts should be<br>treated at any one time.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Pilocarpine eye drops**<br>Chronic open-angle<br>glaucoma (10.12.4)<br>Emergency treatment<br>of acute angle-closure<br>glaucoma (before surgery)<br>(10.12.2)<br>(**Other indications:**Ocular<br>hypertension;<br>to antagonize effects of<br>mydriasis and cycloplegia<br>following surgery<br>or ophthalmoscopic<br>examination)|**Podophyllum resin**<br>External anogenital warts;<br>plantar warts (10.2.3)|

**433** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Contraindications:**Avoid regular or prolonged use in patients<br>with thyroid disorders or those taking lithium.<br>**Administration:**Do not use on large open wounds: may<br>produce systemic adverse effects such as metabolic<br>acidosis, hypernatraemia, impairment of renal function.|**Pregnancy:**Unknown safety in pregnancy.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Not recommended until more known; use only<br>if potential benef t is greater than risk.<br>**Contraindications:**Ileus, GI haemorrhage, haemodynamic<br>instability, uncontrollable vomiting, bowel obstruction or<br>perforation, decreased consciousness with unprotected<br>airway.|**Use with caution:**Avoid occlusive dressings.<br>**Administration:**In exudative eczematous areas, treatment<br>should be stopped when the skin becomes dry.<br>Note: Potassium permanganate is sometimes supplied as an<br>aqueous stock solution of 1:1000 (0.1%) for dilution before<br>use. To be diluted 1 in 10 to provide a 0.01% (1 in 10 000)<br>solution.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Irritation of skin and mucous<br>membranes; may interfere with thyroid function<br>tests|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps,<br>bloating<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Aspiration pneumonia<br>Note: Risk of f uid and electrolyte imbalance if<br>solution is not correctly formulated|**Common:**Irritant to mucous membranes; skin<br>and fabrics can be stained brown.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Solution: 10%_**<br>Apply undiluted solution to the<br>skin area.<br>Apply undiluted solution to the<br>affected area twice daily.|**_Solution_**<br>Bowel irrigation: 2 litres per<br>hour for adults if tablets are<br>present beyond the stomach|**_Aqueous solution: 1:10 000_**<br>**_(0.01%)_**<br>Apply dressings soaked in a<br>1:10 000 solution to affected<br>area until superf cial crusts can<br>be gently separated.<br>Change dressings 2 or 3 times<br>daily.<br>Bathe severe weeping lesions<br>in a 1:10 000 solution every<br>8 hours.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Polyvidone iodine**<br>**(povidone–iodine)**<br>Skin disinfection (10.2)<br>Antiseptic for minor wounds<br>and burns (4)|**Polyethylene glycol**<br>**electrolyte solution**<br>**(osmotically balanced**<br>**mixture)**<br>Acute iron poisoning<br>and overdose with highly<br>toxic sustained-release<br>preparations, e.g. calcium<br>channel blockers (3.8.1)|**Potassium permanganate**<br>Wet dressings to assist<br>healing of suppurating<br>superf cial wounds, tropical<br>ulcers; pemphigus; tinea<br>pedis; infected eczema<br>(10.2)|

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**434** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**May be used for supplementation<br>in pregnancy and breastfeeding; monitor electrolytes to keep<br>maternal serum levels within normal range.<br>**Contraindications:**Severe renal impairment and plasma<br>potassium concentration above 5 mmol/l<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly, mild/moderate renal impairment,<br>history of peptic ulcer<br>**Administration:**Monitor potassium levels. Potassium salts<br>cause nausea and vomiting; therefore, poor compliance is<br>a major limitation to their effectiveness; where appropriate,<br>potassium-sparing diuretics are preferable.<br>**Counselling:**If you have severe nausea and vomiting, stop<br>the medicine and inform your clinician.|**Pregnancy:**Use not recommended in f rst trimester; consider<br>alternatives. Caution in second and third trimesters; use only<br>if treatment of choice.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used for single-day treatment during<br>breastfeeding<br>**Contraindications:**Ocular cysticercosis<br>**Counselling:**Take with food. Swallow with plenty of water<br>to prevent vomiting due to bitter taste. Tablet may be cut into<br>halves or quarters, but do not chew.<br>The medicine may make you feel drowsy or dizzy; if you are<br>affected, do not drive or operate machinery until 24 hours<br>after f nishing your course.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting (severe symptoms<br>may indicate obstruction of oesophagus or<br>small bowel), hyperkalaemia (especially in renal<br>insuff ciency), rapid infusion toxic to heart|Usually mild and transient with short course.<br>Many adverse effects result from death of the<br>parasite and are more severe with high parasite<br>burden.<br>**Common:**Dizziness (dose dependent),<br>headache, malaise, drowsiness; nausea,<br>vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, anorexia,<br>colic; reversible rises in hepatic transaminases;<br>rectal bleeding Infrequent or rare:<br>arrhythmia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Solution: 11.2% in 20 ml_**<br>**_ampoule (equiv to K+ 1.5 mmol/_**<br>**_ml, Cl− 1.5 mmol/ml); Oral_**<br>**_preparations_**<br>Oral: 20–50 mmol daily after<br>meals<br>Slow IV infusion: 20–40 mmol/l<br>in normal saline (not to exceed<br>10-20 mmol/hour)|**_Tablet: 600 mg_**<br>Oral: 40 mg/kg as a single dose|
|**D rug Indication**|**Potassium chloride (KCl)**<br>Hypokalaemia, mild to<br>moderate (5.2.2)<br>Hypokalaemia, severe<br>(5.2.2)|**Praziquantel**<br>Schistosomiasis (11.34)|
