**435** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used at the recommended doses;<br>caution in f rst trimester due to possibility of oral cleft; limited<br>transplacental transfer. Monitor blood glucose, especially in<br>diabetics.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended doses; amount<br>in milk low at doses up to 80 mg (monitor infant’s adrenal<br>function if dose is higher)<br>**Contraindications:**Systemic infection (unless life-<br>threatening or specif c antimicrobial therapy given). Avoid<br>live virus vaccines in those receiving immunosuppressive<br>doses.<br>**Use with caution:**In infections, hypertension, recent<br>myocardial infarction, congestive heart failure, renal<br>impairment, hepatic impairment, diabetes mellitus including<br>family history, osteoporosis, glaucoma including family<br>history, corneal perforation, severe affective disorder,<br>epilepsy, psoriasis, peptic ulcer, hypothyroidism, history<br>of steroid myopathy, and in the elderly; can activate or<br>exacerbate TB, amoebiasis, strongyloidiasis.<br>Prolonged treatment leads to adrenal suppression, which<br>persists for years after stopping treatment.<br>Cushingoid features are increasingly likely with doses above<br>7.5 mg daily.<br>Risk of chickenpox, measles, and activation of tuberculosis<br>increased.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|Overdosage or prolonged use can exaggerate<br>some of the normal physiological actions of<br>corticosteroids, leading to mineralocorticoid<br>and glucocorticoid side-effects.<br>_Mineralocorticoid side-effects:_Hypertension,<br>sodium and water retention, potassium and<br>calcium loss<br>**Common:**Dyspepsia, increased susceptibility<br>to infection (oral, vaginal, intertriginous<br>candidiasis), masking of signs of infected<br>acne, oedema, hypertension, hypokalaemia,<br>hyperglycaemia, weight gain, osteoporosis,<br>spontaneous fractures, increased appetite,<br>delayed wound healing, skin atrophy, growth<br>retardation in children, myopathy, muscle<br>weakness, wasting (particularly symptomatic on<br>drug withdrawal), fat redistribution (producing<br>cushingoid appearance), amenorrhoea,<br>psychosis. euphoria, depression, adrenal<br>suppression, bruising|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet; 5 mg, 25 mg_**<br>Oral: 40–60 mg<br>Oral: 40 mg twice daily for 5<br>days THEN 40 mg daily for<br>5 days THEN 20 mg daily for<br>11 days to complete 21 days of<br>treatment<br>Oral: 0.5 mg/kg daily (reassess<br>weekly; taper when patient<br>stable for 1 week)<br>See Section 11.21 for dosing.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Prednisolone**<br>Also see Section 8.2 steroid<br>equivalents table.<br>Moderate to life-threatening<br>wheezing (3.2.4)<br>_Pneumocystis jirovecii_<br>pneumonia (PCP<br>pneumonia) (10.6.3)<br>Management of severe<br>persistent asthma (10.6.4)<br>Type 2 lepra reaction (11.21)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**436** 

||||
|---|---|---|
||**Special groups/comments**|**Administration:**Monitor weight, blood pressure, f uid and<br>electrolyte balance, and blood glucose levels throughout<br>prolonged treatment.<br>The suppressive action of a corticosteroid on cortisol<br>secretion is least when it is given as a single dose in the<br>morning.<br>During corticosteroid withdrawal the dose may be<br>reduced rapidly down to physiological doses (equivalent<br>to prednisolone 7.5 mg daily) and then reduced more<br>slowly. Assessment of the disease may be needed during<br>withdrawal to ensure that relapse does not occur.<br>**Counselling:**Take with food to reduce stomach upset.<br>Tell your doctor immediately if you have any signs of<br>infection.<br>If you have been on this medicine for more than 3 weeks,<br>don’t stop the treatment suddenly.<br>Tell your doctor, dentist, or pharmacist that you are on<br>steroids before undergoing any new treatment.|
||**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Acute pancreatitis, peptic<br>and oesophageal ulceration, vertebral<br>compression fracture, aseptic necrosis of<br>the talus or femoral and humoral heads;<br>facial erythema, suppression of skin test<br>reactions, hyperhidrosis, skin bruising,<br>telangiectasia, myocardial rupture following<br>recent myocardial infarction, congestive heart<br>failure, leucocytosis, hypersensitivity reactions<br>(including anaphylaxis), thromboembolism,<br>malaise, hiccups, headache, vertigo.<br>_Glucocorticoid side-effects:_Diabetes and<br>osteoporosis can result in osteoporotic<br>fractures, particularly in the elderly.<br>CNS side-effects: Psychological dependence,<br>insomnia, aggravation of schizophrenia,<br>aggravation of epilepsy.<br>Ophthalmologic side-effects: Glaucoma,<br>papilloedema, posterior subcapsular cataracts,<br>corneal or scleral thinning, exacerbation of<br>ophthalmic viral or fungal disease, increased<br>intra-ocular pressure, exophthalmos|
||**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**Note on steroid dosing:**<br>Patients on long term steroid<br>therapy are at risk for a blunted<br>stress response in conditions<br>causing physiologic stress<br>(e.g., severe infection, trauma,<br>during surgery). As a result,<br>standard steroid doses may<br>need to be supplemented.<br>Patients on steroid replacement<br>for primary dysfunction of<br>the hypothalamus-pituitary-<br>adrenal axis HPA axis (for<br>example, Addison disease,<br>hypopituitarism) SHOULD<br>receive higher dose of<br>steroids in conditions causing<br>physiologic stress. A common<br>IV regimen is hydrocortisone<br>50-100 mg every 8 hours for 2<br>days.  Consult a specialist for<br>assistance on how to provide<br>this supplemental steroid<br>treatment.|
||**D rug Indication**|**Other indications:**<br>With antineoplastic drugs<br>for acute and chronic<br>leukaemias; lymphoma;<br>suppression of inf ammatory<br>and allergic reactions;<br>inf ammation of the eye;<br>myasthenia gravis)|
||||

**437** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Uncertain safety in pregnancy; use not<br>recommended.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use with caution; limited data. Monitor<br>infants for adverse effects (e.g. haemolysis, jaundice). Avoid<br>in neonates and infants who are G6PD-def cient.<br>**Contraindications:**Conditions that predispose to<br>granulocytopenia.<br>Monitor blood count.<br>Exclude G6PD def ciency before radical treatment for_P. vivax_<br>and_P. ovale_but not before single-dose gametocytocidal<br>treatment.<br>**Counselling:**Give with food if severe nausea/vomiting or<br>abdominal cramps occur.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in both<br>pregnancy and breastfeeding (monitor infant for side-<br>effects).<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to penicillins or if IV<br>administration needed<br>**Use with caution:**In renal failure.<br>Observe patient with syphilis or borreliosis for several hours<br>after treatment.<br>**Administration:**Give by deep IM injection. (Do not give IV.)<br>Can be used for daily outpatient treatment.<br>Probenecid is added to increase serum penicillin levels in<br>neurosyphilis.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, anorexia,<br>abdominal cramps; dizziness, headache<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Haemolytic anaemia<br>(frequently in G6PD def ciency; withdraw<br>treatment); methaemoglobinaemia (withdraw<br>treatment); haemoglobinuria; agranulocytosis,<br>granulocytopaenia, leukopenia|See benzylpencillin.<br>Pain and inf ammation at injection site;<br>Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (rigors, fever, and<br>hypotension) usually within several hours after<br>treatment of syphilis or borreliosis; probably due<br>to release of endotoxin.<br>Accidental intravascular administration may<br>result in anxiety, agitation, fear of death,<br>hallucinations. These usually resolve in<br>15–30 minutes and rarely last beyond 24 hours.|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 7.5 mg, 15 mg_**<br>Oral: 250 mcg/kg daily for 14<br>days OR 30 mg daily for 14 days|**_Powder for Injection: 1 g vial (1_**<br>**_million IU); 3 g vial (3 million IU)_**<br>IM: 2.4 g (2.4 million IU) daily<br>AND_oral probenecid_500 mg 4<br>times daily) for 10–14 days<br>0.6 g (600 000 IU) daily for 2–4<br>weeks<br>IM: 0.6 to 0.8 g (600 000 to<br>800 000 IU) as a single dose|
|**D rug Indication**|**Primaquine**<br>Radical treatment of P. vivax<br>and P. ovale malaria (after<br>standard chloroquine<br>therapy) (11.25.3)|**Procaine benzylpenicillin G**<br>Neurosyphilis (11.37)<br>Tropical ulcer (10.2.10)<br>Borreliosis (louse-borne<br>relapsing fever) if not able<br>to take orally (10.1)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Diphtheria; animal bites)|

**438** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use with caution. May cause intrauterine growth<br>restriction, neonatal hypoglycaemia, and bradycardia; risk<br>greater in severe hypertension.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Present in milk; safe in usual dosage; monitor<br>infant.<br>**Contraindications:**Asthma or history of obstructive airway<br>disease, uncontrolled heart failure, Prinzmetal’s angina,<br>marked bradycardia, hypotension, sick sinus syndrome,<br>second- or third-degree atrioventricular block, cardiogenic<br>shock, metabolic acidosis, severe peripheral arterial disease;<br>phaeochromocytoma<br>**Use with caution:**In f rst-degree atrioventricular block; renal<br>impairment; liver disease; portal hypertension; diabetes<br>mellitus; myasthenia gravis; history of hypersensitivity<br>**Administration:**When stopping treatment, reduce dosage<br>gradually over at least 2 weeks.<br>**Counselling:**Do not stop taking this medicine suddenly<br>without your clinician’s advice.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>bradycardia, bronchospasm, cold extremities,<br>fatigue, sleep disturbances including<br>nightmares; heart failure, hypotension,<br>conduction disorders, peripheral<br>vasoconstriction, exacerbation of intermittent<br>claudication, Raynaud's phenomenon; alteration<br>of glucose and lipid metabolism<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Rash, dry eyes (reversible),<br>sexual dysfunction, exacerbation of psoriasis.<br>purpura, thrombocytopenia|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 20 mg; 40 mg_**<br>**_(hydrochloride)_**<br>Titrate to achieve a 25%<br>reduction in the heart rate|
|**D rug Indication**|**Propranolol**<br>Primary prevention of<br>variceal bleeding in patients<br>with documented varices<br>(10.9)<br>(**Other indications:**migraine<br>prophylaxis, essential<br>tremor, stable angina,<br>hypertension, some<br>arrhythmias)|

**439** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**May be used in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding; doses >200 mg/day may suppress lactation.|**Pregnancy:**May be used during pregnancy if it is the drug of<br>choice; avoid in f rst trimester when possible.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, if it is the drug of choice.<br>**Contraindications:**Megaloblastic anaemia<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic and renal impairment<br>For treatment of toxoplasmosis, pyrimethamine must always<br>be taken with sulfadiazine and should be administered with<br>folinic acid when available.<br>**Administration:**Give with food if GI disturbances occur.<br>Monitor blood counts in prolonged treatment and give folate<br>supplements throughout treatment.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Sensory neuropathy reported<br>with high doses given for extended periods|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>depression of haematopoiesis with high doses,<br>megaloblastic anaemia, rashes, insomnia, CNS<br>toxicity (at high doses)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 25 mg_**<br>Oral: 50 mg every 6 hours for<br>6 doses<br>Oral: 50–75 mg daily<br>Oral: 10 mg daily<br>Oral: 25 mg, up to 3–4 times daily|**_Tablet: 25 mg, 50 mg_**<br>Oral: 100–200 mg as a single<br>dose THEN 50 mg daily for at<br>least 6 weeks AND sulfadiazine<br>+ folinic acid  for 6 weeks<br>Oral: 75 mg daily for 3 days<br>THEN 25 mg daily for 4 weeks<br>AND sulfadiazine + folinic<br>acid for same duration (In<br>unresponsive patients, 50 mg<br>daily for a further 4 weeks)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Pyridoxine**<br>(vitamin B6)<br>Ethylene glycol poisoning<br>(3.8.1)<br>Peripheral neuropathy<br>(10.10a.6); neuropathic signs<br>on INH (13.3, 15.4.2)<br>Prevention of peripheral<br>neuropathy with INH for<br>prophylaxis or treatment for<br>TB (15.4.2)<br>Antiemetic in pregnancy<br>(14.1.11)|**Pyrimethamine**<br>Toxoplasmosis in<br>immunodef ciency (11.40)<br>Chorioretinitis (10.12.6)|

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**440** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use only if benef t is greater than risk; avoid in<br>f rst trimester when possible, as high doses (>1 g total) may<br>cause fetal deafness.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Caution; monitor for adverse effects. Avoid in<br>G6PD-def cient infants. Contraindications: Haemoglobinuria,<br>optic neuritis, tinnitus<br>**Use with caution:**In atrial f brillation, conduction defects,<br>heart block, renal impairment, G6PD def ciency, myasthenia<br>gravis.<br>With IV use, monitor signs of cardiac toxicity and blood<br>glucose levels.<br>**Administration:**Oral: If part of all of a dose is vomited<br>within 1 hour, the same amount must be re-administered<br>immediately.<br>I**V: Do not give as intravenous bolus injection.**Infuse IV<br>quinine over 4 hours, preferably in glucose 5% to reduce risk<br>of hypoglycaemia.|See doxycycline and quinine.<br>Additionally:<br>**Use with caution:**Rifampicin reduces the plasma<br>concentration of quinine, leading to increased treatment<br>failures. Avoid antiarrhythmics, such as f ecainide and<br>amiodarone. Antihistamines, such as terfenadine, and<br>antipsychotic drugs, such as pimozide and thioridazine, can<br>increase risk of arrhythmias. Cimetidine can increase quinine<br>levels.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>CNS disturbances, reversible hearing loss,<br>cinchonism, tinnitus, headache, hot and f ushed<br>skin, nausea, abdominal pain, rashes, visual<br>disturbances (including temporary blindness),<br>confusion, fever, rash, hypoglycaemia (after<br>parenteral administration), thrombocytopenia,<br>ECG changes<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Angioedema, intravascular<br>haemolysis, acute renal failure, prolonged QT<br>interval|As for quinine and clindamycin|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 300 mg Infusion: 300 mg/_**<br>**_ml in 2 ml ampoule_**<br>IV/IM (in anterior thigh): 20 mg/<br>kg over 4 hours; THEN 10 mg/<br>kg every 8 hours until oral<br>medication is possible<br>Oral: 600 mg quinine sulfate<br>3 times daily for 7 days AND<br>clindamycin or doxycycline|**_Separate blisters with tablets_**<br>**_of 150 or 300 mg quinine and_**<br>**_tablets of 100 mg doxycycline_**<br>Oral: 600 mg of quinine salt<br>given 3 times a day (every 8<br>hours) for 7 days and 600 mg of<br>clindamycin base twice daily<br>for 7 days|
|**D rug Indication**|**Quinine**<br>Severe_P. falciparum_<br>malaria if parenteral<br>artesunate not available<br>(QC p. 20, 11.25.5)|**Quinine + clindamycin**<br>Non-severe_P. falciparum_<br>malaria in f rst-trimester<br>pregnancy (11.25.7);<br>_P. falciparum_malaria in<br>travellers (11.25.3); second-<br>line treatment for malaria<br>(11.25.4)|

**441** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Considered safe<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used, with caution, at recommended<br>doses<br>**Counselling:**Urine, tears, saliva, and sputum may become<br>coloured orange-red-  do not worry about it as the colour<br>change is harmless.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea,<br>anorexia; headache, drowsiness; arthralgia,<br>myalgia (in the f rst weeks of treatment). Those<br>occurring mainly on intermittent therapy include<br>inf uenza-like symptoms (chills, fever, dizziness,<br>bone pain); urine, saliva, other body secretions<br>coloured orange-red.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Respiratory symptoms<br>(including shortness of breath); collapse and<br>shock; haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenic<br>purpura, disseminated intravascular<br>coagulation, leukopenia, eosinophilia; acute<br>renal failure; alterations of liver function,<br>jaundice; f ushing, urticaria, rashes, exfoliative<br>dermatitis, toxic epidermal necrolysis, Stevens-<br>Johnson syndrome, pemphigoid reactions;<br>oedema; psychoses; adrenal insuff ciency;<br>muscular weakness, myopathy;  menstrual<br>disturbances|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Capsule or tablet: 150 mg,_**<br>**_300 mg_**<br>See Section 10.2 for dosing.<br>Oral: 600 mg rifampicin once<br>monthly for 6 months AND daily<br>dapsone<br>600 mg rifampicin once monthly<br>for 12 months AND dapsone +<br>clofazimine.<br>See Section 11.21 for details of<br>dosing.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Rifampicin**<br>Buruli ulcer (in combination<br>therapy with streptomycin)<br>(10.2.10)<br>Leprosy- paucibacillary<br>(11.21)<br>Leprosy- multibacillary<br>(11.21)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**442** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Considered safe in pregnancy;<br>Benef ts of treating TB in pregnant and breastfeeding women<br>outweigh risks of drug side-effects to either mother or infant.<br>Monitor infant for signs of pyridoxine def ciency or jaundice;<br>consider maternal or fetal supplementation.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to rifampicin, hepatic<br>disease, porphyria, optic neuritis, severe renal impairment<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic or renal impairment, diabetes<br>mellitus, gout, chronic alcohol dependence, elderly, epilepsy,<br>history of psychosis. Prophylactic pyridoxine 10 mg daily<br>indicated, particularly in HIV-positive patients.<br>**Administration:**Ocular examination recommended before<br>and during treatment.<br>Three times per week is acceptable alternative provided<br>that patient is receiving directly observed therapy, and is<br>not living with HIV or living in an HIV-prevalent setting. See<br>Section 15.<br>**Counselling:**Discontinue treatment and seek immediate<br>medical attention if you develop persistent nausea, vomiting,<br>malaise, yellow discoloration of the white of your eye or<br>urine.<br>Tell your health care provider immediately about any<br>changes in your vision.<br>If you are using a combined oral contraceptive (the Pill),<br>patch, vaginal ring, or progestin-only pills, use additional<br>contraception, such as condoms.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|See rifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol,<br>pyrazinamide.<br>**Common:**Hyperuricaemia, polyarthritis, nausea<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hepatotoxicity (including<br>fever, anorexia, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly,<br>jaundice, liver failure); f ushing, dysuria,<br>sideroblastic anaemia, photosensitivity|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: f xed dose combination_**<br>**_rifampicin 150 mg, isoniazid_**<br>**_75 mg, pyrazinamide 400 mg,_**<br>**_ethambutol 275 mg_**<br>2HRZE/4HR<br>Oral: Rifampicin 10 mg/kg daily<br>(max 300 mg) AND isoniazid<br>5 mg/kg (max 600 mg) AND<br>pyrazinamide 25 mg/kg AND<br>ethambutol 15 mg/kg once daily<br>OR rifampicin 10 mg/kg daily<br>(maximum 600 mg) AND<br>isoniazid 5 mg/kg (maximum<br>900 mg) AND pyrazinamide<br>25 mg/kg AND ethambutol<br>15 mg/kg 3 times weekly (given<br>as DOT; 3 times weekly regimen<br>NOT recommended for HIV-<br>positive patients and those from<br>high HIV prevalent settings)|
|**D rug Indication**|**Rifampicin + isoniazid +**<br>**pyrazinamide + ethambutol**<br>**hydrochloride (R + H+ Z+ E)**<br>Tuberculosis<br>(13.10 HIV/TB co-<br>management, 15 TB)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**443** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used at recommended doses; asthma<br>management for pregnant and non-pregnant women should<br>be the same.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended doses (monitor<br>infant)<br>**Use with caution:**In hyperthyroidism, myocardial<br>insuff ciency, arrhythmias, susceptibility to QT interval<br>prolongation, hypertension, diabetes mellitus|**Contraindications:**Broken or inf amed skin.<br>**Use with caution:**In signif cant peripheral neuropathy; in<br>diabetics at risk of neuropathic ulcers<br>**Administration:**Avoid application to large areas.<br>**Counselling:**Avoid contact with eyes, lips, and inside of your<br>nose. Protect surrounding skin; rub warts gently with f le or<br>pumice stone once weekly.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|C**ommon:**Palpitations, f ne tremor (usually<br>hands), headache.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**With inhaled dosage forms,<br>hyperglycaemia and hypokalaemia after<br>high doses; muscle cramps; arrhythmias,<br>tachycardia, insomnia, paradoxical<br>bronchospasm, urticaria/angioedema|**Infrequent or rare:**Local irritation, dermatitis,<br>toxicity with excessive application or treatment<br>of large areas|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Inhalation respirator solution_**<br>**_for use in nebulizers: 5 mg (as_**<br>**_sulfate)/ml_**<br>**_Aerosol: 100 mcg (as sulfate)_**<br>**_per dose_**<br>See QC p 17 and 3.2.4 for dosing.<br>See Sections 10.6.4, 10.6.5.<br>By nebulizer: 10–20 mg OR<br>IV: 0.5 mg (500 mcg).<br>Administration should be<br>slow, over 15–20 minutes. If<br>neither of these are available,<br>give salbutamol 1200 mcg by<br>metered-dose inhaler with<br>spacer (12 puffs).|**_Solution: 5%_**<br>Apply directly to affected<br>area once daily, starting with<br>lower strength preparations;<br>gradually increase strength until<br>satisfactory response obtained.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Salbutamol**<br>Acute bronchospasm<br>(QC p. 17, 3.2.4)<br>Asthma and COPD  (10.6)<br>Hyperkalemia (5.2.2)|**Salicylic acid**<br>Hyperkeratotic conditions,<br>including warts; adjunct<br>in treatment of psoriasis,<br>ringworm, seborrhoeic<br>dermatitis, ichthyosis (10.2)|

**444** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**May be used on the scalp; it should not be used<br>on the body to treat skin infections, as it may be absorbed<br>through the skin.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Uncertain safety in breastfeeding; use with<br>caution.<br>**Use with caution:**Do not apply to damaged skin (risk of<br>systemic toxicity); avoid contact with eyes; do not use<br>within 48 hours of applying preparations for hair colouring,<br>straightening, or permanent wave.<br>**Administration:**To minimize absorption, rinse hair thoroughly<br>after use and remove all traces from skin (including nails).<br>Note: Selenium sulf de is widely used in proprietary<br>shampoos.|**Pregnancy:**If dietary and lifestyle changes fail to control<br>constipation in pregnancy, moderate doses of poorly<br>absorbed laxatives may be used. A bulk-forming laxative<br>should be tried f rst. An osmotic laxative, such as lactulose,<br>can also be used. Bisacodyl or senna may be suitable, if a<br>stimulant effect is necessary.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Not known to be harmful<br>**Use with caution:**Avoid prolonged use unless indicated to<br>prevent faecal impaction.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|C**ommon:**Local irritation, hair discolouration<br>or loss.<br>Absorption may result in systemic toxicity<br>including tremors, weakness, lethargy, pain in<br>lower abdomen, occasional vomiting (symptoms<br>usually resolve within 10 days)|**Common:**Abdominal discomfort, cramps<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hypokalaemia (with<br>prolonged use or overdosage)|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Detergent-based suspension_**<br>**_2%, lotion_**<br>Apply lotion with small amount<br>of water to entire affected<br>area; rinse off after 10 minutes.<br>Repeat daily for 7–14 days.<br>OR Apply to affected area at<br>bedtime; rinse off in morning;<br>repeat 1–6 times over 2 weeks;<br>repeat course if necessary.<br>Detergent-based suspension/<br>shampoo: Massage 5–10 ml<br>into wet hair and leave for<br>2–3 minutes before rinsing<br>thoroughly; repeat twice weekly<br>for 2 weeks; then once weekly<br>for 2 weeks; thereafter only<br>when needed.|**_Tablet: 7.5 mg (sennosides)_**<br>Oral: 2–4 tablets, usually at<br>night. Initial dose should be<br>low; then gradually increased<br>to 30 mg.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Selenium sulf de**<br>Pityriasis versicolor (10.2.7)<br>Seborrhoeic dermatitis<br>(10.2.7)|**Senna**<br>Constipation (10.7d.4, 20.2)|

**445** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Unknown safety in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding; use with caution, if benef t is greater than risk.|**Administration:**Start treatment with cromoglicate 1 month<br>before the onset of the hay fever season.<br>**Counselling:**It can take 3–4 weeks to reach full effect.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Unknown safety in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding; use with caution; cyanide poisoning likely to<br>be the more signif cant risk.<br>**Use with caution:**In severe cardiovascular or<br>cerebrovascular disease. Monitor plasma methaemoglobin<br>levels.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|No common side-effects.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**severe allergic reactions<br>(rash; hives; diff culty breathing; tightness in<br>chest; swelling of mouth, face, lips, or tongue);<br>irritability; muscle spasms or twitching; pain,<br>redness, or swelling at the injection site<br>Overly aggressive therapy with sodium<br>bicarbonate injection can result in metabolic<br>alkalosis (associated with muscle twitching,<br>irritability, tetany) and hypernatraemia.<br>Therefore, blood pH and electrolytes should be<br>monitored.|**Common:**Burning, stinging|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, abdominal<br>pain, vasodilatation (resulting in syncope,<br>hypotension, tachycardia, f ushing), headache,<br>methaemo-globinaemia, cyanosis, dyspnoea/<br>tachypnea|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: IV: 4.2%, 8.4%_**<br>**_(intravenous concentrations_**<br>**_1 ampoule = 50 meq = 4.2 grams_**<br>**_= 100 mmol)_**<br>IV: Emergency dosing: Initially,<br>1–2 mmol/kg over 1–2 minutes.<br>Additional dose: 0.5 mmol/kg<br>every 10 minutes.<br>Maintenance: 100–150 mmol<br>sodium bicarbonate in 1 litre 5%<br>dextrose at 250 ml/hour.|**_Ophthalmic drops: 2%_**<br>Apply 4 times daily.|**_Injection: 30 mg/ml in 10 ml_**<br>**_ampoule (3% solution)_**<br>Slow IV infusion: 10 ml of<br>3% solution over 2–4 minutes<br>THEN sodium thiosulfate|
|**D rug Indication**|**Sodium bicarbonate**<br>Treatment of cardiotoxicity<br>from drug overdose (e.g.<br>tricyclic antidepressant,<br>carbamazepine);<br>alkalinization of urine to<br>enhance excretion of<br>salicylate and chlorphenoxy<br>pesticides; correction of<br>metabolic acidosis (3.8.1)|**Sodium cromoglycate**<br>Allergic conjunctivitis;<br>seasonal<br>keratoconjunctivitis (10.12)|**Sodium nitrite**<br>Cyanide poisoning (3.8.2)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**446** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended in hypertensive crisis in<br>pregnancy.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, if benef ts are greater<br>than risks; monitor infant for effects such as hypotension,<br>bradycardia, fatigue.<br>**Contraindications:**Severe hepatic impairment,<br>compensatory hypertension, severe vitamin B12 def ciency|**Pregnancy:**Uncertain safety in pregnancy; use, with caution,<br>if benef t is greater than risk.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Limited information suggests that doses up to<br>1.4 g daily produce low levels in milk, not expected to cause<br>any adverse effects, especially if the infant is older than 2<br>months; if withholding nursing during therapy is preferred,<br>breastfeeding can be resumed 24–48 hours after last dose.<br>**Contraindications:**Cardiac, liver and kidney disorders.<br>**Use with caution:**Successful treatment of mucocutaneous<br>leishmaniasis may induce severe inf ammation around the<br>lesions (may be life-threatening if pharyngeal or tracheal<br>involvement); may require corticosteroid.<br>**Administration:**IV injections must be given slowly over 5<br>minutes (to reduce risk of local thrombosis) and stopped if<br>coughing or substernal pain.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Severe hypotension, headache,<br>dizziness, retching, abdominal pain,<br>perspiration, palpitation, arrythmias,<br>apprehension, retrosternal discomfort|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, anorexia, abdominal<br>pain, diarrhoea; ECG changes; coughing (see<br>Cautions); headache, lethargy; arthralgia,<br>myalgia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Jaundice, f ushing,<br>bleeding from nose or gum, substernal pain,<br>vertigo, fever, sweating,  rash; also reported,<br>pancreatitis and anaphylaxis; pain and<br>thrombosis on intravenous administration;<br>intramuscular injection also painful|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Powder for infusion: 50 mg in_**<br>**_ampoule_**<br>IV: Initially 0.3–0.5 mcg/kg/<br>minute increase gradually to<br>0.5–6 mcg/kg/minute for the<br>desired hemodynamic effect or<br>the appearance of headache<br>or nausea (maximum 8 mcg/<br>kg/minute). Stop infusion if<br>response satisfactory after<br>10 minutes.|**_Injection: 100 mg/ml vial_**<br>IV/IM: 20 mg/kg daily for 21 days<br>IV/IM: 20 mg/kg per month<br>See table in Section 11.20.2|
|**D rug Indication**|**Sodium nitroprusside**<br>MAO-I toxicity (3.8.1)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Hypertensive crisis)|**Sodium stibogluconate**<br>**(pentavalent antimony**<br>**compound)**<br>Cutaneous leishmaniasis<br>(11.20.1)<br>Secondary prophylaxis<br>of visceral leishmaniasis<br>(11.20.3)<br>Post kala-azar dermal<br>leishmaniasis, alternative<br>treatment<br>(11.20.2)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**447** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Unknown safety in pregnancy and<br>breastfeeding; use with caution. Cyanide poisoning likely to<br>be the more signif cant risk.|**Pregnancy/breastfeeding:**Use only if indicated. No<br>controlled studies or reports teratogenicity.<br>**Use with caution:**In renal impairment|**Pregnancy:**Not recommended; consider alternatives where<br>possible.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Use, with caution, if benef t is greater than<br>risk; amount to infant appears very small, unlikely to cause<br>adverse effects; theoretically may suppress lactation due to<br>diuresis<br>**Contraindications:**Hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia, severe<br>renal impairment, Addison’s disease. Monitor blood<br>urea nitrogen and plasma electrolytes; discontinue if<br>hyperkalaemia.<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly; in patients with diabetes<br>mellitus, renal impairment, hepatic impairment, porphyria<br>Avoid concurrent administration of potassium supplements.|
|---|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|Sodium thiosulfate has low toxicity.<br>Osmotic disturbances may occur but at the<br>recommended doses are usually mild.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**When used in cyanide<br>poisoning, symptoms of thiocyanate toxicity<br>may  occur: pain in the joints, blurred vision,<br>hyperref exia, muscle cramps, nausea and<br>vomiting, agitation, delusions, hallucinations,<br>tinnitus|**Infrequent or rare:**Dizziness; nausea; urticaria;<br>chills; fever; headache, insomnia, mild to<br>moderate pain after injection, anaphylaxis|**Common:**Hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia,<br>hyperchloraemic acidosis, weakness,<br>headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, breast<br>tenderness<br>**Infrequent or rare:**GI cramps, drowsiness,<br>menstrual irregularities, transient increase in<br>blood urea nitrogen, gynaecomastia, impotence,<br>rash, ataxia, hepatotoxicity|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 250 mg/ml in 50 ml_**<br>**_ampoule (25% solution)_**<br>**_Topical solution: 15%_**<br>IV: 50 ml of 25% solution (12.5 g)<br>over 10 minutes<br>Topical: Apply twice daily for<br>4 weeks.|**_Powder for injection: 2 g (as_**<br>**_hydrochloride) in vial_**<br>IM: 2 g as a single dose<br>IM: 2 g twice daily for 7 days<br>(some data suggest that 3 days<br>is adequate)|**_Tablet: 25 mg_**<br>Oral: 100–200 mg daily. Increase<br>if necessary to 400 mg daily<br>in resistant oedema (usual<br>maintenance dose 25–200 mg<br>daily) AND furosemide|
|**D rug Indication**|**Sodium thiosulfate**<br>Cyanide poisoning (together<br>with sodium nitrite) (3.8.1)<br>Pityriasis versicolor (10.2.7)|**Spectinomycin**<br>Alternative treatment<br>for gonorrhoea without<br>dissemination (11.13);<br>gonococcal conjunctivitis<br>(10.12.2)<br>Disseminated gonococcal<br>infection (11.13)|**Spironolactone**<br>Oedema (10.4.3); ascites<br>(10.9)<br>(**Other indications:**<br>Nephritic syndrome;<br>primary hyper-<br>aldosteronism; moderate<br>to severe heart failure in<br>patients taking an ACE<br>inhibitor and a beta-blocker)|

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Avoid streptomycin during pregnancy; auditory<br>or vestibular nerve damage possible<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used at recommended doses (monitor<br>infant for thrush or diarrhoea)<br>**Contraindications:**Hearing disorders, myasthenia gravis<br>**Use with caution:**In patients with renal impairment; elderly<br>patients. Patients over 60 years or those weighing <50 kg<br>may not tolerate doses above 500–750 mg daily.<br>**Administration:**Monitor auditory, and vestibular function. If<br>poor renal function, adjust dose.|**Pregnancy:**Uncertain safety in pregnancy. Use, with caution,<br>only if potential benef t is greater than risk; avoid in f rst<br>trimester due to bone marrow toxicity; avoid in third trimester<br>due to risk of neonatal jaundice.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Limited data; use with caution. Avoid in<br>G6PD-def cient neonates and infants; monitor infants for<br>adverse effects (e.g. haemolysis, jaundice).<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity to sulphonamides,<br>porphyria<br>**Use with caution:**In hepatic or renal impairment. Maintain<br>adequate f uid intake to avoid crystalluria. |**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection: 1 g vial_**<br>IM: 15 mg/kg daily. Indicated in<br>combination as 2HRZES/1HRZE/<br>5HRE.<br>IM: 15 mg/kg daily for 8 weeks<br>(in combination therapy with<br>rifampicin 10 mg/kg daily)|**_Tablet: 500 mg_**<br>Oral: 4–6 g daily in 4 divided<br>doses for at least 6 weeks<br>AND_folinic acid +_<br>pyrimethamine for 6 weeks<br>Oral: 4 g daily in 4 divided<br>doses AND_folinic acid +_<br>pyrimethamine<br>Oral: 1 g daily|
|**D rug Indication**|**Streptomycin**<br>Tuberculosis (initial phase<br>of combination therapy in<br>TB patients returning after<br>defaulting or relapsing<br>from their f rst treatment<br>course) (15)<br>Buruli ulcer (10.2.10)|**Sulfadiazine**<br>Toxoplasmosis in<br>immunodef ciency (11.40);<br>chorioretinitis (10.12)<br>Toxoplasmosis (in second<br>and third trimesters of<br>pregnancy if fetal infection<br>has been documented)<br>Rheumatic fever, secondary<br>prophylaxis (11.32)|

**449** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Use during pregnancy if treatment of choice;<br>consider folic acid supplementation 5 mg/day.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Unknown safety; not recommended<br>**Contraindications:**Already taking cotrimoxazole,<br>hypersensitive to sulfonamides or pyrimethamine, severe<br>hepatic or renal impairment. Avoid in blood disorders (except<br>with specialist supervision). Withdraw treatment if blood<br>disorder, rash, sore throat, mouth ulcers, shortness of breath,<br>or cough occurs.<br>**Use with caution:**In G6PD def ciency, predisposition to<br>folate def ciency|**Pregnancy:**Uncertain safety in pregnancy; use, with<br>caution, only if potential benef t is greater than risk (e.g. in<br>trypanosomiasis).<br>Breastfeeding: Unknown safety in breastfeeding; avoid if<br>possible.<br>**Contraindications:**Hypersensitivity; totally blind patients with<br>onchocerciasis (unless they require relief from the intensely<br>itchy lesions not relieved by safer alternative)<br>**Use with caution:**In anaphylaxis, renal/hepatic impairment,<br>total blindness, elderly|

**452** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**TXA has been used in pregnancy and no harmful<br>effects have been reported. In the CRASH-2 trial, pregnancy<br>was not an exclusion criteria. Weigh the potential risks and<br>benef ts for each woman.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Very small amounts pass into breast milk; an<br>antif brinolytic effect in the infant is unlikely.<br>**Contraindications:**History of thromboembolic disease<br>**Administration:**As early as possible, within 3–4 hours<br>of injury. Reduce injection dose in patients with renal<br>insuff ciency.<br>TXA solution for injection should not be mixed with blood for<br>transfusion or with infusion solutions containing penicillin or<br>mannitol.|**Pregnancy:**Topical retinoids contraindicated in<br>pregnancy; women of child-bearing age must use effective<br>contraception (oral progestogen-only contraceptives not<br>considered suff ciently effective).<br>**Use with caution:**Topical retinoids should be avoided in<br>severe acne involving large areas. Avoid contact with eyes,<br>nostrils, mouth, mucous membranes; eczematous, broken,<br>or sunburned skin. Avoid exposure to UV light (including<br>sunlight, solariums). If sun exposure is unavoidable, an<br>appropriate sunscreen or protective clothing should be used.<br>Avoid use of retinoids with abrasive cleaners, comedogenic<br>or astringent cosmetics.<br>Allow peeling (e.g. resulting from use of benzoyl peroxide) to<br>subside before using a topical retinoid.<br>**Counselling:**Some redness and skin peeling may occur<br>initially but settles with time.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea/vomiting, diarrhoea, allergic<br>skin reactions. Rapid intravenous injection<br>may cause dizziness and/or hypotension. To<br>avoid this response, the solution should not be<br>injected more rapidly than 1 ml per minute.<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Thromboembolic events,<br>disturbances in colour vision (discontinue)|**Common:**Local reactions include burning,<br>erythema, stinging, pruritus, dry or peeling skin<br>(discontinue if severe). Increased sensitivity to<br>UVB light or sunlight; temporary changes of skin<br>pigmentation reported<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Eye irritation and oedema,<br>blistering or crusting of skin|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Injection, solution: 100 mg/ml_**<br>**_(10 ml)_**<br>IV: loading dose of 1 g over<br>10 minutes THEN infusion of 1 g<br>over 8 hours.|**_Gel: tretinoin 0.01% (tretinoin is_**<br>**_the acid form of vitamin A)_**<br>Apply thinly 1–2 times daily.<br>Several months of treatment<br>may be needed to achieve<br>optimal response. Treatment<br>should continue until no new<br>lesions develop in 2 weeks.|
|**D rug Indication**|**Tranexamic acid**<br>(TXA)<br>Trauma patients with<br>ongoing signif cant<br>haemorrhage or at risk of<br>signif cant haemorrhage,<br>within 3-4 hours (4.2)|**Tretinoin**<br>Severe acne (10.2.3)|

Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**454** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Risk of teratogenicity; use only if benef t is<br>greater than risk; consider folic acid supplementation 5 mg/<br>day and vitamin K supplementation.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used in breastfeeding. Small amounts<br>excreted in breast milk; use minimum effective dose and<br>monitor infant for adverse effects (e.g. jaundice).<br>**Use with caution:**Monitor coagulation studies and liver<br>function tests regularly during therapy.<br>Prone to multiple drug interactions through CYP enzymes;<br>check for interactions with all new and current medications.<br>Elderly: See cautions in Section 10.11.5.<br>**Administration:**Do not use for alcohol withdrawal.<br>**Counselling:**Take with food to reduce stomach upset.<br>Your appetite may increase when taking this medicines; pay<br>attention to your diet to avoid weight gain.<br>This medication may impair your ability to perform hazardous<br>activities requiring mental alertness.<br>Tell your clinician immediately if fever, rash, abdominal<br>pain, vomiting, yellow eyes or urine, bruising, or bleeding<br>develops.<br>Do not stop taking this medicine suddenly unless advised by<br>your doctor.|
|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Common:**Nausea, gastric irritation, diarrhoea,<br>increased appetite; weight gain; ataxia, tremor;<br>paraesthesia, drowsiness: elevated liver<br>transaminases, hyperammonaemia<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hepatotoxicity (can be fatal),<br>pancreatitis, hyponatraemia from drinking<br>excess f uid; blood dyscrasias (anaemia,<br>leukopenia, pancytopenia, thrombocytopenia);<br>severe allergic reaction (including toxic<br>epidermal necrolysis, Stevens-Johnson<br>syndrome).<br>Transient hair loss (regrowth may be curly);<br>increased alertness, aggression, hyperactivity,<br>behavioural disturbances, vasculitis; lethargy,<br>drowsiness, confusion, stupor, hallucinations,<br>menstrual disturbances, hearing loss, rash;<br>peripheral oedema, increase in bleeding<br>time, extrapyramidal symptoms, dementia,<br>encephalopathy, coma, gynaecomastia,<br>Fanconi’s syndrome, hirsutism, acne, enuresis,<br>hyponatraemia.<br>**Withdraw treatment immediately if persistent**<br>**vomiting and abdominal pain, anorexia,**<br>**jaundice, oedema, malaise, drowsiness, loss of**<br>**seizure control.**|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Tablet: 200 mg, 500 mg_**<br>**_Liquid: 200 mg/ml_**<br>Oral: Initiate at 400 mg daily;<br>increase by 200 mg daily to<br>maximum of 2 g daily in divided<br>doses.<br>Oral: Initiate at 500 mg at night;<br>gradually increase by 200 mg<br>every 7 days until response;<br>typical dose: 1–2 g daily<br>In elderly or medically ill<br>patients incuding HIV stage 3 or<br>4: initiate at 200 mg in morning<br>and at night. Increase dose 200<br>mg every 7 days until clinical<br>response. See 10.11.5|
|**D rug Indication**|**Valproic acid**<br>**(sodium valproate)**<br>Epilepsy<br>(10.10c)<br>Mood stabilization in bipolar<br>disorder, acute mania<br>(10.11.5)|



Summary tables 

Vol. 1 • 8. Medicines/therapies: July 2011 

**455** 

|**Special groups/comments**|**Pregnancy:**Safe, but megaloblastic anaemia of pregnancy<br>is usually due to folate def ciency and should be treated with<br>folate plus vitamin B12.<br>**Breastfeeding:**Safe to use Contraindications: Sensitivity to<br>B12 (hydroxocobalamin).<br>**Use with caution:**Establish which def ciency is present –<br>vitamin B12 or folate – with a marrow examination and treat<br>the underlying cause.<br>Always give B12 with folic acid in pernicious anaemia;<br>folic acid given alone can precipitate neuropathy if there is<br>underlying unrecognized vitamin B12 def ciency.<br>Cardiac arrhythmias secondary to hypokalaemia have been<br>reported during initial therapy; therefore, potassium should<br>be monitored during this period.<br>**Administration: Do not give by IV injection**. Store below 25<br>°C; protect from light.|**Pregnancy:**Use only if benef t is greater than risk.<br>**Breastfeeding:**May be used short-term; caution if chronic<br>dosing required<br>**Use with caution:**In elderly and hepatic impairment<br>**Administration:**IV vitamin K should be given slowly over 20<br>minutes.<br>Note: Vitamin K is not an antidote to heparin.|
|---|---|---|
|**Adverse effects**|**Infrequent or rare:**Nausea, headache, dizziness;<br>fever, hypersensitivity reactions including rash<br>and pruritus, anaphylaxis; injection-site pain;<br>hypokalaemia during initial treatment|**Common:**Pain, tenderness, erythema at IM site<br>**Infrequent or rare:**Hypersensitivity reactions|
|**_Formulations_ ****_Dosage_**|**_Intramuscular anhydrous_**<br>**_hydroxo-cobalamin 1 mg/ml_**<br>IM: 1 mg 2–4 times weekly<br>for 2 weeks; then 1 mg every<br>3 months.<br>IM: 1 mg on alternate days until<br>no further improvement; then 1<br>mg every 2 months<br>IM: 1 mg IM every 2–3 months|**_Injection: 10 mg/ml in 5 ml_**<br>**_ampoule Tablet: 10 mg_**<br>Oral: 10–20 mg; may need to<br>continue for weeks if long-<br>acting anticoagulant rodenticide<br>Oral: 2.5–5 mg<br>IV: 10 mg|
|**D rug Indication**|**Vitamin B12**<br>**(hydroxocobalamin)**<br>Pernicious anaemia and<br>other macrocytic anaemia<br>without neurological<br>involvement<br>Pernicious anaemia<br>and other macrocytic<br>anaemias with neurological<br>involvement<br>Prophylaxis and treatment<br>of other macrocytic<br>anaemias due to vitamin<br>B12 def ciency|**Vitamin K**<br>**(phytomenadion)**<br>Rodenticide poisoning<br>(3.8.1) with no bleeding but<br>prolonged INR<br>Warfarin therapy (3.8.1) with<br>INR > 9.0 but no bleeding<br>Rodenticide poisoning or<br>warfarin therapy (3.8.1) with<br>severe haemorrhage|

## **Index to syndromes, diseases, conditions (in both Volumes 1 and 2)** 

To fi nd the indications and Section locations of specifi c medicines, as well as dosing, adverse effects, use in pregnancy/breastfeeding, contraindications, cautions, administration details, and patient counselling, see Section 8.4. 

The Quick Check and Emergency Treatments (Section 2) is referenced as QC followed by the page number. With this exception, the subsections are provided below.

## **Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2** 

## **Overall clinical editing and writing of Volume 1 of the** _**IMAI District Clinician Manual**_ 

Sandy Gove (WHO HIV/AIDS – IMAI team leader), Kirsty McHarry (U KwaZuluNatal Centre for Rural Health, South Africa), Hillary Cohen (Maimonides Medical Center, NY, USA), Neeri Moodley (U KwaZulu-Natal Centre for Rural Health, South Africa), Ed Zuroweste (Migrant Clinicians Network, USA), Janet Diaz (WHO GIP consultant and UCSF/SFGH, USA), Matthew Chersich (Centre for Health Policy, U Witwatersrand, South Africa), and Shevin Jacob (U Washington). 

Editors: Sarah Johnson, Emily Tuthill, John Liddy, Sandra Woods, Ward Rinehart, Cynthia Bloomquist 

Overall development of the manual was coordinated at WHO on the IMAI team by Fareed Ramzi Asfour (2005–2006), Kirsty McHarry (2006–2009), Sandy Gove (2009–publication), and Neeri Moodley (2009–2011). Other writers contributing to specifi c sections are indicated in bold in the lists at the end of this section. 

## **Process for development of the** _**IMAI District Clinician Manual**_ **, Volumes 1 & 2** 

The implementation of many clinical interventions for public health at the The implementation of many clinical interventions for public health at the primary care level requires district hospital clinicians who are able to manage uncomplicated and complicated cases, patients who fail initial empirical treatment interventions, and patients with severe illness requiring urgent treatment and inpatient care. Therefore, a manual outlining the key steps for this clinical management can make an important contribution to improving the quality of care in a district network and thus strengthening the health system. 

The WHO _IMAI District Clinician Manual_ is a how-to manual addressed to the district clinician, who may be a doctor, clinical offi cer, senior nurse, or other senior health worker at a district hospital in a limited-resource setting. The manual covers adolescents from 10 years of age and adults through to old age and death. It consists of simplifi ed, operationalized prevention and treatment recommendations for the primary care of patients on initial presentation to a district-level facility. The manual assumes that district hospitals in resource-limited settings have general multipurpose practitioners such as a medical or clinical offi cer but do not have specialist clinicians such as an internist, paediatrician, or psychiatrist (although it may be possible to consult with one). 

This manual is divided into two volumes, each comprising a number of sections. This, the fi rst Volume, covers emergency triage assessment and treatment, and acute care for a severely ill or acutely injured patient within approximately the fi rst 24 hours of care. This Volume also describes the clinical procedures commonly applied in this care and gives a summary of drugs used and the steps necessary for infection control. The companion Volume 2 provides a symptom-based approach 

Vol. 1 • Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2: July 2011 

**475** 

to clinical care for acute and subacute conditions (including mental health) and to the chronic or long-term care of HIV, TB, and alcohol and substance use disorders. 

Within the manual operationalized guidelines are provided for second-level outpatient and inpatient care of severely ill or complicated patients as well as for primary care of uncomplicated patients. The primary care guidelines for the outpatient care of uncomplicated patients are consistent with the IMAI fi rst-level facility guideline modules for chronic HIV care with ART and prevention, acute care, palliative care (symptom management and end-of-life care), MDR and TBHIV co-management, as well as the IMPAC PCPNC and mhGAP guidelines. In addition to clinical guidelines, the manual emphasizes the district clinician’s role in the district as clinical mentor and supervisor to nurse-led clinical teams at the health centre level. 

## **Development of the manual** 

The development of each Section has been overseen by WHO, with input from expert subgroups. The work for each Section was a collaborative effort between the IMAI team and each applicable WHO department, as part of joint and ongoing activities between IMAI and these departments. Many recommendations in the manual are based on WHO normative guidelines developed by various WHO departments and disease control programmes, and they support their diseasecontrol strategies. These include HIV/AIDS, Stop TB, Global Malaria Programme, Neglected Tropical Diseases, Mental Health Gap (mhGAP), RHR STI and cervical cancer guidelines, IMEESC, IMPAC, Global Infl uenza Programme (GIP), Global Alert Response (GAR), and others. Where WHO guidelines do not yet exist or are outdated, a review of evidence was conducted. 

Experts in the subgroups (each subgroup addressed a particular content area) were chosen based on their experience in providing or organizing clinical care in resource-limited settings and their up-to-date knowledge of both the relevant literature and the public health approach to delivering HIV, TB, and other adult medical services through strengthened district networks. Most external experts are either global content experts from academic institutions or active clinicians with in-depth expertise in their content areas. Selection of experts was also based on recommendations from collaborating WHO departments and the academic publications of experts, especially aiming to identify those who have experience with supporting implementation of services at district hospitals and health centres in countries with high HIV and TB burdens. Experts were drawn from all WHO regions. Preference was given to those familiar with the realities of working with a limited drug formulary and with limited laboratory and equipment at the district hospital level in limited-resource settings. 

The subgroups also include WHO medical offi cers. Moreover, the manual refl ects a broader collaboration of medical and technical offi cers from multiple WHO Departments, themselves advised by expert groups, who contributed their updated normative guidelines and operational tools. 

Whenever possible, the expert subgroups simplifi ed and operationalized existing evidence-based WHO normative guidelines. Treatment recommendations are consistent with the WHO Formulary unless superseded by more up-to-date WHO guidelines or evidence. The relevant WHO normative guidelines are listed in footnotes in each Section, including, where available, an indication of when these will next be revised. 

Vol. 1 • Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2: July 2011 

**476** 

Drafts of the sections were developed in the following manner: Writers from the expert subgroups, WHO medical offi cers, or consultants produced fi rst drafts of each Section. Wherever available, they based the Sections on WHO guidelines from various departments. When evidence-based WHO guidelines had not yet been developed, they added to these initial drafts on the basis of evidence and expert consensus. These drafts were then circulated for peer review and comment, revised, and then circulated again. This iterative improvement entailed multiple cycles of review and discussion for each Section. Each Section was sent for review to the relevant WHO departments, while the general reviewers reviewed all sections. Each Section thus refl ects evidence reviews of the literature combined with practical experience and/or constitutes operationalized derivatives of WHO evidence-based normative guidelines, which themselves often have been developed to refl ect a public health and clinical care approach feasible in limitedresource settings. 

Expert subgroups also contributed to the evidence reviews, suggested best practice approaches, discussed drafts, reached consensus through discussion in meetings and by email, and assisted in preparing draft sections for fi eld-testing and with the fi eld-testing itself. The core group members identifi ed other experts for consultation when necessary. 

For many of the conditions considered, there was a lack of evidence from limitedresource settings with limited diagnostic capabilities; thus, evidence reviews often identifi ed evidence predominantly from developed-country settings. Although this is indirect evidence, it was used to inform decisions, while taking into account the experts’ extensive clinical and programme experience that suggested modifi cations based on feasibility, cost, and other resource considerations. 

Two initial developmental meetings were held in Geneva, in March and October 2006. 

## **Development of specifi c sections in Volume 1** 

Section 2 (Quick Check and emergency treatments) was developed to be compatible with the existing emergency triage assessment and treatment guidelines for paediatrics (ETAT), for pregnant women (from IMPAC PCPNC), and for adults (from IMAI Acute Care). Section 3, Approach to the severely ill patient, draws on WHO formulary recommendations, the evidence review described below, and input from the emergency, pulmonary, and sepsis expert subgroups. The WHO departments of HIV/AIDS (IMAI team), GAR, and GIP initially constituted these as separate subgroups but then combined them to work together, as the WHO Working Group on Critical Care in Limited-Resource Settings, to develop the emergency guidance on management of septic shock and severe respiratory distress (and other severe illnesses). In 2009 expert meetings were held in March (Geneva, Switzerland), April (Addis Ababa, Ethiopia), June (Geneva), and September (Florence, Italy). This Group was composed of highly qualifi ed professionals who have expertise and experience in the areas of pneumonia, acute lung injury, septic shock, infl uenza, and the treatment of critically ill patients in general. The recommendations provide both guidance to countries experiencing outbreaks of febrile disease causing critical illness (including, but not limited to, pandemic infl uenza) where local resources are not able to provide mechanical ventilation for medical patients and the full spectrum of «ICU-level care» and guidance for the management of patients severely ill from HIV/AIDS, TB, severe malaria, maternal sepsis, dengue, and other endemic diseases. On an emergency basis, an extract of these guidelines for management of severe complications of infl uenza H1N1 was released. 

Vol. 1 • Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2: July 2011 

**477** 

The mental health recommendations in Section 3 (and Section 10.11) were developed by an expert group that originally met during the March and December 2006 second-level learning programme meetings in Geneva and then shared drafts and references by email and teleconferences. Several members of the expert subgroup met in November 2008 to fi nish the Section and to review the mental health content of all other sections. In view of the limited evidence in this fi eld from resource-limited settings and the often relatively neglected mental health and psychiatric services in many developing country settings, the Section was written using evidence from resource-rich countries, with adaptations to developing country settings. To ensure that the recommendations are feasible, the Section was further reviewed and adapted by psychiatrists and psychologists who have signifi cant expertise in adapting mental health interventions to developing country settings. The WHO mhGap GRADE reviews were completed in late 2009, and all recommendations on mental health, neurology, and substance use in the Manual were then made fully compatible with the mhGAP recommendations. 

Various treatment recommendations in the Quick Check, Section 3.10 Burns, Section 4 Trauma (management of the acutely injured patient) were adapted from _Surgical care at the district hospital (SCDH)_ (WHO, 2003), with updates based on the expert meeting in Addis Ababa in April 2009 (convened collaboratively by the IMAI and IMEESC teams). These sections then underwent the evidence check detailed below. 

Review of Section 3.8, on poisoning, was organized by the WHO International Programme on Chemical Safety, Evidence & Policy on Environmental Health (EPE). A panel of clinical toxicologists reviewed the fi rst draft by email. The revised draft was submitted to an evidence check as described below. The management recommendations for specifi c substances, together with the outcomes of the evidence check, were then distributed to individual clinical toxicologists to check for completeness and to comment on questions raised by the evidence check. The outcome of this process was tabulated and reviewed once again by a guideline panel of clinical toxicologists convened by EPE in July 2010 in Edinburgh, Scotland. 

## **Process of evidence check** 

In 2009–2010 an additional check of the evidence was carried out based on a protocol agreed with the WHO Guideline Review Committee for each treatment recommendation in the manual, unless these recommendations came from a current WHO guideline. A team of reviewers was contracted to perform these evidence reviews. The process was used as an opportunity to build capacity in evidence-based medicine; thus, a considerable portion of the evidence reviews were done by reviewers from Ethiopia and South Africa. A two-week training course was held in Addis Ababa in 2009 on the review protocol and topics such as assessing the quality of evidence and data extraction. 

The evidence check process aimed to be fully transparent and replicable. Thus, several steps were taken to enhance standardization of the processes used by the review team. These included use of a protocol outlining the pre-specifi ed review methods and having an overall coordinator responsible for overseeing the evidence review (Matthew Chersich, assisted by Janet Diaz). Each step, including the listing of treatment recommendations, searches made, and the results of evidence identifi cation and evidence retrieval, was stipulated in the evidence review protocol. 

Details of each search strategy were documented in an evidence review log, together with the date that the fi nal search was done and the evidence located 

Vol. 1 • Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2: July 2011 

**478** 

for each recommendation. Review questions were formulated as an “answerable review question”, containing the components of the PICO acronym: the target population (P), the intervention (I), the intervention it is compared with (C), and the outcome of interest (O). The full detailed evidence summary logs are posted on the IMAI EZcollab site and are available to the public on request. The outputs from the evidence review will also be used to inform adaptation of the manual to the circumstances in different countries. Evidence retrieval addressed only treatment recommendations and not prevention or counselling messages. Economic evaluations were not systematically reviewed. 

The WHO Library & Information Networks for Knowledge Database (WHOLIS, http://dosei.who.int) was searched to locate existing WHO normative or policy recommendations; these were assessed to determine if they were current and valid guidelines and their state of revision. The reviewer then assessed whether recommendations in the IMAI manual were fully consistent with recommendations in current WHO guidelines. For some topics WHO guidelines have recently been developed and cover all the treatment recommendations within a Section of the IMAI manual. In these instances – after cross-checking that all treatment recommendations in the Manual Section are consistent with the WHO guideline – no evidence summaries were made. The WHO Model Formulary (http://apps. who.int/emlib/Medicines.aspx?Language=EN) was searched for all treatment recommendations that involve administration of drug therapy. Treatment recommendations located in the WHO Model Formulary were checked for consistency with the manual and other evidence, and the reviewer documented this in the evidence log. If drugs are mentioned in the IMAI manual and not included in the WHO Formulary, then evidence for their effectiveness was sought, as with all other interventions. These drugs are in italics in Section 8 Medicines/therapies. 

When no WHO guidelines currently address a treatment recommendation in the manual, selected national-level authorities were searched. These authorities were chosen because they develop guidelines using clearly documented methodology, including conducting systematic reviews. The sources searched were: the UK National Institute for Clinical Excellence (NICE, http://guidance.nice.org.uk), the Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network (SIGN, http://www.sign.ac.uk), and the US AIDSInfo Clinical Guidelines Portal (http://www.aidsinfo.nih.gov/guidelines). Some of these authorities make use of the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) system in developing their guidelines. When a treatment recommendation was located in a national authority guideline, a reviewer determined whether the national-level guideline is consistent with the WHO treatment recommendation and extracted information from the guideline to complete the evidence log form. 

If the intervention and population in the district clinician manual recommendation were not located in a national-level guideline, then evidence-based medicine sources were searched, provided they use acceptable systematic methods: namely, British Medical Journal Clinical Evidence (http://clinicalevidence.bmj. com), the Cochrane Collaboration (http://www.thecochranelibrary.org), and the Database of Abstracts of Reviews of Effects (http://www.crd.york.ac.uk/CRDWeb), which systematically identifi es and assesses the quality of systematic reviews. UpToDate (http://www.utdol.com) was searched only if no systematic reviews were identifi ed in the other sources. As the next step in the hierarchical system, a reviewer searched for systematic reviews of evidence in the MEDLINE database, using the PubMed interface. A search strategy was developed using a validated search fi lter for systematic reviews. An evidence summary of systematic review fi ndings was made, as applicable. Where independent systematic reviews were 

Vol. 1 • Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2: July 2011 

**479** 

not located, randomized controlled trials were sought on MEDLINE and, if located, summarized in a table in the evidence logs. 

Once the evidence summaries were completed for a Section, the evidence review team then searched the log forms to identify the new WHO recommendations in the manual and instances where the evidence check showed a discrepancy between new WHO recommendations and recommendations in the manual. WHO then organized small, unconfl icted guideline panels to consider each of these new or discrepant recommendations. Members of these fi nal unconfl icted guideline panels are included in the related expert groups in the table of expert writers and reviewers, below, with a superscript designating the guideline panel they participated in. Reports can be found on the EZcollab site under “Y expert panel report.” 

These panellists assessed the evidence extracted from the evidence-based medicine sources or primary evidence located by the review and considered the overall balance of risks and benefi ts (including such considerations as feasibility, resource constraints, and diversity of values and preferences). Each guideline panel then decided if the evidence review findings were applicable – most importantly, the _directness (or external validity)_ of the evidence with respect to the populations, the interventions, and the settings where the proposed intervention will be used. For example, the panels considered whether the recommendation required modifi cation to limited-resource settings, based on the level of technology available in these settings. When review fi ndings were not directly applicable to limited-resource settings, the experts had to decide whether this _indirectness_ introduced important uncertainty as to whether the effectiveness of an intervention is likely to differ according to setting. It was necessary, at times, to modify recommendations based on appropriate technology use in limited-resource settings, as the diagnostic process and treatment protocols in this manual assume that only the minimum essential laboratory tests are available. Through consensus techniques, the expert panels agreed whether the recommendation required modifi cation to make it relevant and feasible in resource-constrained settings or to leave the recommendation unchanged if the difference with the evidence check was due to resource implications of alternative recommendations, which may include health system implications, such as training and supervision requirements, referral support, equipment and infrastructure requirements. 

Because the recommendations for septic shock, severe pneumonia, and acute pulmonary oedema might be used in caring for adult patients whose conditions refl ect different etiologies, (e.g. maternal sepsis, disseminated TB, severe malaria, severe infl uenza, and dengue), the appropriateness of the recommendations for these conditions was reviewed against current WHO condition-specifi c guidelines (for TB, dengue, and malaria). In addition, two guideline panels discussed their appropriateness for pregnant or postpartum patients and those with severe malaria. 

## **Field-testing of the manual** 

Field-testing of the entire draft manual was carried out with representatives of the intended audience in 6 countries – Uganda, Rwanda, Ethiopia, India, Zambia, and Tanzania – in 2009–2010, in parallel with the evidence check. Field-testing provided valuable feedback on feasibility and utility of the manual and on district clinicians’ preferences, as well as practical suggestions to improve the relevance and presentation of the manual. The Quick Check and the guidelines on severe respiratory distress and septic shock were fi eld-tested with a training course in 

Vol. 1 • Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2: July 2011 

**480** 

Uganda, Rwanda, Ethiopia, and Malawi in the same period. A second fi eld test was conducted in the same 6 countries after the district clinicians had used the manual for more than a year. Over the course of fi eld-testing, a detailed survey in Survey Monkey and focus group discussions provided specifi c information on the usefulness and adaptability of the various Sections of the manual, based on district clinicians’ use of the fi rst version and then review of the new version.

## **Final steps in development of the manual** 

Modifi cations in format, fl ow, and clarity, based on the results of fi eld-testing, further review suggestions by expanded expert subgroups, and internal WHO review after submission to the WHO Guideline Review Committee (GRC), and the decisions of the fi nal guideline panels were incorporated into the manual Sections. The GRC chair and DGO referred the manual to an external review prior to its publication. The members of the external review group, which met 20–22 June 2011, are listed in the table below. Technical recommendations from this review were incorporated into the manual. 

## **Plans for updates** 

It is important that this manual remains consistent with new WHO guidelines as these are updated or newly developed. Within 3 months of the revision or release of a relevant WHO normative guideline, an updated Section of the manual will be posted on the manual web site (IMAI second-level EZcollab web site). Revisions will also take into account further fi eld-testing and experience from closely monitored 

Vol. 1 • Writers and reviewers, Volume 1, and process of development, Volumes 1 and 2: July 2011 

**481** 

early use. The updated Sections of the IMAI manual will be incorporated into an annual revision of each Volume, which will be reprinted yearly. Before adapting the manual, users are advised to check for the most up-to-date Sections on the EZcollab site. 

## **Declarations of interest** 

Declarations of interest were received from the contributors to Volume 1. Nine of the contributors declared an interest, two of which were relevant to the development of the _IMAI District Clinician Manual_ . Drs Moore and Jacob had confl icts of interest related to the manual. They received funding from Pfi zer™ through grants to their institutions for a research training programme for students and fellows and for a study of fl uid resuscitation, PRISM-U2. 

For those contributors with potential conflicts of interest, declarations are summarized below: 

1. Dr Ortiz received a travel allowance from Merck to attend the American Thoracic award conference. 2. Dr Runyon received funds from Abbot Tanzania for developing an emergency department and a training programme at Muhimbili Hospital in Dar es Salaam. 3. Dr Molyneux received a 2–3 year grant for malaria research in Malawi from The Leverhulme Trust. 4. Dr Bukham received a grant for equipment from Sonosite Inc. 5. Dr Vuylsteke received a grant from iMDsoft Fukuda Denshi, a software developer, for research on application in a clinical environment. 6. Dr Cruz received honoraria for lectures from GSK, Mantecorp, LIBBS, Astrazencea, and Novartis and a further donation from Novartis for public work in a health facility and donations from Mantecorp, CHIESI, Novartis, and Ache for NGO work in Brazil. 7. Dr Dawson received funds from Wellcome Trust for capacity-building in the management of poisoning and for research on activated charcoal and gastric decontamination. All of the above were considered unconfl icted. 

Declarations of potential confl icts of interest were also received from all participants of the fi nal expert review meeting. Dr David Cohn declared having received funds from NIAID and CDC for research on HIV and TB when employed by Denver Health Hospital Authority, from which he retired in 2011. His past participation in publicly funded research was not considered to constitute a confl ict of interest. Dr Michael Runyon declared that his institution has been reimbursed by Abbott Fund Tanzania for his work on the development of and support to an emergency department at Muhimbili Hospital in Dar Es Salaam in the United Republic of Tanzania. He was considered unconfl icted.
